  • Persisting Bloom bits but not the parameters. Recreating needs m, k, and the exact hash functions. Encode them in the serialized payload.
  • Confusing approximate with wrong. A 1% false positive rate on a spam check is fine; on a "do they have permission to access this row?" check it's a security bug. Match the structure's error model to the use case.

docs.title: Easy
docs.text:
1. What's a Bloom filter and why use it? Bit-array + k hashes for set membership. Used to avoid an expensive lookup when the answer is "no." False positives possible, false negatives impossible.

  2. A Bloom filter says "yes" — what does that mean? The item might be in the set. You must verify against the authoritative source. Only "no" is certain.

docs.title: Medium
docs.text:
1. How would you count daily unique visitors across 100 web servers, each seeing tens of thousands of events per second? Each server maintains an HLL in memory (~12 KB). Each minute, server pushes its HLL to a central aggregator. Aggregator merges HLLs (pairwise max of registers) into hourly/daily HLLs. Query the daily HLL for the count. Total RAM cost: trivial. Network: tiny.

  2. Size a Bloom for 10M items at 1% false-positive rate. Use m = -n·ln(p) / (ln 2)² → m ≈ 95.85 M bits ≈ 12 MB. k = (m/n)·ln 2 ≈ 6.6 → use 7 hashes. Memory-efficient, but for a single server. For a distributed deployment, partition.

  3. Why can't you delete from a Bloom filter? Multiple items may share each bit. Clearing a bit could remove other items' membership signals — introducing false negatives, which Bloom guarantees never happen. Use counting Bloom (each cell is a small counter, increment on add, decrement on remove) at the cost of more memory.

docs.title: Hard
docs.text:
1. Design a real-time "trending hashtags" service at Twitter scale. Ingest tweet stream → per-shard Count-Min Sketch keyed by hashtag, plus a per-shard min-heap of top-K candidates. Every minute, each shard ships its (CMS, top-K) to a global merger. Global merger combines CMSes element-wise, picks top-K of the combined heaps. Decay the CMS counts on each merge (multiply all cells by 0.95) so old hashtags fade. Query: read the global top-K. Memory: O(1) per shard regardless of hashtag diversity.

  2. You serve a recommendation system; reading from a cold store on each query is 100ms. How can you use a Bloom filter to cut p99? In front of the cold store, maintain a Bloom of all keys known to exist. On a query, hit the Bloom first (in-memory, <1 µs). If "no," return empty immediately. If "yes," go to the cold store. Sized for, say, 10M items at 0.5% false positives, the Bloom is ~17 MB and you save 100ms on every miss — which is most of them in a cold-tail workload. Rebuild the Bloom from a snapshot daily.

  3. A teammate proposes using HLL to count distinct IPs hitting an endpoint per hour. What edge cases do you raise? (a) HLL is biased for very small cardinalities — switch to exact below ~100. (b) HLL gives count, not the actual IPs — you can't list them. © HLLs over different time windows aren't trivially subtractable (HLL(today) - HLL(yesterday) ≠ HLL(today yesterday)). For "new uniques today," compute HLL(today ∪ yesterday) and subtract HLL(yesterday) → approximate, but biased on tails. (d) Persistence: store the HLL register state, not the estimated count, so you can re-aggregate.

docs.title: TL;DR cheat sheet
docs.text:
• Bloom = "is x in set?" — bit array + k hashes — false positives, no false negatives.
  • HyperLogLog = "how many distinct?" — ~12 KB → billions of distincts at ~1% error, merges cleanly.
  • Count-Min Sketch = "frequency of x?" — overestimates, never under.
  • Top-K = CMS + min-heap, or Misra-Gries / Space-Saving.
  • T-Digest / DDSketch = quantiles (p50/p99).
  • Sizing: Bloom m ≈ 1.44 · n · log2(1/p) bits; pick k ≈ ln 2 · m/n.
  • Always verify Bloom positives in the source of truth.
  • No deletes from plain Bloom — use counting variant or rebuild.
  • HLL biased at low cardinalities; switch to exact below a threshold.

docs.title: Go deeper
docs.text:
• "Space/Time Trade-offs in Hash Coding with Allowable Errors" — Burton Bloom, 1970 (the original paper)
  • "HyperLogLog: the analysis of a near-optimal cardinality estimation algorithm" — Flajolet et al., 2007
  • "An Improved Data Stream Summary: The Count-Min Sketch and its Applications" — Cormode & Muthukrishnan
  • Redis Probabilistic Data Structures docs
  • "Approximate Membership Query Filters" — survey by Graf & Lemire

docs.title: 77 — Stream Processing: Flink, Kafka Streams, Windowing, Watermarks
docs.text: Phase 10 • Modern Additions • Topic 77/77

docs.title: Definition (interview-ready)
docs.text: Stream processing is computation over an unbounded sequence of events with low latency, typically driven by Kafka or another log. Apache Flink is a distributed engine with sophisticated state management, event-time semantics, watermarks, and exactly-once guarantees. Kafka Streams is a library (not a cluster) that runs inside your app, scaling via Kafka consumer-group rebalancing and using Kafka topics as state-store backups. Windowing chops the infinite stream into bounded buckets (tumbling, sliding, session); watermarks are the engine's mechanism for deciding "no more late events of this kind will arrive" so it can emit results.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
Batch (Spark daily job) gives correct answers at high latency. Stream processing gives "good enough now" results in seconds, which most modern products need: fraud detection, real-time pricing, live dashboards, recommendation features, ad attribution, alerting. The hard parts — out-of-order events, late data, exactly-once, large state, restart-from-savepoint — are the topics interviews dig into.

flowchart LR
    K[(Kafka topic)] --> F[Stream processor]
    F --> WIN[Windowing<br/>tumbling 1min]
    WIN --> AGG[aggregate per key]
    AGG --> STATE[(RocksDB state<br/>checkpointed to S3)]
    AGG --> OUT[(output topic<br/>or sink)]
    F -. watermark .-> WIN

docs.title: Event time vs processing time
docs.text:
• Event time = when the event happened (timestamp in the payload).
  • Processing time = when the engine sees it (wall clock).
  • Out-of-order arrival is normal: an event from t=10:00:05 may arrive at t=10:00:12.
  • Correct results require event-time windowing. Processing-time is faster but wrong on retries, replays, or burst recovery.

docs.title: Watermarks
docs.text:
A watermark W(t) is a promise from the source: "no events with event time < t will arrive after this point." When watermark passes the window end, the engine closes and emits the window.

sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant S as Source
    participant W as Window 10:00-10:01
    S->>W: event t=10:00:05
    S->>W: event t=10:00:48
    S->>W: event t=10:00:30 (late)
    S->>W: watermark 10:01:05 (allowed lateness 5s)
    W->>W: close window, emit result
    S->>W: event t=10:00:55 (very late)
    Note over W: routed to side-output, not the window
  • Tight watermarks → low latency, more "late" data rejected.
  • Loose watermarks → higher latency, more correctness.
  • Standard approach: watermark = max_event_time_seen - allowed_lateness.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• Tumbling: fixed, non-overlapping (every minute). Most common.
  • Sliding: fixed-size, overlapping (every 10s, size 1min). Higher emission rate.
  • Session: keyed gap-based ("close when idle 5 min"). User-session analytics.
  • Global / count-based: not time, but event count (every N events).
flowchart LR
    subgraph TUMB["Tumbling 1m"]
        T1[10:00 → 10:01]
        T2[10:01 → 10:02]
    end
    subgraph SLID["Sliding 1m / step 10s"]
        S1[10:00:00 → 10:01:00]
        S2[10:00:10 → 10:01:10]
        S3[10:00:20 → 10:01:20]
    end
    subgraph SESS["Session (gap=5m)"]
        E1[event] --- E2[event] --- E3[5min gap] --- E4[event]
    end

docs.title: State
docs.text:
A stream job's state is keyed state (per-key counters, dictionaries) and operator state (broadcast configs, source offsets). Sources of correctness pain:

  • State must be fault-tolerant → checkpointed to durable store (S3, GCS).
  • On restart, restore from latest checkpoint → resume processing exactly where you left off.
  • For Flink: RocksDB-backed state, periodic snapshots, chained barriers propagate through the DAG to ensure all operators checkpoint at the same logical instant.

docs.title: Exactly-once
docs.text:
Misunderstood. Three flavours:

Flavour What it guarantees At-most-once may drop, never duplicate At-least-once may duplicate, never drop Exactly-once (effectively-once) side-effects happen exactly once, even on retries

Real exactly-once requires: - Source replay (Kafka has this). - Idempotent or transactional sink (Kafka transactions; Iceberg/Delta commits; idempotent upserts in DB). - Operator state checkpointed atomically with the input offsets.

Flink calls this end-to-end exactly-once when source + sink support it. Most "exactly-once" claims you hear are at-least-once + idempotent consumer downstream.

docs.title: Backpressure
docs.text: If a downstream operator slows down, the engine must signal upstream operators to slow down too — otherwise queues unbounded-grow. Flink propagates backpressure via TCP buffer fullness. Kafka Streams via consumer pause. Symptoms: rising consumer lag.

docs.title: Flink architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    JM[JobManager<br/>scheduling + checkpointing] --> TM1[TaskManager 1]
    JM --> TM2[TaskManager 2]
    JM --> TM3[TaskManager N]
    subgraph TM1
        SUB1[source subtask] --> OP1[map] --> KEYED1[keyBy] --> WIN1[window]
        WIN1 --> RDB1[(RocksDB local)]
    end
    subgraph TM2
        SUB2[source subtask] --> OP2[map] --> KEYED2[keyBy] --> WIN2[window]
        WIN2 --> RDB2[(RocksDB local)]
    end
    JM -.checkpoint trigger.-> TM1
    JM -.checkpoint trigger.-> TM2
    RDB1 -. snapshot .-> S3[(S3 / GCS checkpoints)]
    RDB2 -. snapshot .-> S3

docs.title: Kafka Streams architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    APP1[Your app instance 1<br/>KStream library] -->|consume partitions 0-3| KIN[(input topic)]
    APP2[App 2] -->|consume partitions 4-7| KIN
    APP1 -->|state| ST1[(local RocksDB)]
    APP2 -->|state| ST2[(local RocksDB)]
    ST1 -. changelog .-> KCH[(changelog topic)]
    ST2 -. changelog .-> KCH
    APP1 --> KOUT[(output topic)]
    APP2 --> KOUT
  • No cluster of its own — it's a library you embed in your app/JVM.
  • Scales by adding instances → Kafka rebalances partitions → state migrates by replaying the changelog topic.
  • Simpler operationally if you already run Kafka + JVM apps; less powerful than Flink (no event-time guarantees as strong, simpler windowing, no real Python ecosystem).

docs.title: Flink vs Kafka Streams vs Spark Structured Streaming
docs.text: Flink Kafka Streams Spark Structured Streaming Model true streaming (per-event) true streaming (per-event) micro-batch (small batches) Cluster yes (JobManager/TM) no (library) yes State RocksDB + checkpoints RocksDB + changelog topic versioned KV Latency tens of ms tens of ms seconds (~100ms in continuous mode) Throughput very high high very high Watermarks first-class limited yes Languages Java, Scala, Python (PyFlink) Java, Scala Scala, Python, SQL Best for low-latency complex pipelines "stream processing inside my service" unified batch+stream on existing Spark infra

docs.title: Real-world examples
docs.text:
• Uber: Flink for surge pricing, ETA estimation, fraud — sub-second feature pipelines.
  • Netflix: Flink for content recommendations, anomaly detection, payment risk.
  • Alibaba: Flink at massive scale (powers Singles Day real-time dashboards).
  • Pinterest: Flink for ML feature freshness.
  • Stripe: Kafka Streams for fraud signals embedded in services.
  • Robinhood, Coinbase: Flink for real-time market data and risk.
  • Cloudflare: Materialize / ksqlDB-style streams for live analytics.

docs.title: Common pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using processing time when you mean event time. Replays, recoveries, backfills will silently corrupt aggregates.
  • No allowed-lateness. Real event streams always have stragglers; without lateness, every late event is lost.
  • Unbounded state. A keyBy on an ever-growing key space (e.g., raw UUIDs) accumulates state forever. Add TTL or scope by window.
  • No state size monitoring. RocksDB grows; checkpoints get expensive; eventually slow snapshots block processing.
  • Treating "exactly-once" as automatic. It's a chain — break any link and you're at-least-once. Verify sink supports transactions or is idempotent.
  • Skew on a hot key. All events for "celebrity_user_id" land on one operator instance. Mitigate by adding a salt: keyBy = (key, hash(event) % N) then union → aggregate.
  • Restart from scratch instead of from savepoint. Restoring from a savepoint preserves state; restart from offset 0 reprocesses everything (correct only if everything is idempotent).
  • Kafka Streams with too few partitions. Parallelism is capped at # of input partitions. Repartition early if you need more.
  • Side-effects in operators. Calling an external API from a map() adds latency and breaks exactly-once. Use async I/O with checkpointed deduplication.

docs.title: Easy
docs.text:
1. What's the difference between stream and batch processing? Batch: process bounded data, results minutes to hours later. Stream: process unbounded data, results in milliseconds to seconds. Stream pays for low latency with more complex state and ordering semantics.

  2. What's a watermark? A guarantee from the engine that "no event with event-time earlier than W will arrive." Used to decide when to close a window and emit.

docs.title: Medium
docs.text:
1. Tumbling vs sliding vs session windows — when do you use each? Tumbling: simple periodic aggregates ("requests per minute"). Sliding: smoothed metrics with overlapping windows ("5-minute moving average updated every 10s"). Session: per-user activity bursts ("page views per session, defined by 5-min inactivity gap").

  2. Your Flink job is correct in dev but produces wrong aggregates after restart in prod. Why? Likely processing-time windows + unaligned source replay → events from before the restart land in different windows than originally. Switch to event-time windows with watermarks. Or: the sink isn't transactional, so the same outputs are emitted twice on restart; switch to a sink with exactly-once support, or make it idempotent.

  3. Compare Flink vs Kafka Streams for a stream-processing project. Decision criteria? Pick Kafka Streams if: (a) you already run Kafka and JVM apps, (b) you want stream logic embedded next to your service code, © low-to-moderate scale, (d) simpler operational model. Pick Flink if: (a) you need very low latency with strong event-time + watermark semantics, (b) state grows large (tens of GB+), © you have non-JVM language requirements (PyFlink), (d) sophisticated joins / multi-stream processing, (e) you can afford to operate the cluster.

docs.title: Hard
docs.text:
1. Design real-time fraud detection on credit card swipes — 10K transactions/sec. Kafka topic transactions partitioned by card_id. Flink job: keyBy(card_id) → per-card sliding window (last 5 minutes) → compute features (count, sum, distinct merchants, geo-diff from last) → feed to a model served as an async I/O call (with cache). If risk_score > threshold, emit to alerts topic. State sized: ~10M active cards × ~1 KB feature state = ~10 GB across the cluster. Checkpoint to S3 every 30 s. Watermarks at max_event_time - 30s. Exactly-once with Kafka source + transactional Kafka sink.

  2. You're seeing growing consumer lag in production Flink. Walk through diagnosis. Check (a) backpressure — Flink UI shows the slow operator; usually a sink or a stateful op. (b) State size — RocksDB compactions or large state explosion; check disk usage. © Skew — heatmap of subtask throughput; identify a hot key. (d) Checkpoint duration — slow checkpoints block processing. (e) GC pressure in JVM. Fix per cause: scale out, repartition / salt hot key, async I/O for sink, tune checkpoint storage.

  3. Explain end-to-end exactly-once between Kafka → Flink → Postgres. Source: Kafka consumer commits offsets only as part of Flink checkpoints. Operators: state snapshotted atomically with the offset via barriers. Sink: two-phase commit — Flink writes to Postgres in a transaction prepared at the input checkpoint, then committed when the checkpoint finishes. On failure: restore state from checkpoint, replay source from last committed offset; uncommitted Postgres transactions roll back; redo and commit. Result: every input event affects Postgres exactly once.

docs.title: TL;DR cheat sheet
docs.text:
• Event time, not processing time. Always.
  • Watermark = "no more events before t" → triggers window close.
  • Allowed lateness so late stragglers still update results.
  • Windows: tumbling (periodic), sliding (smoothed), session (gap-based).
  • State: keyed + operator; in RocksDB; checkpointed to durable store.
  • Exactly-once is a chain: source replay + atomic checkpoints + transactional or idempotent sink.
  • Flink = cluster, complex, low latency, strong semantics. Kafka Streams = library in your app, simpler, leans on Kafka for everything.
  • Backpressure propagates via slow buffers → upstream slows automatically.
  • Skew fix: salt the key, then aggregate.
  • Always monitor: consumer lag, state size, checkpoint duration, GC.

docs.title: Go deeper
docs.text:
• "Streaming Systems" by Tyler Akidau et al. — the canonical book (concepts apply to all engines).
  • Flink Docs — Event Time & Watermarks
  • Kafka Streams docs
  • "The Dataflow Model" — Akidau, Bradshaw, et al. (Google, 2015) — original watermark paper
  • "Lightweight Asynchronous Snapshots for Distributed Dataflows" — Carbone et al. (Flink checkpointing paper)

docs.title: Phase 11 · DSA — Interview Prep
docs.text:
Learn algorithms by watching them run. Every algorithm here ships with a step-by-step visualization where you see the state evolve and the corresponding line of code light up. Pattern-first, the way working engineers think about problems, the way HelloInterview and USACO Guide structure them.

00 Foundations

Big-O, recursion, the pattern map — read this first if you're rusty.

01 Two Pointers

Pairs of indices walking the array — converge from ends, slow/fast, partition.

02 Sliding Window

Maintain a window [l, r] over a sequence; expand right, shrink left.

03 Binary Search

Halve the search space — over indices, over answers, over rotated arrays.

04 BFS

Layered exploration — shortest path in unweighted graphs and grids.

05 DFS

Go deep first — flood fill, cycle detection, tree traversals.

06 Backtracking

Try, recurse, undo — subsets, permutations, N-queens, word search.

07 Heap / Priority Queue

Top-K, median of stream, K closest, scheduling — anywhere you need fast min/max.

08 Graph Algorithms

Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, MST, Union-Find, topological sort, SCC.

09 Dynamic Programming

Tabulation vs memoization — Fibonacci, knapsack, LIS, LCS, edit distance.

10 Greedy

Local choice → global optimum when an exchange argument holds.

11 Trie

Prefix trees for autocomplete, word dictionaries, multi-pattern search.

12 Bit Manipulation

XOR tricks, popcount DP, bitmask DP for exponential state compression.

13 Sorting

Bubble, insertion, selection, merge, quick, heap, counting, radix.

14 Linked List

Reverse, cycle, merge, LRU cache — pointer surgery patterns.

15 Monotonic Stack

Next greater element, daily temperatures, largest rectangle.

docs.title: How to use this section
docs.text:
1. Pick a pattern — the cards above. Start with the ones that come up most: Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Binary Search, BFS, DFS, DP.
  2. Read the explanation — every page has the algorithm in plain English, intuition, complexity, and pitfalls.
  3. Step through the visualization — every page has an interactive widget with Play / Step / Reset and a code panel that highlights the currently-executing line. Click step › and watch the state evolve.
  4. Practice on LeetCode — every page ends with curated practice problems (top ~10 per pattern) ranked easy → hard.

docs.title: Why pattern-first
docs.text: Interview problems are rarely truly novel — they're variations on a small set of patterns. Once you can recognize the pattern in the first 60 seconds of reading the problem ("this is sliding window because we want the longest contiguous range satisfying X"), the implementation becomes mechanical. This section trains pattern recognition.

docs.title: Time / space cheat sheet
docs.text:
Operation Array Sorted Array Hash Map BST (balanced) Heap Trie Lookup O(n) O(log n) O(1) avg O(log n) O(n) O(L) Insert O(1) end / O(n) middle O(n) O(1) avg O(log n) O(log n) O(L) Delete O(n) O(n) O(1) avg O(log n) O(log n) O(L) Min/Max O(n) O(1) O(n) O(log n) O(1) O(L) Range O(n) O(log n + k) O(n) O(log n + k) — —

L = key length. k = output size.

docs.title: External references
docs.text:
• HelloInterview — Learn Code · interview-focused, pattern-first.
  • USACO Guide · competitive-programming grade content, very thorough.
  • NeetCode 150 / 250 · LeetCode list mapped to patterns.
  • CP Algorithms · reference implementations and proofs.

docs.title: DSA · 00 · Foundations
docs.text:
Three short pages to get the basics right before you go pattern-hunting.

01 Big-O & complexity analysis

How to count operations without running code — the language of interviews.

02 Recursion fundamentals

Base case, recursive case, call stack. Memoization. Tail calls.

03 Common patterns map

How to spot which pattern fits — keywords in the problem statement.

docs.title: 01 — Big-O & Complexity Analysis
docs.text: Foundations • ⅓

docs.title: What it is
docs.text: Big-O describes how an algorithm's runtime or space scales with input size n, ignoring constants and lower-order terms. It's an upper bound on growth. Interviewers expect you to state Big-O for time and space before you write code.

docs.title: The growth ladder
docs.text:
From fastest to slowest:

Big-O Name Example O(1) constant hash lookup, array index O(log n) logarithmic binary search, balanced BST O(n) linear scan an array O(n log n) linearithmic merge sort, heapsort O(n²) quadratic bubble sort, naïve 2-loop O(2ⁿ) exponential brute-force subsets O(n!) factorial brute-force permutations

A 100× jump in n at O(n log n) costs ~700× more work; at O(2ⁿ) it's astronomical.

docs.title: How to derive Big-O
docs.text:
1. Count loops — nested loops multiply: two nested loops over n is O(n²).
  2. Count work inside the loop — if each iteration does O(log n), the total is O(n log n).
  3. Recurrences — solve with the Master Theorem (T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n) — that's merge sort).
  4. Drop constants — O(3n) → O(n). 2n + 100 is still O(n).
  5. Keep the dominant term — O(n² + n) → O(n²).

docs.title: Common pitfalls
docs.text:
• s.contains(x) in a Python list is O(n), but in a set it's O(1). Pick your data structures.
  • String concatenation in a loop is O(n²) in many languages — use a builder / join.
  • Recursive Fibonacci is O(2ⁿ) without memoization, O(n) with.
  • Sorting plus a linear pass is O(n log n), not O(n) — the sort dominates.

docs.title: Amortized analysis
docs.text:
When an operation is usually fast but occasionally expensive, average the cost across many operations.

  • ArrayList.add() is O(1) amortized — capacity doubling makes the rare resize cost a single O(n) for each O(n) ops elsewhere.
  • Union-Find with path compression is O(α(n)) amortized — practically constant.

docs.title: Space complexity
docs.text:
Same idea, but for memory:

  • Recursion uses O(depth) stack space.
  • A DP table of size n × m uses O(n·m) space.
  • "In-place" usually means O(1) extra space (excluding the input itself).

docs.title: The interview move
docs.text:
When you sketch a solution, announce your Big-O before coding:

"I'll use a hash map to dedupe, then a single pass — that's O(n) time, O(n) space."

If the interviewer flinches, ask if they want better (e.g. O(1) space using two pointers). You've just demoed seniority.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text: Read Recursion fundamentals — it's the prerequisite for DFS, backtracking, and DP. Then Common patterns map to learn how to recognize which pattern a problem wants.

docs.title: 02 — Recursion fundamentals
docs.text: Foundations • ⅔

docs.title: The shape
docs.text:
A recursive function solves a problem by:

  1. Base case — a small input it can answer directly (no recursion).
  2. Recursive case — reduce the problem to a smaller version of itself, then combine.

Miss the base case → infinite recursion → stack overflow. Miss the reduction → same.

def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1: return 1        # base case
    return n * factorial(n - 1) # recursive case

docs.title: The call stack
docs.text:
Each call gets its own frame: local variables, parameters, return address. The stack grows on every call and shrinks on every return. Most languages limit recursion depth to ~1000–10000 frames before a StackOverflowError.

For deep recursion (e.g. tree depth in the millions), convert to an explicit stack iteratively — same complexity, no recursion limit.

docs.title: Tail recursion
docs.text: A function is tail-recursive if the recursive call is the last thing it does. Some compilers/runtimes (Scala, Scheme, Kotlin with tailrec) optimize this into a loop — no stack growth. Most do not (Python, Java, JavaScript) — convert manually.

docs.title: Memoization — turn O(2ⁿ) into O(n)
docs.text:
Naïve Fibonacci:

def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)   # recomputes the same subproblems

This is O(2ⁿ). Cache the answers:

from functools import lru_cache
@lru_cache
def fib(n):
    if n < 2: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

Now O(n) time, O(n) space. Memoization = top-down DP.

docs.title: Recursion vs iteration
docs.text:
Recursion Iteration Natural for trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer Natural for arrays, fixed loops Easier to write for backtracking No stack-overflow risk O(depth) stack space O(1) extra space Slower in practice (function call overhead) Faster

In interviews, prefer the clearer one. If the interviewer asks for the iterative version, convert it.

docs.title: Recurrence relations
docs.text:
A recursive function's runtime obeys a recurrence:

  • Merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n)
  • Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → O(log n)
  • Naïve Fibonacci: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) → O(2ⁿ)

The Master Theorem solves T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d):

  • a > b^d → O(n^{log_b a})
  • a = b^d → O(n^d log n)
  • a < b^d → O(n^d)

docs.title: Patterns where recursion shines
docs.text:
• Tree traversal — recurse on children.
  • Backtracking — try a choice, recurse, undo.
  • Divide and conquer — split, recurse, merge.
  • DP top-down — solve subproblems lazily with memoization.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text: Common patterns map — learn to read a problem and pick the right pattern in 60 seconds.

docs.title: 03 — Common patterns map
docs.text:
Foundations • 3/3

The single most useful interview skill is pattern recognition — reading a problem in 30 seconds and immediately knowing which family of solutions to reach for. This page is a cheat sheet.

docs.title: Keyword → pattern
docs.text: You see in the problem Try "sorted array" + "find" Binary Search "pair" / "triplet" summing to X Two Pointers (sort first) "longest / shortest contiguous subarray with property" Sliding Window "shortest path" + unweighted BFS "shortest path" + weighted, non-negative Dijkstra "shortest path" + negative edges Bellman-Ford "all subsets / all permutations / generate all..." Backtracking "top K" / "K closest" / "K largest" Heap "stream" + "median / running stat" Heap (two heaps) "schedule with dependencies" / "build order" Topological Sort "groups" / "connected components" / "redundant edge" Union-Find "minimum operations to ..." DP "count number of ways" DP "max / min subsequence ..." DP "next greater / previous smaller" Monotonic Stack "intervals" Greedy (sort by start or end) "prefix lookup" / "auto-complete" / "many words" Trie "appears once, rest appear twice" XOR

docs.title: Two-question filter
docs.text:
When you see a problem, ask:

  1. Is the input sorted / can it be sorted? → Binary search, two pointers, greedy on intervals.
  2. Is the output a single value or a structure? → Counting → DP / heap. Generating → backtracking. Yes/no → DFS / BFS / DP.

docs.title: Common red flags
docs.text:
• "All permutations / combinations of ..." but with non-trivial n → backtracking is exponential by nature; check constraints.
  • "On the order of 10⁹" → must be O(log) or O(1) per query; think prefix sums, binary search, hashing.
  • "Cyclic / circular" → trick is often to concatenate the array with itself and do a linear pass.
  • "K matters" → heap of size K → O(n log K) is the usual win.

docs.title: When you don't recognize anything
docs.text:
Fall back to the brute force:

  1. State the brute force out loud, get its complexity, agree it's too slow.
  2. Identify what work is repeated in the brute force. That's almost always the entry point — caching it leads to DP/memoization; eliminating it via a smart data structure leads to a heap or hash.
  3. Ask the interviewer: "Are duplicates allowed? Negatives? What's the range of n?" — constraints shape the algorithm.

docs.title: Pattern coverage
docs.text:
Every page in this section follows the same template:

  1. What it is — interview-ready definition.
  2. When to use — pattern triggers.
  3. The algorithm — clean pseudocode.
  4. Walkthrough — step-by-step on a small example.
  5. Visualization — interactive widget with code-panel highlighting.
  6. Complexity — time and space.
  7. Pitfalls — common bugs.
  8. Practice — top LeetCode problems for the pattern.

Now pick a pattern from the DSA overview and start clicking through.

docs.title: DSA · 01 · Two Pointers
docs.text: Pairs of indices walking the array — converge from ends, slow/fast partition.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The input is a sorted array and the question asks about a pair / triplet with some sum or difference property.
  • The problem says "in place" — remove an element, compact zeros, dedupe — and bans extra memory.
  • You're checking a palindrome or any symmetric property where the ends matter.
  • Two walls / heights define an area, volume, or capacity (container with most water, trapping rain water).
  • A slow/fast split is needed to partition the array (move zeros, sort colors, Dutch flag).

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Maintain two indices — most often l at the left and r at the right, or slow and fast both starting at 0. On each step, look at the elements they point to and make one decision: which pointer advances, and what (if anything) gets written. The invariant is that everything outside [l, r] (or before slow) has already been resolved correctly, so when the pointers meet or cross you're done. The trick is choosing the comparison: arr[l] + arr[r] vs target, height[l] vs height[r], arr[fast] vs arr[slow]. Get the comparison right and the rest is mechanical.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Reverse array in place

The simplest two-pointer move — swap ends and walk inward.

02 Two Sum on sorted array

Converge from both ends, using the sum to decide which pointer moves.

03 Remove duplicates (in place)

Slow/fast partition — slow marks the write head, fast scans.

04 Container With Most Water

Two walls, shrinking width — always move the shorter side.

05 3Sum

Fix one element, two-pointer the rest — the canonical reduction.

06 Trapping Rain Water

Track the running max on each side — the lower side decides water held.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Same direction (slow/fast) vs opposite directions (l/r). Slow/fast partitions in place; opposite-end converges on a sum or area.
  • Need to sort first. 3Sum, 4Sum, K-diff pairs — sorting is the enabling step; budget it into your complexity.
  • Skip duplicates. When the array can contain repeats and the question asks for unique tuples, advance the pointer past equal neighbours after each match.
  • Circular array. Concatenate the array with itself and run a windowed two-pointer over 2n.
  • Linked list flavour. Slow/fast on a list gives you cycle detection (Floyd) and middle-of-list — the same pattern, different cursor type.

docs.title: 01 — Reverse array in place
docs.text: Two Pointers • ⅙

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an array, reverse it in place using O(1) extra space.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The element at index i ultimately belongs at index n - 1 - i. Pair them up: swap (0, n-1), then (1, n-2), and so on, until the two indices meet in the middle. Each swap fixes two positions, so the loop runs exactly n/2 times. The invariant is that everything outside [l, r] is already reversed; everything inside is untouched original order.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Start l = 0, r = n - 1.
  2. While l < r: swap arr[l] and arr[r], then l++, r--.
  3. Stop when the pointers meet or cross.
def reverse(arr):
    l, r = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while l < r:
        arr[l], arr[r] = arr[r], arr[l]
        l += 1
        r -= 1
    return arr

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:

  • Step 1 — l=0, r=4: swap 1 and 5 → [5, 2, 3, 4, 1].
  • Step 2 — l=1, r=3: swap 2 and 4 → [5, 4, 3, 2, 1].
  • Step 3 — l=2, r=2: pointers meet, loop exits; middle element stays in place.

For even-length arrays [1, 2, 3, 4], the loop ends with l=2, r=1 after two swaps.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using l <= r swaps the middle element with itself — harmless but wasteful, and a bug if the swap has side effects.
  • Off-by-one when seeding r = n instead of r = n - 1.
  • For strings in immutable-string languages (Java, Python str), you can't swap in place — convert to a char array first.
  • Reversing only a subrange [i, j] is the same loop with different seeds — don't rewrite the logic, parameterize it.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 344. Reverse String (Easy) — the canonical in-place reverse on a char array.
  • 541. Reverse String II (Easy) — reverse every K-th chunk; reuse the subrange variant.
  • 345. Reverse Vowels of a String (Easy) — two pointers, skip non-vowels on each side.
  • 917. Reverse Only Letters (Easy) — same trick, letter-only filter.
  • 125. Valid Palindrome (Easy) — compare-then-advance instead of swap.
  • 557. Reverse Words in a String III (Easy) — reverse each word's range independently.
  • 189. Rotate Array (Medium) — three reverses: whole, prefix, suffix.
  • 151. Reverse Words in a String (Medium) — reverse the whole string, then each word.
  • 234. Palindrome Linked List (Easy) — reverse the second half, then compare halves.
  • 25. Reverse Nodes in K-Group (Hard) — pointer surgery on linked-list chunks.

docs.title: 02 — Two Sum on sorted array
docs.text: Two Pointers • 2/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a sorted array and a target T, return the indices of two elements that sum to T (or report that none exist). O(1) extra space.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: If arr[l] + arr[r] < T, the smallest available pair on the right (arr[l] + arr[r]) already underflows — every pair using l underflows too, so l must move right. Symmetrically, if the sum overflows, r must move left. Each move discards a whole row or column of candidate pairs, so the algorithm visits at most n pointer positions total. The invariant: every pair we've skipped is provably not the answer.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Start l = 0, r = n - 1.
  2. Compute s = arr[l] + arr[r].
  3. If s == T, return (l, r).
  4. If s < T, increment l. Else decrement r.
  5. Repeat until l >= r.
def two_sum_sorted(arr, T):
    l, r = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while l < r:
        s = arr[l] + arr[r]
        if s == T:
            return (l, r)
        if s < T:
            l += 1
        else:
            r -= 1
    return None

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On [1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 11], target 10:

  • Step 1 — l=0, r=5: 1 + 11 = 12 > 10 → r--.
  • Step 2 — l=0, r=4: 1 + 8 = 9 < 10 → l++.
  • Step 3 — l=1, r=4: 3 + 8 = 11 > 10 → r--.
  • Step 4 — l=1, r=3: 3 + 6 = 9 < 10 → l++.
  • Step 5 — l=2, r=3: 4 + 6 = 10 → return (2, 3).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the array is already sorted — if it isn't, a hash-map solution in O(n) time / O(n) space is the right call.
  • LeetCode 167 uses 1-indexed return values — read the problem statement carefully.
  • With duplicates and a "find all unique pairs" twist, advance l and r past equal neighbours after each match.
  • For 3Sum / 4Sum, the outer loop fixes one (or two) elements; only the inner pair is two-pointer. Don't try to two-pointer everything at once.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 167. Two Sum II — Input Array Is Sorted (Easy) — the canonical statement; mind the 1-indexing.
  • 1. Two Sum (Easy) — unsorted variant; hash-map is the cleaner path.
  • 977. Squares of a Sorted Array (Easy) — merge from both ends because squares grow at both extremes.
  • 633. Sum of Square Numbers (Medium) — same template over the implicit range `[0, sqrt(c)]`.
  • 653. Two Sum IV — Input is a BST (Easy) — in-order iterators from both ends mimic two pointers.
  • 88. Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — write from the tail to avoid overwriting.
  • 532. K-diff Pairs in an Array (Medium) — same idea with difference instead of sum.
  • 15. 3Sum (Medium) — fix one element, two-pointer the rest.
  • 16. 3Sum Closest (Medium) — track the running best difference.
  • 18. 4Sum (Medium) — two nested fixed indices, then two-pointer.

docs.title: 03 — Remove duplicates from sorted array
docs.text: Two Pointers • 3/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in place so each element appears once. Return the new length k; the first k slots should hold the unique values. O(1) extra space.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Because the array is sorted, all duplicates of a value sit next to each other. Use a slow/fast pair: slow is the write head pointing at the next slot to fill; fast scans forward. Whenever arr[fast] is new (differs from arr[slow]), bump slow and copy. The invariant is that arr[0..slow] is the deduped prefix at all times — and because slow <= fast, the write never overwrites unread data.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. If the array is empty, return 0.
  2. Set slow = 0. For fast = 1 .. n-1:
  3. If arr[fast] != arr[slow], increment slow, then arr[slow] = arr[fast].
  4. Return slow + 1.
def remove_duplicates(arr):
    if not arr:
        return 0
    slow = 0
    for fast in range(1, len(arr)):
        if arr[fast] != arr[slow]:
            slow += 1
            arr[slow] = arr[fast]
    return slow + 1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4]:

  • Step 1 — slow=0, fast=1: arr[1]=1 == arr[0]=1, skip.
  • Step 2 — slow=0, fast=2: arr[2]=2 != 1, advance → slow=1, write arr[1]=2 → [1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4].
  • Step 3 — slow=1, fast=3: arr[3]=2 == arr[1]=2, skip.
  • Step 4 — slow=1, fast=4: 3 != 2, advance → slow=2, write arr[2]=3 → [1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 4].
  • Step 5 — slow=2, fast=5: 4 != 3, advance → slow=3, write arr[3]=4 → [1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 4, 4].

Final unique prefix length is 4; the tail past slow is leftover garbage you ignore.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Returning the modified array when the problem expects the length — LeetCode 26 wants k.
  • Comparing arr[fast] != arr[fast - 1] works only because the array is sorted; on unsorted input you'd need a hash set.
  • For "at most twice" (LC 80), keep a counter or compare arr[fast] against arr[slow - 1] — the same template with a wider window.
  • "Move zeros to the end" looks different but is the same slow/fast pattern with a different predicate.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 26. Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array (Easy) — the canonical problem.
  • 27. Remove Element (Easy) — slow/fast with a "skip this value" predicate.
  • 283. Move Zeroes (Easy) — same idea; non-zeros get written forward.
  • 905. Sort Array By Parity (Easy) — partition evens left, odds right.
  • 1089. Duplicate Zeros (Easy) — count, then write from the tail to avoid overwriting.
  • 922. Sort Array By Parity II (Easy) — two write pointers, one for evens and one for odds.
  • 80. Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II (Medium) — allow at most two of each.
  • 88. Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — slow/fast from the tail.
  • 443. String Compression (Medium) — run-length encode in place with a write head.
  • 75. Sort Colors / Dutch National Flag (Medium) — three-way partition with `low`, `mid`, `high`.

docs.title: 04 — Container With Most Water
docs.text: Two Pointers • 4/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given heights h[0..n-1] (vertical lines on the x-axis), pick two lines that together with the x-axis form a container. Return the maximum amount of water it can hold.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Area between l and r is min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l). Start at the widest pair (0, n-1). The width can only shrink, so the only way to win is to find a strictly taller minimum height. Moving the taller side inward can never raise min(h[l], h[r]) past its current shorter side — the new min is still capped by the shorter wall. So always move the shorter side; that's the only move that can possibly improve the answer. The invariant is that every pair we've skipped is dominated by a pair we've already evaluated.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Set l = 0, r = n - 1, best = 0.
  2. While l < r:
  3. Compute area = min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l). Update best.
  4. If h[l] < h[r], advance l. Else retreat r.
  5. Return best.
def max_area(h):
    l, r, best = 0, len(h) - 1, 0
    while l < r:
        area = min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l)
        best = max(best, area)
        if h[l] < h[r]:
            l += 1
        else:
            r -= 1
    return best

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On h = [1, 8, 6, 2, 5, 4, 8, 3, 7]:

  • Step 1 — l=0, r=8: min(1, 7) * 8 = 8. h[l]=1 < 7, so l++.
  • Step 2 — l=1, r=8: min(8, 7) * 7 = 49. h[l]=8 >= 7, so r--.
  • Step 3 — l=1, r=7: min(8, 3) * 6 = 18. h[r]=3 < 8, so r--.
  • Step 4 — l=1, r=6: min(8, 8) * 5 = 40. Tie — by convention move r, so r--.
  • After full sweep, best = 49.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Moving the taller side instead of the shorter — you'd skip the optimal pair.
  • When h[l] == h[r], either move is safe; pick a convention and stick with it.
  • Confusing this with Trapping Rain Water — that one sums water across every column, not the area between two chosen walls.
  • Brute force is O(n²); state that out loud, then derive the linear two-pointer.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 11. Container With Most Water (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 962. Maximum Width Ramp (Medium) — monotonic stack of decreasing heights, then walk from the right.
  • 845. Longest Mountain in Array (Medium) — expand around each peak.
  • 581. Shortest Unsorted Continuous Subarray (Medium) — two-pointer sweep to find the disorder boundary.
  • 1011. Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days (Medium) — binary search on the answer (capacity).
  • 1838. Frequency of the Most Frequent Element (Medium) — sort, then sliding window with operation budget.
  • 152. Maximum Product Subarray (Medium) — track running max and min; not two-pointer but the "shorter side wins" intuition reappears.
  • 84. Largest Rectangle in Histogram (Hard) — monotonic stack; same area-maximization flavour.
  • 42. Trapping Rain Water (Hard) — the two-pointer cousin, summing instead of maximizing.
  • 407. Trapping Rain Water II (Hard) — 2D version with a min-heap.

docs.title: 05 — 3Sum
docs.text: Two Pointers • ⅚

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an integer array, return all unique triplets (a, b, c) with a + b + c == 0. No duplicate triplets in the output.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Sort the array, then reduce 3Sum to 2Sum. Fix i as the smallest element of the triplet; then on the suffix [i+1, n-1], run a two-pointer search for target = -arr[i]. The sort enables both the two-pointer step and the cheap duplicate dedup (just skip equal neighbours). The invariant: once you fix i, every valid (l, r) is found in a single O(n) sweep, so the whole algorithm is O(n²) — the sort is dominated.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Sort arr.
  2. For i from 0 to n - 3:
  3. If arr[i] > 0, break — no negative + non-negative can equal -positive.
  4. If i > 0 and arr[i] == arr[i - 1], skip (duplicate triplet root).
  5. Set l = i + 1, r = n - 1, target = -arr[i].
  6. While l < r:
    • s = arr[l] + arr[r].
    • If s == target: record (arr[i], arr[l], arr[r]); advance l past duplicates and retreat r past duplicates.
    • Else if s < target: l++. Else r--.
def three_sum(arr):
    arr.sort()
    out = []
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n - 2):
        if arr[i] > 0:
            break
        if i > 0 and arr[i] == arr[i - 1]:
            continue
        l, r, target = i + 1, n - 1, -arr[i]
        while l < r:
            s = arr[l] + arr[r]
            if s == target:
                out.append([arr[i], arr[l], arr[r]])
                while l < r and arr[l] == arr[l + 1]:
                    l += 1
                while l < r and arr[r] == arr[r - 1]:
                    r -= 1
                l += 1
                r -= 1
            elif s < target:
                l += 1
            else:
                r -= 1
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On [-1, 0, 1, 2, -1, -4], sorted to [-4, -1, -1, 0, 1, 2]:

  • Step 1 — i=0, arr[0]=-4, target=4: l=1, r=5. -1 + 2 = 1 < 4 → l++. Sweep finds no pair.
  • Step 2 — i=1, arr[1]=-1, target=1: l=2, r=5. -1 + 2 = 1 → record (-1, -1, 2). Advance.
  • Step 3 — l=3, r=4: 0 + 1 = 1 → record (-1, 0, 1).
  • Step 4 — i=2, arr[2]=-1: equals arr[1], skip to avoid duplicates.
  • Step 5 — i=3, arr[3]=0, target=0: l=4, r=5. 1 + 2 = 3 > 0 → r--. Loop exits.

Output: [[-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1]].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n²) space O(1) extra (excluding output)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting to sort first — without sort, you can't skip duplicates cleanly and the two-pointer step doesn't apply.
  • Skipping duplicates only on i but not on l and r — you'll emit duplicate triplets.
  • Not breaking when arr[i] > 0 — an easy pruning that halves the constant factor.
  • Returning indices instead of values when the problem asks for values, or vice versa.
  • Using a hash-set for dedup of triplets — works, but O(n²) extra space; the sort-then-skip approach is cleaner.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 15. 3Sum (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 16. 3Sum Closest (Medium) — track the running best `|sum - target|`.
  • 259. 3Sum Smaller (Medium) — `arr[l] + arr[r] < target` adds `r - l` triplets at once.
  • 1099. Two Sum Less Than K (Easy) — the 2Sum lemma 3Sum builds on.
  • 611. Valid Triangle Number (Medium) — fix the largest side, two-pointer the rest.
  • 923. 3Sum With Multiplicity (Medium) — count combinations using frequency arithmetic.
  • 1010. Pairs of Songs With Total Durations Divisible by 60 (Medium) — modular two-sum.
  • 1679. Max Number of K-Sum Pairs (Medium) — repeated two-sum until exhausted.
  • 18. 4Sum (Medium) — two nested fixed indices, then two-pointer.
  • 454. 4Sum II (Medium) — split into two pairs, hash one half.

docs.title: 06 — Trapping Rain Water
docs.text: Two Pointers • 6/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an elevation map h[0..n-1] (non-negative bar heights), compute how much rainwater is trapped between the bars.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Water above column i is min(maxLeft[i], maxRight[i]) - h[i], clamped at zero. A naive solution precomputes the two max arrays in O(n) space. The two-pointer trick avoids that: maintain leftMax and rightMax as running maxes seen by l and r. Whichever side is currently shorter is the bottleneck — its column's trapped water is fully determined by its own running max (the other side is guaranteed taller). Process that column, advance that pointer, repeat. The invariant: at every step, the shorter side's running max is the true min of left/right max for the column we're settling.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. l = 0, r = n - 1, leftMax = rightMax = 0, total = 0.
  2. While l < r:
  3. If h[l] < h[r]:
    • If h[l] >= leftMax, update leftMax = h[l]. Else add leftMax - h[l] to total.
    • l++.
  4. Else (mirror): update rightMax or accumulate, then r--.
  5. Return total.
def trap(h):
    l, r = 0, len(h) - 1
    left_max = right_max = total = 0
    while l < r:
        if h[l] < h[r]:
            if h[l] >= left_max:
                left_max = h[l]
            else:
                total += left_max - h[l]
            l += 1
        else:
            if h[r] >= right_max:
                right_max = h[r]
            else:
                total += right_max - h[r]
            r -= 1
    return total

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On h = [0, 1, 0, 2, 1, 0, 1, 3, 2, 1, 2, 1]:

  • Step 1 — l=0, r=11, h[l]=0 < h[r]=1: leftMax stays 0, no water, l++.
  • Step 2 — l=1, r=11, h[l]=1 == h[r]=1: take the else branch, rightMax = 1, r--.
  • Step 3 — l=1, r=10, h[l]=1 < h[r]=2: leftMax = 1, no water, l++.
  • Step 4 — l=2, r=10, h[l]=0 < h[r]=2: h[l]=0 < leftMax=1, add 1 - 0 = 1. total = 1.
  • ... continuing the sweep, total ends at 6.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Updating total before deciding the running max — you'd add negative water at the new max column.
  • Tie case h[l] == h[r] — either branch is correct; pick one and be consistent.
  • Confusing this with Container With Most Water — there you pick two walls and ignore everything between; here every column traps water based on its surrounding maxes.
  • Trying to compute it column-by-column with two max(...) calls — that's the brute O(n²); the two-pointer or prefix-max approach beats it.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 42. Trapping Rain Water (Hard) — the canonical problem.
  • 11. Container With Most Water (Medium) — the two-wall cousin.
  • 121. Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock (Easy) — running min, then sweep — same shape.
  • 238. Product of Array Except Self (Medium) — left and right running products; same prefix/suffix idea.
  • 53. Maximum Subarray (Medium) — Kadane's algorithm; running max with reset.
  • 152. Maximum Product Subarray (Medium) — running max *and* min because negatives flip.
  • 1793. Maximum Score of a Good Subarray (Hard) — expand outward from a fixed index, two-pointer on min height.
  • 84. Largest Rectangle in Histogram (Hard) — monotonic stack on heights.
  • 768. Max Chunks To Make Sorted II (Hard) — prefix-max vs suffix-min boundaries.
  • 407. Trapping Rain Water II (Hard) — 2D version with a min-heap from the boundary.

docs.title: DSA · 02 · Sliding Window
docs.text: Maintain a window [l, r] over a sequence; expand right, shrink left.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem asks for the longest / shortest contiguous subarray or substring satisfying some property.
  • A fixed-size window of length k slides across the array (max sum / max average / specific counts).
  • You see phrasing like "at most K of something", "at most K distinct", or "exactly K" — the latter reduces to "at most K minus at most K - 1".
  • The problem involves anagrams / permutations of a target inside a string.
  • Brute force is "for every pair (l, r)" — that's O(n²) and is almost always replaceable by a window.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Keep two indices l and r and a small piece of window state — a counter, a sum, a hash map of character frequencies. Expand: move r right, ingest s[r]. While the window violates the invariant, shrink: evict s[l], move l right. Record the answer when the window is valid. Each index enters and leaves the window at most once, so the whole sweep is O(n) regardless of how nested the inner while looks. The art is choosing the right window state and the right "is this window valid?" check.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Fixed-size window — max sum of K

The simplest variant — slide a length-K window and roll the sum.

02 Longest substring without repeating characters

Variable-size window with a "last seen" hash map.

03 Minimum Window Substring

Shrink-then-record — the hardest of the classic windows.

04 Longest subarray with sum ≤ K

Window monotonicity — when all elements are non-negative, shrinking is monotone.

05 Permutation in String

Fixed-size window matching a frequency map — fast hash equality.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Fixed vs variable size. Fixed: enforce window length == k every step. Variable: the window grows and shrinks based on a predicate.
  • "At most K" vs "exactly K". Compute "at most K" and "at most K - 1"; subtract. A classic trick used in subarrays-with-K-distinct-integers.
  • String windows with frequency maps. Track a matched counter that increments only when a character's count hits the required count, decrements only when it falls past. Saves a costly dict comparison every step.
  • Window over a stream. When n doesn't fit in memory, use a deque for the window contents.
  • Two-pointer vs sliding-window distinction. Both walk indices, but a window keeps state about everything inside [l, r]; pure two-pointer only cares about the endpoints.

docs.title: 01 — Fixed-size sliding window — max sum of K
docs.text: Sliding Window • ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an array and an integer k, return the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of length exactly k.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Two consecutive windows differ by one element on each end. Compute the sum of the first window in O(k), then for each subsequent step roll the sum: add the new right element, subtract the leftmost. Each transition is O(1), so the whole pass is O(n). The invariant is windowSum == sum(arr[l..r]) at every step — never recompute, just adjust.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Compute s = sum(arr[0..k-1]). Set best = s.
  2. For r = k .. n-1:
  3. s += arr[r] - arr[r - k] (add new, drop old).
  4. best = max(best, s).
  5. Return best.
def max_sum_k(arr, k):
    s = sum(arr[:k])
    best = s
    for r in range(k, len(arr)):
        s += arr[r] - arr[r - k]
        best = max(best, s)
    return best

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On arr = [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], k = 3:

  • Step 1 — initial window [2, 1, 5]: s = 8, best = 8.
  • Step 2 — r=3: add arr[3]=1, drop arr[0]=2. s = 7. best stays at 8.
  • Step 3 — r=4: add arr[4]=3, drop arr[1]=1. s = 9. best = 9.
  • Step 4 — r=5: add arr[5]=2, drop arr[2]=5. s = 6. best stays at 9.

Return 9 (window [5, 1, 3]).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Off-by-one on the initial window — make sure the first sum covers exactly arr[0..k-1].
  • Subtracting arr[r - k + 1] instead of arr[r - k] — the element leaving is the one at the old left index.
  • Recomputing the sum each step — that's O(n·k); the whole point of rolling is to drop that factor.
  • For "max of window" (not sum), use a monotonic deque — running max needs more than addition/subtraction.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 643. Maximum Average Subarray I (Easy) — divide the rolled sum by k at the end.
  • 1652. Defuse the Bomb (Easy) — circular rolling window.
  • 2090. K Radius Subarray Averages (Medium) — window of size `2k+1` centered on each index.
  • 1456. Maximum Number of Vowels in a Substring of Given Length (Medium) — same template; the rolling state is a vowel counter.
  • 1100. Find K-Length Substrings With No Repeated Characters (Medium) — rolling frequency map of size k.
  • 567. Permutation in String (Medium) — fixed window matching a frequency map.
  • 438. Find All Anagrams in a String (Medium) — same as 567 but emit all start indices.
  • 1004. Max Consecutive Ones III (Medium) — variable window with a "zeros used" cap of k.
  • 2461. Maximum Sum of Distinct Subarrays With Length K (Medium) — fixed window plus a distinctness check.
  • 239. Sliding Window Maximum (Hard) — max instead of sum; monotonic deque.

docs.title: 02 — Longest substring without repeating characters
docs.text: Sliding Window • ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a string s, return the length of the longest substring with all distinct characters.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Maintain a window [l, r] that always contains distinct characters. Extend by adding s[r]. If s[r] is already inside (we've seen it at index last), the leftmost we can keep l is last + 1. Use a hash map last_seen so this lookup is O(1). The invariant: s[l..r] is always a substring with no repeats, and r - l + 1 is its length. Each index is added once and l only moves right, so the sweep is O(n).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. last_seen = {}, l = 0, best = 0.
  2. For r in 0..n-1:
  3. If s[r] is in last_seen and last_seen[s[r]] >= l, jump l = last_seen[s[r]] + 1.
  4. Update last_seen[s[r]] = r.
  5. best = max(best, r - l + 1).
  6. Return best.
def length_of_longest_substring(s):
    last = {}
    l = best = 0
    for r, ch in enumerate(s):
        if ch in last and last[ch] >= l:
            l = last[ch] + 1
        last[ch] = r
        best = max(best, r - l + 1)
    return best

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On s = "abcabcbb":

  • r=0, 'a': window [0,0]="a", best=1.
  • r=1, 'b': window [0,1]="ab", best=2.
  • r=2, 'c': window [0,2]="abc", best=3.
  • r=3, 'a': 'a' last seen at 0, jump l=1. Window [1,3]="bca", best=3.
  • r=4, 'b': 'b' last seen at 1, jump l=2. Window [2,4]="cab".
  • r=6, 'b': jump again. By r=7, the best is still 3.

Return 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(min(n, σ)) where σ is the alphabet size

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the last_seen[ch] >= l check. A character may have been seen before the current window; you shouldn't shrink past where you already are.
  • Using set and removing characters one-by-one as l advances — correct but more code; the "jump l" trick is cleaner.
  • Updating best before the last_seen update vs after — both work because the window is valid by the time you measure it; pick a convention.
  • For "at most K distinct" extend the same idea — counter map plus a distinct count.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 3. Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 1763. Longest Nice Substring (Easy) — divide-and-conquer or window with case checks.
  • 904. Fruit Into Baskets (Medium) — longest subarray with at most 2 distinct.
  • 159. Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters (Medium) — direct generalization with K=2.
  • 340. Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters (Medium) — generic K.
  • 1208. Get Equal Substrings Within Budget (Medium) — variable window with a cost budget.
  • 1695. Maximum Erasure Value (Medium) — same template with running sum.
  • 424. Longest Repeating Character Replacement (Medium) — window valid when `len - maxFreq <= k`.
  • 1493. Longest Subarray of 1s After Deleting One Element (Medium) — window with at most one zero.
  • 1234. Replace the Substring for Balanced String (Medium) — smallest window whose *outside* is balanced.

docs.title: 03 — Minimum Window Substring
docs.text: Sliding Window • ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given strings s and t, return the shortest substring of s that contains every character of t (with multiplicity). Return "" if none exists.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Two-phase window: expand r until the window covers all of t; shrink l while it still covers; record the answer at each valid shrink; resume expanding. To avoid recomputing "does the window cover t?" on every step, keep a need map (required counts) and a have counter that tracks how many characters have already hit their required count. Increment have only when a character's window-count reaches the requirement; decrement only when it drops below. The window is valid iff have == required_distinct(t). The invariant: each index enters and leaves the window once, giving O(n + m).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Build need = Counter(t). Let required = len(need).
  2. window = {}, have = 0, best = (∞, 0, 0), l = 0.
  3. For r from 0 to n-1:
  4. Update window[s[r]] += 1. If window[s[r]] == need.get(s[r], -1), have += 1.
  5. While have == required:
    • If r - l + 1 < best[0], update best = (r - l + 1, l, r).
    • window[s[l]] -= 1. If window[s[l]] < need.get(s[l], 0), have -= 1.
    • l += 1.
  6. Return s[best[1]:best[2]+1] or "".
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

def min_window(s, t):
    if not t: return ""
    need = Counter(t)
    required = len(need)
    window = defaultdict(int)
    have = 0
    best = (float("inf"), 0, 0)
    l = 0
    for r, ch in enumerate(s):
        window[ch] += 1
        if ch in need and window[ch] == need[ch]:
            have += 1
        while have == required:
            if r - l + 1 < best[0]:
                best = (r - l + 1, l, r)
            lch = s[l]
            window[lch] -= 1
            if lch in need and window[lch] < need[lch]:
                have -= 1
            l += 1
    return "" if best[0] == float("inf") else s[best[1]:best[2]+1]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On s = "ADOBECODEBANC", t = "ABC":

  • Expand to r=5: window "ADOBEC" — has A, B, C → valid. have == 3. Record (6, 0, 5).
  • Shrink: drop A. have falls to 2. l=1. Resume expanding.
  • Expand to r=10: window "DOBECODEB"... eventually "DOBECODEBA", valid again. Record smaller window (...).
  • Continue. Final best is "BANC" (length 4), corresponding to (10..13).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n + m) space O(σ) alphabet size

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Comparing full hash maps on every step (window == need) — that's O(σ) per step and turns the whole thing into O(n·σ). Use the have counter.
  • Incrementing have every time a character is added — only increment when it exactly matches the required count.
  • Forgetting that t can contain duplicates — need['A'] = 2 means the window needs two A's.
  • Returning indices instead of the substring — read the signature.
  • Using this pattern for "contains as a subsequence" (not contiguous) — wrong tool; that's LC 727.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 76. Minimum Window Substring (Hard) — the canonical problem.
  • 567. Permutation in String (Medium) — fixed-size window matching exactly.
  • 438. Find All Anagrams in a String (Medium) — emit all matching window starts.
  • 209. Minimum Size Subarray Sum (Medium) — shrink while sum is still >= target.
  • 1004. Max Consecutive Ones III (Medium) — shrink when zero-count exceeds k.
  • 1208. Get Equal Substrings Within Budget (Medium) — variable window with cost budget.
  • 1234. Replace the Substring for Balanced String (Medium) — smallest window whose outside is balanced.
  • 1438. Longest Continuous Subarray With Absolute Diff Less Than or Equal to Limit (Medium) — window with two monotonic deques.
  • 727. Minimum Window Subsequence (Hard) — subsequence, not substring; two-pointer DP.
  • 30. Substring with Concatenation of All Words (Hard) — word-level sliding window.
  • 632. Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists (Hard) — heap-driven multi-pointer window.

docs.title: 04 — Longest subarray with sum ≤ K
docs.text: Sliding Window • ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an array of non-negative integers and an integer K, return the length of the longest contiguous subarray whose sum is at most K.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Non-negativity makes the window monotone: adding an element can only increase the sum, so if [l, r] violates sum <= K, every wider window does too — l must advance. That's the magic that makes the sliding window correct. Maintain a running s; expand r, and whenever s > K, shrink l until s <= K again. The invariant: at every step, s == sum(arr[l..r]) and the window is the longest valid window ending at r. Each index moves at most twice, so the sweep is O(n). Without the non-negativity assumption, you'd need prefix sums + a BIT or deque trick.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. l = 0, s = 0, best = 0.
  2. For r in 0..n-1:
  3. s += arr[r].
  4. While s > K: s -= arr[l]; l += 1.
  5. best = max(best, r - l + 1).
  6. Return best.
def longest_sum_at_most_k(arr, K):
    l = s = best = 0
    for r in range(len(arr)):
        s += arr[r]
        while s > K:
            s -= arr[l]
            l += 1
        best = max(best, r - l + 1)
    return best

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On arr = [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], K = 8:

  • r=0: s=2. Window [2], best=1.
  • r=1: s=3. Window [2,1], best=2.
  • r=2: s=8. Window [2,1,5], best=3.
  • r=3: s=9 > 8. Shrink: drop arr[0]=2, s=7, l=1. Window [1,5,1], best=3.
  • r=4: s=10 > 8. Shrink: drop arr[1]=1, s=9. Still > 8. Drop arr[2]=5, s=4, l=3. Window [1,3], best=3.
  • r=5: s=6. Window [1,3,2], best=3.

Return 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Negative numbers break the monotonicity. With negatives, shrinking left can make the sum grow, so the window can't shrink-only. Switch to prefix sums + sorted structure.
  • Confusing <= K with == K. For exact-K problems, use a hash map of prefix sums.
  • Forgetting to update best after the inner while — the window must be valid first.
  • "Minimum size subarray sum ≥ K" (LC 209) is the mirror — shrink while s >= K, record on shrink.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 209. Minimum Size Subarray Sum (Medium) — the mirror: shortest subarray with sum >= K.
  • 1004. Max Consecutive Ones III (Medium) — same template; treat each zero as cost 1.
  • 1208. Get Equal Substrings Within Budget (Medium) — window with a numeric cost budget.
  • 1493. Longest Subarray of 1s After Deleting One Element (Medium) — at most one zero allowed.
  • 713. Subarray Product Less Than K (Medium) — multiply instead of add; count subarrays.
  • 904. Fruit Into Baskets (Medium) — at most two distinct values.
  • 2461. Maximum Sum of Distinct Subarrays With Length K (Medium) — fixed window with distinctness.
  • 930. Binary Subarrays With Sum (Medium) — "exactly K = atMost(K) - atMost(K - 1)".
  • 1248. Count Number of Nice Subarrays (Medium) — same atMost trick on odd-counts.
  • 992. Subarrays with K Different Integers (Hard) — the canonical "exactly K" reduction.

docs.title: 05 — Permutation in String
docs.text: Sliding Window • 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given strings s1 and s2, return true if some permutation of s1 appears as a contiguous substring of s2. Equivalently: does any window of length |s1| in s2 have the same character frequencies as s1?

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A permutation is fully characterized by its character-frequency map. Slide a fixed-size window of length k = |s1| across s2. Maintain a rolling frequency map for the window; compare against need = Counter(s1). Naively comparing two maps every step is O(σ) per step — replace it with a match counter matches that increments when a character's window-count equals need[ch], decrements when it stops matching. The window is a permutation iff matches == 26 (over the lowercase-English alphabet). The invariant: each step does O(1) work, so the total is O(n + σ).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. If |s1| > |s2|, return false.
  2. Build need and have as size-26 arrays. Initialize matches: number of letters where have[i] == need[i].
  3. For r in k..n-1:
  4. Add s2[r] to have; update matches.
  5. Remove s2[r - k] from have; update matches.
  6. If matches == 26, return true.
  7. Final check on the initial window, then return false.
def check_inclusion(s1, s2):
    k, n = len(s1), len(s2)
    if k > n: return False
    need, have = [0]*26, [0]*26
    for ch in s1: need[ord(ch) - 97] += 1
    for i in range(k): have[ord(s2[i]) - 97] += 1
    matches = sum(1 for i in range(26) if need[i] == have[i])
    if matches == 26: return True
    for r in range(k, n):
        ai = ord(s2[r]) - 97
        have[ai] += 1
        if have[ai] == need[ai]: matches += 1
        elif have[ai] == need[ai] + 1: matches -= 1
        bi = ord(s2[r - k]) - 97
        have[bi] -= 1
        if have[bi] == need[bi]: matches += 1
        elif have[bi] == need[bi] - 1: matches -= 1
        if matches == 26: return True
    return False

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On s1 = "ab", s2 = "eidbaooo", k = 2:

  • Initial window "ei": have['e']=1, have['i']=1; nowhere near need = {a:1, b:1}. matches < 26.
  • Slide to "id": still off.
  • Slide to "db": have a b now; closer.
  • Slide to "ba": have = {a:1, b:1}, exactly equal to need. matches == 26. Return true.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n + σ) space O(σ) alphabet size

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Comparing full frequency maps every step — works, but O(n·σ); use the matches trick to keep it O(n).
  • Off-by-one when initializing the first window — it's s2[0..k-1], not s2[0..k].
  • Updating matches on the wrong side — increment when count just hit the requirement; decrement when it just left. Use exact equality checks; >= is wrong.
  • For "find all anagrams" (LC 438), the engine is identical — just emit l whenever matches == 26 instead of returning.
  • Unicode strings — replace the [26] array with a hash map.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 567. Permutation in String (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 242. Valid Anagram (Easy) — the frequency-map building block.
  • 438. Find All Anagrams in a String (Medium) — emit every matching window start.
  • 49. Group Anagrams (Medium) — key by sorted string or by frequency tuple.
  • 1456. Maximum Number of Vowels in a Substring of Given Length (Medium) — fixed window with rolling counter.
  • 1100. Find K-Length Substrings With No Repeated Characters (Medium) — fixed window, all-distinct check.
  • 187. Repeated DNA Sequences (Medium) — fixed window of size 10 plus a seen-set.
  • 76. Minimum Window Substring (Hard) — variable-size cousin.
  • 30. Substring with Concatenation of All Words (Hard) — word-level permutation matching.
  • 1396. Design Underground System (Medium) — not a window per se, but the running-stat hash-map pattern shows up everywhere.

docs.title: DSA · 03 · Binary Search
docs.text: Halve the search space — over indices, over answers, over rotated arrays.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The input is sorted (or you can sort it cheaply), or has a monotonic property along some axis.
  • The problem asks "find / check if there exists an element" in O(log n) instead of O(n).
  • You see phrasing like "minimum X such that ..." or "maximum X such that ..." — that's binary search on the answer.
  • The array is sorted but rotated — search still collapses to O(log n) with a twist.
  • You're hunting a boundary (first/last occurrence, leftmost true in a FFFTTT predicate).

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Maintain two pointers lo and hi that bracket the candidate range. Each iteration computes mid = (lo + hi) // 2, evaluates a predicate at mid, and discards half the range. The predicate is what changes between variants — for classic search it's arr[mid] == target; for "search on answer" it's feasible(mid). The invariant is that the answer always lives in [lo, hi]. Pick your loop condition (lo < hi vs lo <= hi) and your halving (hi = mid vs hi = mid - 1) deliberately — off-by-one is the single biggest source of bugs in this family.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Classic binary search

Find a target in a sorted array — the canonical template.

02 First / last occurrence

Boundary search — the leftmost or rightmost index that satisfies a predicate.

03 Search in rotated sorted array

The array is sorted but rotated — one half is always sorted.

04 Binary search on answer

The answer itself is the search space — bracket it with a feasibility predicate.

05 Search 2D matrix (staircase walk)

Sorted matrix — start from a corner and walk monotonically.

06 Median of two sorted arrays

O(log min(n, m)) — the hardest classic binary search problem.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Inclusive vs exclusive bounds. lo <= hi with mid - 1 / mid + 1 updates is the safest default; lo < hi with hi = mid is cleaner for boundary searches but trickier.
  • Overflow on mid. In languages with fixed-width ints, use mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2 to avoid lo + hi overflow. Python is immune but the habit is good.
  • Predicate shape FFFTTT. When searching for a boundary, mentally label each index as F or T — you're hunting the first T (or last F).
  • Duplicates flip the math. With duplicates, you may need to advance lo past equal elements (e.g. rotated array II) which degrades the worst case to O(n).
  • Floating-point search. Loop until hi - lo < ε, not until they meet exactly.

docs.title: 01 — Classic Binary Search
docs.text: Binary Search • Position ⅙

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a sorted array arr and a target value, return the index of the target or -1 if it's not present.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Because the array is sorted, comparing the target to the middle element tells you which half can be safely discarded. Each step halves the search range, so after at most ⌈log₂ n⌉ probes you've either found the target or proven it isn't there. The invariant is simple: if target exists, it lives in [lo, hi].

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Initialize lo = 0, hi = n - 1.
  2. While lo <= hi:
  3. Compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2.
  4. If arr[mid] == target, return mid.
  5. If arr[mid] < target, move lo = mid + 1.
  6. Else move hi = mid - 1.
  7. If the loop exits, return -1.
def binary_search(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid
        if arr[mid] < target:
            lo = mid + 1
        else:
            hi = mid - 1
    return -1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On arr = [-1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12], target = 9:

  1. lo=0, hi=5 → mid=2, arr[2]=3 < 9 → discard left half, lo=3.
  2. lo=3, hi=5 → mid=4, arr[4]=9 == 9 → return 4.

Two probes against six elements — exactly ⌈log₂ 6⌉ = 3 upper bound.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(log n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using (lo + hi) // 2 in languages with fixed-width ints risks overflow — prefer lo + (hi - lo) // 2.
  • Mixing up lo <= hi with hi = mid causes infinite loops. Stick to one template.
  • Forgetting to return -1 when the loop exits — easy to fall off the function.
  • Assuming the array is sorted when the problem only says "given an array". Re-read the constraints.
  • Off-by-one on hi: it should be n - 1 for an inclusive upper bound, not n.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 704. Binary Search (Easy) — the literal template.
  • 35. Search Insert Position (Easy) — return `lo` instead of `-1`.
  • 374. Guess Number Higher or Lower (Easy) — predicate is the API itself.
  • 367. Valid Perfect Square (Easy) — search over `[1, n]` for `mid*mid == n`.
  • 69. Sqrt(x) (Easy) — same trick, return floor.
  • 278. First Bad Version (Easy) — leftmost true on a `FFFTTT` predicate.
  • 875. Koko Eating Bananas (Medium) — search on the eating speed.
  • 1011. Capacity To Ship Packages (Medium) — search on capacity.
  • 410. Split Array Largest Sum (Hard) — search on the largest subarray sum.
  • 2226. Maximum Candies Allocated (Medium) — search on candies per child.

docs.title: 02 — First / Last Occurrence
docs.text: Binary Search • Position 2/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a sorted array arr with possible duplicates and a target value, return the index of the first and last occurrence of the target. If the target is absent, return [-1, -1].

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Classic binary search returns some index that matches — but with duplicates we want a specific boundary. The trick is to bias the search: when arr[mid] == target, don't stop — keep narrowing toward the boundary. For the leftmost match, push hi = mid - 1 even on a hit; for the rightmost, push lo = mid + 1. The predicate is no longer "found / not found" — it's FFFTTT (or TTTFFF) and we're hunting the flip.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Run binary search twice — once biased left, once biased right.
  2. For the leftmost: when arr[mid] >= target, narrow hi = mid - 1; else lo = mid + 1. After the loop, check arr[lo] == target.
  3. For the rightmost: when arr[mid] <= target, narrow lo = mid + 1; else hi = mid - 1. After the loop, hi is the rightmost match.
def search_range(arr, target):
    def leftmost():
        lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
            if arr[mid] >= target:
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        return lo if lo < len(arr) and arr[lo] == target else -1

    def rightmost():
        lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
            if arr[mid] <= target:
                lo = mid + 1
            else:
                hi = mid - 1
        return hi if hi >= 0 and arr[hi] == target else -1

    return [leftmost(), rightmost()]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On arr = [5, 7, 7, 8, 8, 10], target = 8:

  1. Leftmost. lo=0, hi=5 → mid=2, arr[2]=7 < 8 → lo=3.
  2. lo=3, hi=5 → mid=4, arr[4]=8 >= 8 → hi=3.
  3. lo=3, hi=3 → mid=3, arr[3]=8 >= 8 → hi=2. Loop ends with lo=3. First = 3.
  4. Rightmost. Mirror image lands hi=4. Last = 4.

Result: [3, 4].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(log n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Don't return mid as soon as you find the target — that's the classic-search instinct and it gives you some occurrence, not the boundary you want.
  • After the loop, validate arr[lo] == target (or arr[hi]) — the index might land just past the run.
  • Bounds-check lo < len(arr) and hi >= 0 before indexing — the boundary can fall off the ends.
  • Don't mix the two templates in one function — keep the leftmost and rightmost searches structurally identical except for the >= / <= flip.
  • For "smallest element greater than target" (bisect_right), the predicate is arr[mid] > target, not >=.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 34. Find First and Last Position (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 35. Search Insert Position (Easy) — leftmost insert point.
  • 278. First Bad Version (Easy) — leftmost true on a `FFFTTT` predicate.
  • 875. Koko Eating Bananas (Medium) — leftmost feasible speed.
  • 540. Single Element in a Sorted Array (Medium) — parity-based predicate.
  • 658. Find K Closest Elements (Medium) — binary search on the left endpoint.
  • 744. Find Smallest Letter Greater Than Target (Easy) — `bisect_right` on chars.
  • 1539. Kth Missing Positive Number (Easy) — predicate on missing count.
  • 1283. Find the Smallest Divisor (Medium) — leftmost divisor that fits.
  • 162. Find Peak Element (Medium) — boundary search on slope direction.

docs.title: 03 — Search in Rotated Sorted Array
docs.text: Binary Search • Position 3/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A sorted array of distinct values was rotated at an unknown pivot, e.g. [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2]. Given a target, return its index or -1 — in O(log n).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The crucial observation: at any mid, at least one of the two halves [lo, mid] or [mid, hi] is still sorted.
