Compare arr[mid] with arr[lo] to figure out which half is sorted, then ask whether the target lies inside the sorted half (use plain range check) or outside it. Discard the wrong half. The invariant is preserved across iterations because the sorted-half property is local.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Initialize lo = 0, hi = n - 1.
  2. While lo <= hi:
  3. Compute mid. If arr[mid] == target, return mid.
  4. Left half sorted (arr[lo] <= arr[mid]): if arr[lo] <= target < arr[mid], go left; else go right.
  5. Right half sorted (else): if arr[mid] < target <= arr[hi], go right; else go left.
  6. Return -1.
def search_rotated(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid
        if arr[lo] <= arr[mid]:                  # left half sorted
            if arr[lo] <= target < arr[mid]:
                hi = mid - 1
            else:
                lo = mid + 1
        else:                                     # right half sorted
            if arr[mid] < target <= arr[hi]:
                lo = mid + 1
            else:
                hi = mid - 1
    return -1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On arr = [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], target = 0:

  1. lo=0, hi=6 → mid=3, arr[3]=7. Left half [4,5,6,7] is sorted (arr[0]=4 <= 7). Target 0 is not in [4, 7), so go right: lo=4.
  2. lo=4, hi=6 → mid=5, arr[5]=1. Left half [0, 1] is sorted (arr[4]=0 <= 1). Target 0 is in [0, 1), so go left: hi=4.
  3. lo=4, hi=4 → mid=4, arr[4]=0 == target. Return 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(log n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• The comparison arr[lo] <= arr[mid] must be <= (not <) to handle the case when lo == mid.
  • The range check inside the sorted half uses < on the open end and <= on the closed end — flipping either breaks the algorithm.
  • Duplicates change the worst case. If arr[lo] == arr[mid] == arr[hi], you can't tell which half is sorted — fall back to lo += 1 and the worst case becomes O(n) (LC 81).
  • Be careful with single-element and two-element arrays — trace them by hand.
  • For "find minimum in rotated sorted" (LC 153), the template flips: compare arr[mid] against arr[hi] to find the inflection point.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 33. Search in Rotated Sorted Array (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 81. Search in Rotated Sorted Array II (Medium) — with duplicates.
  • 153. Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array (Medium) — locate the pivot.
  • 154. Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II (Hard) — with duplicates.
  • 162. Find Peak Element (Medium) — local-max binary search.
  • 852. Peak Index in a Mountain Array (Medium) — easier peak finder.
  • 240. Search a 2D Matrix II (Medium) — staircase variant.
  • 74. Search a 2D Matrix (Medium) — treat as flattened sorted array.
  • 1095. Find in Mountain Array (Hard) — peak first, then two halves.
  • 4. Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Hard) — partition-based binary search.

docs.title: 04 — Binary Search on Answer
docs.text: Binary Search • Position 4/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The input isn't sorted — but the answer is a number in a known range [lo, hi], and there's a monotonic feasibility check: if mid works, every value above (or below) also works. Find the optimal value.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Frame the problem as a FFFTTT predicate. Example: "Can Koko finish all the bananas in H hours eating k per hour?" If k=10 works then k=11 certainly works — feasibility is monotonic in k. The optimal eating speed is the leftmost T. The actual problem (finishing bananas) becomes a black-box feasible(k) function that you call inside an otherwise-vanilla binary search over k. You never sort anything — you sort the answer space.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Determine the answer range [lo, hi] from the problem constraints.
  2. Write feasible(mid) — does this candidate satisfy the constraint?
  3. Binary search for the leftmost mid where feasible(mid) is true (or rightmost where it's false, depending on the variant).
def search_on_answer(lo, hi, feasible):
    # Leftmost mid such that feasible(mid) is True.
    while lo < hi:
        mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
        if feasible(mid):
            hi = mid
        else:
            lo = mid + 1
    return lo

# LC 875 — Koko Eating Bananas
def min_eating_speed(piles, h):
    def feasible(k):
        return sum((p + k - 1) // k for p in piles) <= h
    return search_on_answer(1, max(piles), feasible)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
LC 875 — piles = [3, 6, 7, 11], h = 8:

  1. Range: lo=1, hi=11. mid=6 → hours = 1+1+2+2 = 6 <= 8 → feasible. hi=6.
  2. lo=1, hi=6 → mid=3 → hours = 1+2+3+4 = 10 > 8 → not feasible. lo=4.
  3. lo=4, hi=6 → mid=5 → hours = 1+2+2+3 = 8 <= 8 → feasible. hi=5.
  4. lo=4, hi=5 → mid=4 → hours = 1+2+2+3 = 8 <= 8 → feasible. hi=4.
  5. Loop ends. Answer = 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(n · log R) space O(1)

R is the size of the answer range; n is the cost of one feasible() call.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting to prove monotonicity. If feasible isn't monotonic, binary search is invalid. State it explicitly.
  • Wrong bounds. Set lo to the smallest plausible answer and hi to the largest. Sloppy bounds either crash feasible or miss the answer.
  • Floor vs ceil arithmetic inside feasible. (p + k - 1) // k is "ceiling of p/k" — the common gotcha.
  • Maximize vs minimize. Decide whether you want the leftmost T (minimize feasible) or the rightmost T (maximize feasible) — they need different templates.
  • Cost of feasible can dominate. If it's O(n²) you've built an O(n² log R) solution; check before submitting.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 69. Sqrt(x) (Easy) — search on integer square root.
  • 367. Valid Perfect Square (Easy) — feasibility = `mid*mid >= n`.
  • 875. Koko Eating Bananas (Medium) — the canonical example.
  • 1011. Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days (Medium) — search on ship capacity.
  • 410. Split Array Largest Sum (Hard) — search on the largest subarray sum.
  • 1283. Find the Smallest Divisor Given a Threshold (Medium) — search on the divisor.
  • 1482. Minimum Days to Make Bouquets (Medium) — search on day count.
  • 2616. Minimize the Maximum Difference of Pairs (Medium) — search on the maximum gap.
  • 1631. Path With Minimum Effort (Medium) — feasibility = reachable under max-effort threshold.
  • 778. Swim in Rising Water (Hard) — search on water level.

docs.title: 05 — Search 2D Matrix (Staircase Walk)
docs.text: Binary Search • Position ⅚

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an m × n matrix where each row is sorted left-to-right and each column is sorted top-to-bottom, return whether a target exists in the matrix.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Start at a corner where one direction increases and the other decreases — the top-right ((0, n-1)) is the canonical choice. From there: if the current cell is bigger than target, you can drop the entire column (move left); if it's smaller, you can drop the entire row (move down). Each step eliminates an entire row or column, so the walk visits at most m + n cells. It looks like a staircase descending from the top-right corner.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Start at (r, c) = (0, n - 1).
  2. While r < m and c >= 0:
  3. If matrix[r][c] == target, return true.
  4. If matrix[r][c] > target, move left (c -= 1).
  5. Else move down (r += 1).
  6. Return false.
def search_matrix(matrix, target):
    if not matrix or not matrix[0]:
        return False
    m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0])
    r, c = 0, n - 1
    while r < m and c >= 0:
        if matrix[r][c] == target:
            return True
        if matrix[r][c] > target:
            c -= 1
        else:
            r += 1
    return False

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
On a 5×5 matrix with target = 5:

[ 1,  4,  7, 11, 15]
[ 2,  5,  8, 12, 19]
[ 3,  6,  9, 16, 22]
[10, 13, 14, 17, 24]
[18, 21, 23, 26, 30]
  1. Start at (0, 4) = 15. 15 > 5 → left, (0, 3) = 11.
  2. 11 > 5 → left, (0, 2) = 7. 7 > 5 → left, (0, 1) = 4.
  3. 4 < 5 → down, (1, 1) = 5. Match — return true.

Four steps on a 25-cell matrix.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(m + n) space O(1)

For the stricter LC 74 variant (every row starts after the previous row's end), a true O(log(m·n)) binary search is possible by treating the matrix as a flat sorted array.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Starting at (0, 0) or (m-1, n-1) doesn't work — both directions either increase or decrease, so you can't eliminate.
  • The bottom-left corner (m-1, 0) is equally valid (swap directions) — but pick one and stick with it.
  • For LC 74 (strictly increasing across row boundaries), staircase is correct but suboptimal — use flattened binary search for O(log(m·n)).
  • Don't confuse this with "kth smallest in sorted matrix" (LC 378) — that's binary search on the answer with count_le(mid) as the predicate.
  • Bounds-check inside the loop — r < m and c >= 0, not r <= m.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 74. Search a 2D Matrix (Medium) — flattened binary search variant.
  • 240. Search a 2D Matrix II (Medium) — the canonical staircase problem.
  • 378. Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix (Medium) — search on answer with staircase counter.
  • 668. Kth Smallest Number in Multiplication Table (Hard) — same idea, virtual matrix.
  • 4. Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Hard) — partition-based binary search.
  • 719. Find K-th Smallest Pair Distance (Hard) — search on the gap.
  • 1351. Count Negative Numbers in a Sorted Matrix (Easy) — staircase counting.
  • 1428. Leftmost Column With at Least a One (Medium) — interactive matrix staircase.
  • 162. Find Peak Element (Medium) — 1D peak finder, prereq for 2D.
  • 1901. Find a Peak Element II (Medium) — 2D binary search on the column with max.

docs.title: 06 — Median of Two Sorted Arrays
docs.text: Binary Search • Position 6/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given two sorted arrays A and B of sizes m and n, find the median of the combined sorted array in O(log min(m, n)).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The median splits the combined array of m + n elements into two halves of equal size where every element in the left half is <= every element in the right half. We don't actually merge — we partition. Pick a cut i in A (0..m); the matching cut in B is j = (m + n + 1) // 2 - i. The cut is valid when A[i-1] <= B[j] and B[j-1] <= A[i]. Binary-search i on the smaller array. When the cut is valid, the median is max(A[i-1], B[j-1]) (odd total) or its average with min(A[i], B[j]) (even total).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Ensure A is the smaller array — swap if not.
  2. Binary search i in [0, m]. Let j = (m + n + 1) // 2 - i.
  3. Use sentinels -∞ and +∞ for out-of-bound indices.
  4. If A[i-1] > B[j], the cut in A is too far right — move left (hi = i - 1).
  5. If B[j-1] > A[i], the cut in A is too far left — move right (lo = i + 1).
  6. Otherwise the cut is valid — compute the median from the four boundary values.
def find_median(A, B):
    if len(A) > len(B):
        A, B = B, A
    m, n = len(A), len(B)
    half = (m + n + 1) // 2
    lo, hi = 0, m
    while lo <= hi:
        i = (lo + hi) // 2
        j = half - i
        a_left  = A[i - 1] if i > 0 else float('-inf')
        a_right = A[i]     if i < m else float('inf')
        b_left  = B[j - 1] if j > 0 else float('-inf')
        b_right = B[j]     if j < n else float('inf')
        if a_left <= b_right and b_left <= a_right:
            if (m + n) % 2:
                return max(a_left, b_left)
            return (max(a_left, b_left) + min(a_right, b_right)) / 2
        if a_left > b_right:
            hi = i - 1
        else:
            lo = i + 1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
A = [1, 3], B = [2, 4, 5]. m=2, n=3, total = 5 (odd), half = 3.

  1. lo=0, hi=2 → i=1, j=2. a_left=A[0]=1, a_right=A[1]=3, b_left=B[1]=4, b_right=B[2]=5. Check: 1 <= 5 yes, 4 <= 3 no. b_left > a_right → lo = 2.
  2. lo=2, hi=2 → i=2, j=1. a_left=A[1]=3, a_right=+∞, b_left=B[0]=2, b_right=B[1]=4. Check: 3 <= 4 yes, 2 <= +∞ yes. Valid cut.
  3. Odd total → median = max(a_left, b_left) = max(3, 2) = 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(log min(m, n)) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Always binary-search the smaller array — otherwise j can go negative or beyond n.
  • The sentinel trick (±∞) is what makes the boundary cases (cut at 0 or at the end) collapse into the same code. Don't try to special-case them.
  • half = (m + n + 1) // 2 is correct for both odd and even totals — derive it on paper before trusting it.
  • Off-by-one between i (size of left part of A) and i-1 (last index of left part) is the most common bug — use clear variable names.
  • For the "kth element" generalization, the same partition trick works with k instead of half — but the median problem is the cleanest case.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 4. Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Hard) — the canonical problem.
  • 88. Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — warm-up for two-array sorted merging.
  • 295. Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — heap-based median, complementary skill.
  • 1093. Statistics from a Large Sample (Medium) — median via cumulative frequency.
  • 786. K-th Smallest Prime Fraction (Medium) — binary search on fraction value.
  • 1918. Kth Smallest Subarray Sum (Medium) — search on answer with sliding-window counter.
  • 1755. Closest Subsequence Sum (Hard) — meet-in-the-middle plus binary search.
  • 378. Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix (Medium) — kth-smallest via predicate.
  • 668. Kth Smallest Number in Multiplication Table (Hard) — virtual sorted matrix.
  • 719. Find K-th Smallest Pair Distance (Hard) — binary search on the gap.

docs.title: DSA · 04 · BFS
docs.text: Layered exploration — shortest path in unweighted graphs and grids.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem asks for shortest path / fewest steps between two nodes in an unweighted graph.
  • You need to process nodes level by level — e.g. tree-by-depth, all cells at distance k.
  • The state space is a graph or grid maze, and you can model moves as edges.
  • You have multiple sources expanding simultaneously (rotting oranges, walls-and-gates).
  • The "edges" are abstract — string transformations (Word Ladder), lock combinations, sliding puzzles.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Push the start node(s) into a queue, mark them visited, then repeatedly pop and expand neighbours. Each level of the queue corresponds to one extra step of distance from the source(s). The visited set (or distance map) prevents re-expanding nodes and guarantees O(V + E) total work. The key invariant: the first time BFS reaches a node, it does so via a shortest path — that's why BFS beats DFS for unweighted shortest path. For grids, neighbours are the 4 (or 8) adjacent cells; for graphs, they're adjacency-list entries; for abstract puzzles, they're whatever moves the problem defines.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 BFS on graph

Queue + visited set on a generic adjacency list.

02 BFS on grid

4-directional moves with bounds and obstacle checks.

03 Tree level-order traversal

Process nodes one level at a time — the bedrock tree BFS.

04 Multi-source BFS

Seed the queue with all sources at once — distances stay correct.

05 Word Ladder

BFS on a string-transformation graph — the canonical abstract BFS.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Bidirectional BFS — expand from source and target simultaneously, stop when they meet. Cuts search space from b^d to 2·b^(d/2).
  • 0-1 BFS — for graphs with edge weights 0 and 1, use a deque (push-front for 0, push-back for 1) instead of a heap. O(V + E).
  • Multi-source seed — instead of running BFS from each source independently, seed them all into the queue at distance 0.
  • Track parents — to reconstruct the shortest path itself, store parent[node] and walk back from the target.
  • Distance vs visited — use a distance map when you need the actual distance; a visited set when you only need reachability.

docs.title: 01 — BFS on Graph
docs.text: BFS • Position ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an undirected (or directed) graph and a start node s, compute the shortest distance from s to every reachable node — where every edge has weight 1.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: BFS explores nodes in non-decreasing order of distance from the source. The first time you dequeue a node, you've arrived via a shortest path — and you never need to re-enqueue it. This is why a visited set (or a distance map initialized to -1/∞) is enough; you don't need a priority queue like Dijkstra. The queue itself encodes the frontier of "currently expanding" nodes.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Initialize dist[s] = 0 and a queue containing s. All other nodes have dist = ∞.
  2. While the queue is non-empty:
  3. Pop u.
  4. For each neighbour v of u: if dist[v] == ∞, set dist[v] = dist[u] + 1 and enqueue v.
  5. Return dist.
from collections import deque

def bfs(graph, start):
    dist = {start: 0}
    q = deque([start])
    while q:
        u = q.popleft()
        for v in graph[u]:
            if v not in dist:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + 1
                q.append(v)
    return dist

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Graph: 0 — 1 — 2, 0 — 3, 3 — 4. Start at 0:

  1. Init: dist = {0: 0}, q = [0].
  2. Pop 0. Visit 1, 3. dist = {0:0, 1:1, 3:1}, q = [1, 3].
  3. Pop 1. Visit 2 (skip 0). dist = {0:0, 1:1, 3:1, 2:2}, q = [3, 2].
  4. Pop 3. Visit 4 (skip 0). dist = {..., 4:2}, q = [2, 4].
  5. Pop 2, 4. No new neighbours. Done.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V + E) space O(V)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Marking visited on pop, not push. This lets a node enter the queue multiple times, blowing up time complexity. Always mark when you enqueue.
  • Using a list as the queue in Python — list.pop(0) is O(n). Always collections.deque.
  • For weighted graphs with non-unit weights, BFS gives wrong answers — switch to Dijkstra.
  • Don't confuse "reachable" with "shortest path" — BFS gives both, but the predicate for early termination differs.
  • For bidirectional BFS, you must check intersection between the frontiers at each level — checking only the visited sets misses the meet.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1971. Find if Path Exists in Graph (Easy) — pure reachability.
  • 994. Rotting Oranges (Medium) — multi-source BFS on grid.
  • 286. Walls and Gates (Medium) — multi-source BFS from every gate.
  • 102. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (Medium) — BFS on trees.
  • 127. Word Ladder (Hard) — abstract BFS on string transformations.
  • 200. Number of Islands (Medium) — BFS or DFS to count components.
  • 752. Open the Lock (Medium) — BFS on 4-digit lock state space.
  • 542. 01 Matrix (Medium) — multi-source BFS from every 0.
  • 1162. As Far from Land as Possible (Medium) — multi-source BFS, take max.
  • 433. Minimum Genetic Mutation (Medium) — Word Ladder twin.

docs.title: 02 — BFS on Grid
docs.text: BFS • Position ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an m × n grid with open cells and obstacles, find the shortest path (in cell hops) from a start cell to a target cell — or compute distances to every reachable cell.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A grid is just a graph in disguise: each cell is a node, and each cell has up to 4 (or 8) neighbours. Run BFS exactly as on a graph — the only difference is generating neighbours on the fly via direction vectors, and pruning out-of-bounds or obstacle cells. Marking visited as soon as you enqueue keeps the queue size bounded by O(m·n) and ensures O(m·n) total work.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Define DIRS = [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)] for 4-connectivity (or include diagonals for 8).
  2. Push the start cell into the queue, mark it visited.
  3. While the queue is non-empty:
  4. Pop (r, c). If it's the target, return its distance.
  5. For each (dr, dc) in DIRS, compute (nr, nc). If it's in bounds, not an obstacle, and not visited, enqueue it and mark visited.
from collections import deque

DIRS = [(-1, 0), (1, 0), (0, -1), (0, 1)]

def shortest_path(grid, start, target):
    m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])
    q = deque([(*start, 0)])
    seen = {start}
    while q:
        r, c, d = q.popleft()
        if (r, c) == target:
            return d
        for dr, dc in DIRS:
            nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
            if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n and grid[nr][nc] != '#' and (nr, nc) not in seen:
                seen.add((nr, nc))
                q.append((nr, nc, d + 1))
    return -1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
3×3 grid, . open, # wall, start (0,0), target (2,2):

. . .
. # .
. . .
  1. Enqueue (0,0,0). Pop → expand to (0,1) and (1,0), both at dist 1.
  2. Pop (0,1,1) → expand to (0,2) at dist 2. (1,1) is a wall, skip.
  3. Pop (1,0,1) → expand to (2,0) at dist 2.
  4. Pop (0,2,2) → expand to (1,2) at dist 3.
  5. Pop (2,0,2) → expand to (2,1) at dist 3.
  6. Pop (1,2,3) → expand to (2,2) at dist 4. Target reached — return 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(m · n) space O(m · n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Bounds-check before indexing — 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n. Python lists with negative indices silently wrap around and corrupt results.
  • Mark visited on enqueue, not dequeue — otherwise the same cell ends up in the queue multiple times.
  • For 8-connectivity, double-check whether the problem actually allows diagonal moves — many do not.
  • Multi-source BFS is just BFS with all sources seeded at distance 0 — don't write a loop running BFS once per source (that's O(s · m · n)).
  • For weighted grid moves (e.g. some cells cost 5, some cost 1), BFS is wrong — use Dijkstra or 0-1 BFS depending on weight values.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 200. Number of Islands (Medium) — flood-fill components.
  • 994. Rotting Oranges (Medium) — multi-source BFS with time.
  • 1091. Shortest Path in Binary Matrix (Medium) — 8-directional BFS.
  • 542. 01 Matrix (Medium) — multi-source from every 0.
  • 286. Walls and Gates (Medium) — multi-source from every gate.
  • 130. Surrounded Regions (Medium) — BFS from border to mark safe cells.
  • 695. Max Area of Island (Medium) — flood-fill with size tracking.
  • 1162. As Far from Land as Possible (Medium) — multi-source BFS for max distance.
  • 934. Shortest Bridge (Medium) — DFS to find first island, BFS to expand.
  • 815. Bus Routes (Hard) — BFS on bus-route-as-node abstraction.

docs.title: 03 — Tree Level-Order Traversal
docs.text: BFS • Position ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given the root of a binary tree, return a list of lists where the i-th list contains all node values at depth i.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A tree is a graph with no cycles, so BFS doesn't even need a visited set — each node has exactly one path from the root. The standard trick to separate levels is to snapshot the queue size at the start of each iteration and dequeue exactly that many nodes — those are the current level. Append them to a sub-list, then push their children. After the inner loop completes, the sub-list is a full level.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. If root is null, return [].
  2. Initialize q = [root] and result = [].
  3. While q is non-empty:
  4. Snapshot level_size = len(q).
  5. For i in range(level_size): pop a node, append its value to the current level, push its non-null children.
  6. Append the current level to result.
from collections import deque

def level_order(root):
    if not root:
        return []
    result = []
    q = deque([root])
    while q:
        level = []
        for _ in range(len(q)):
            node = q.popleft()
            level.append(node.val)
            if node.left:  q.append(node.left)
            if node.right: q.append(node.right)
        result.append(level)
    return result

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Tree:

      3
     / \
    9  20
       / \
      15  7
  1. Init q = [3], result = [].
  2. Level 0. Size 1. Pop 3, append children [9, 20]. result = [[3]].
  3. Level 1. Size 2. Pop 9 (no children). Pop 20, append children [15, 7]. result = [[3], [9, 20]].
  4. Level 2. Size 2. Pop 15 (no children). Pop 7 (no children). result = [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]].
  5. Queue empty — done.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(n) space O(w)

w is the maximum tree width (up to n/2 for a complete tree).

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Snapshot len(q) before the inner loop — if you use for _ in q, Python iterates as you mutate. Save the size first.
  • Forgetting to check null children — if node.left and if node.right are mandatory.
  • For N-ary trees, iterate over node.children instead of left/right.
  • Reverse level order (LC 107) — collect levels then result[::-1], or use collections.deque.appendleft.
  • Right-side view (LC 199) — same template, but append only the last element of each level.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 102. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 107. Level Order II (Medium) — same, reversed.
  • 199. Right Side View (Medium) — last node per level.
  • 515. Largest Value in Each Tree Row (Medium) — max per level.
  • 637. Average of Levels (Easy) — mean per level.
  • 116. Populating Next Right Pointers (Medium) — link nodes within a level.
  • 117. Populating Next Right Pointers II (Medium) — non-perfect tree variant.
  • 314. Vertical Order Traversal (Medium) — BFS with column index.
  • 987. Vertical Order Traversal II (Hard) — same, tie-break by value.
  • 993. Cousins in Binary Tree (Easy) — BFS with parent tracking.

docs.title: 04 — Multi-Source BFS — Rotting Oranges
docs.text: BFS • Position ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: LC 994: an m × n grid contains empty cells (0), fresh oranges (1), and rotten oranges (2). Every minute, each rotten orange rots its 4-directional fresh neighbours. Return the minimum minutes until no fresh oranges remain, or -1 if impossible.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Running BFS from each rotten orange independently and merging distances is wasteful. Instead, seed every source into the queue at distance 0 and let them expand in parallel. Because BFS only updates a cell the first time it's reached, every fresh orange gets stamped with the distance to its nearest rotten source — exactly what we want. The answer is the maximum distance assigned, since all rotten oranges spread simultaneously.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Scan the grid. Enqueue every rotten orange as (r, c, 0). Count fresh oranges.
  2. Run BFS. For each pop, expand to 4-directional fresh neighbours; flip them rotten, decrement fresh count, enqueue with t + 1. Track the max t seen.
  3. After BFS, return max_t if fresh == 0, else -1.
from collections import deque

def oranges_rotting(grid):
    m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])
    q = deque()
    fresh = 0
    for r in range(m):
        for c in range(n):
            if grid[r][c] == 2:
                q.append((r, c, 0))
            elif grid[r][c] == 1:
                fresh += 1

    max_t = 0
    while q:
        r, c, t = q.popleft()
        max_t = max(max_t, t)
        for dr, dc in [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]:
            nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
            if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n and grid[nr][nc] == 1:
                grid[nr][nc] = 2
                fresh -= 1
                q.append((nr, nc, t + 1))

    return max_t if fresh == 0 else -1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Grid (R rotten, F fresh, . empty):

R F F
F F .
. F F
  1. Init. Enqueue (0,0,0). fresh = 6.
  2. t=0. Pop (0,0,0). Rot (0,1) and (1,0) at t=1. fresh=4.
  3. t=1. Pop (0,1,1) → rot (0,2) at t=2. Pop (1,0,1) → rot (1,1) at t=2. fresh=2.
  4. t=2. Pop (0,2,2) (no fresh neighbour). Pop (1,1,2) → rot (2,1) at t=3. fresh=1.
  5. t=3. Pop (2,1,3) → rot (2,2) at t=4. fresh=0.
  6. t=4. Pop (2,2,4). No fresh neighbour. Queue empty.

Answer: max_t = 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(m · n) space O(m · n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Don't run BFS once per source. That's O(s · m · n) — multi-source BFS is O(m · n) regardless of source count.
  • Count fresh oranges before BFS starts so you can detect "unreachable fresh" cases cleanly.
  • Mutating the grid in place is fine here (it's the "visited" mark), but document it or use a separate visited set if the grid must be preserved.
  • If the queue is empty at start because there are no rotten oranges, return 0 if fresh == 0, else -1. Edge case.
  • For "all fresh must be reached" (this problem) the answer is the max distance — not the queue size or the number of pops.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 994. Rotting Oranges (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 542. 01 Matrix (Medium) — multi-source from every 0.
  • 286. Walls and Gates (Medium) — multi-source from every gate.
  • 1162. As Far from Land as Possible (Medium) — multi-source, take max distance.
  • 1765. Map of Highest Peak (Medium) — multi-source from every water cell.
  • 934. Shortest Bridge (Medium) — DFS to seed one island, BFS to expand.
  • 815. Bus Routes (Hard) — multi-source on bus-route graph.
  • 417. Pacific Atlantic Water Flow (Medium) — two multi-source BFS / DFS passes.
  • 1129. Shortest Path with Alternating Colors (Medium) — BFS with edge-color state.
  • 854. K-Similar Strings (Hard) — BFS on string state.

docs.title: 05 — Word Ladder
docs.text: BFS • Position 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: LC 127: given beginWord, endWord, and a wordList, return the length of the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord where each step changes exactly one letter and every intermediate word must be in wordList. Return 0 if no transformation exists.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Model each word as a graph node and connect two words with an edge if they differ by exactly one letter. The answer is the shortest path from beginWord to endWord in that graph — pure BFS. The trick that makes it efficient: don't build the full adjacency list (that's O(N² · L)). Instead, generate neighbours on the fly by trying every single-letter swap and checking against a hash set of valid words. Or pre-index by "patterns" like h*t, *ot, ho* so each word has 26·L → L lookups.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
1. Put wordList in a hash set S. If endWord not in S, return 0.
  2. BFS from beginWord with distance 1.
  3. For each popped word, generate every single-letter variant. If a variant is in S and unvisited, enqueue with dist + 1 and remove it from S (visited marker).
  4. If the variant equals endWord, return dist + 1. If the queue drains, return 0.
from collections import deque

def ladder_length(begin, end, word_list):
    S = set(word_list)
    if end not in S:
        return 0
    q = deque([(begin, 1)])
    while q:
        word, d = q.popleft()
        if word == end:
            return d
        for i in range(len(word)):
            for ch in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz':
                if ch == word[i]:
                    continue
                nxt = word[:i] + ch + word[i+1:]
                if nxt in S:
                    S.remove(nxt)         # visited marker
                    q.append((nxt, d + 1))
    return 0

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
begin = "hit", end = "cog", wordList = ["hot","dot","dog","lot","log","cog"]:

  1. Init S = {hot, dot, dog, lot, log, cog}. q = [("hit", 1)].
  2. Pop ("hit", 1). Try variants: *it, h*t, hi*. Only hot is in S. Enqueue ("hot", 2), remove from S.
  3. Pop ("hot", 2). Variants find dot and lot. Enqueue both at dist 3.
  4. Pop ("dot", 3) → finds dog, enqueue at dist 4.
  5. Pop ("lot", 3) → finds log, enqueue at dist 4.
  6. Pop ("dog", 4) → finds cog, enqueue at dist 5.
  7. Pop ("log", 4) → cog already removed, skip.
  8. Pop ("cog", 5) — matches end. Return 5.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(N · L · 26) space O(N · L)

N is the word-list size, L is the word length. Pattern indexing reduces to O(N · L²) but with a much smaller constant.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Removing from the set as visited marker is fine for LC 127, but if you need to enumerate all shortest paths (LC 126), you must defer removal until the level is complete — otherwise you cut off parallel paths.
  • Always check endWord in S upfront — if it's not in the list, the answer is immediately 0.
  • The branching factor is 26 · L, not just L. State this in interviews when estimating cost.
  • Bidirectional BFS halves the effective depth: expand from begin and end simultaneously, swap to whichever frontier is smaller each step.
  • For very long words, pattern indexing (h*t → [hot, hit, ...]) wins decisively over the brute character swap.
Practice — LeetCode

  • 126. Word Ladder II (Hard) — enumerate all shortest paths.
  • 752. Open the Lock (Medium) — BFS on lock-combination graph.
  • 433. Minimum Genetic Mutation (Medium) — Word Ladder twin on DNA.
  • 854. K-Similar Strings (Hard) — BFS on string swaps.
  • 815. Bus Routes (Hard) — BFS on bus-route-as-node abstraction.
  • 773. Sliding Puzzle (Hard) — BFS on 3×2 puzzle state.
  • 909. Snakes and Ladders (Medium) — BFS on board state.
  • 752. Open the Lock (Medium) — bidirectional BFS variant.
  • 1345. Jump Game IV (Hard) — BFS with equal-value group teleports.

docs.title: DSA · 05 · DFS
docs.text: Go deep first — flood fill, cycle detection, tree traversals.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• You need to explore every node of a connected region (islands, flood fill, enclaves).
  • You want any path from A to B, not the shortest one (BFS owns shortest-in-unweighted).
  • You're working on a tree — preorder, inorder, postorder, path sums, diameter.
  • You need to detect a cycle in a directed or undirected graph.
  • The recursion depth fits the stack (or you've rewritten it iteratively for huge inputs).

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: DFS is a single recursive function — dfs(node) — that marks the node visited, then calls itself on each unvisited neighbour. The recursion stack is the path you're currently exploring; when the function returns, you've finished one branch and the caller continues with the next. For graphs you carry a visited set; for trees you don't need one because there are no back-edges. The two superpowers are returning a value from below (subtree sum, height, path-existence) and mutating state on the way down and undoing it on the way up (backtracking, three-color cycle detection).

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 DFS on graph

The skeleton: visited set, recursive neighbour walk.

02 Number of Islands

Flood fill — the canonical 2D DFS.

03 Tree traversals

Preorder, inorder, postorder — when to do the work.

04 Path Sum

Root-to-leaf accumulation with backtracking.

05 Cycle detection

Three-color marking for directed graphs.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Iterative DFS with an explicit stack mirrors the recursive version one-to-one and avoids stack overflow on deep graphs. For undirected graphs cycle detection only needs a visited set plus the parent pointer; for directed graphs you need the three-color trick (white = unseen, gray = on current path, black = finished) because revisiting a black node is fine but hitting a gray one is a back-edge. Tree problems often blend DFS with postorder accumulation — compute children first, then combine — which is how diameter, balanced-check, and max-path-sum all work in one pass.

docs.title: 01 — DFS on graph
docs.text: DFS • Position ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Visit every node reachable from a starting vertex, exploring as deep as possible before backtracking.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: DFS commits to one neighbour and follows it all the way down before trying the next. The invariant is that every node on the current recursion stack is on the path from the source to the deepest frontier — when the call returns, that node is fully explored. A visited set prevents re-entry, which turns the search graph into a DFS tree.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Mark the start visited, recurse into each unvisited neighbour. The recursion stack itself records the current path; you can hook work into the pre position (before recursing) or the post position (after all children return) depending on what you want to compute.

def dfs(node, graph, visited):
    visited.add(node)
    # pre-order work goes here
    for nei in graph[node]:
        if nei not in visited:
            dfs(nei, graph, visited)
    # post-order work goes here

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Graph: 0 — 1 — 3 and 0 — 2. Start at 0.
  2. Mark 0 visited. Pick neighbour 1, recurse.
  3. Mark 1 visited. Recurse into 3. 3 has no new neighbours — return.
  4. Back at 1 — no more neighbours, return to 0.
  5. From 0, recurse into 2. Mark 2, return. Done.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V + E) space O(V) recursion + visited

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting to mark the node visited before recursing — leads to infinite loops on cycles.
  • Stack overflow on long chains (10⁵+ depth) — convert to iterative with an explicit stack.
  • On undirected graphs, treating a back-edge to the parent as a cycle — pass parent or use edge IDs.
  • Mutating shared state in pre-order without undoing it in post-order when you need backtracking.
  • Building graph as a list of edges and re-scanning it each call instead of an adjacency map.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 200. Number of Islands (Medium) — 2D DFS, the canonical flood fill.
  • 547. Number of Provinces (Medium) — count connected components via DFS.
  • 695. Max Area of Island (Medium) — return a value from each DFS call.
  • 207. Course Schedule (Medium) — cycle detection in a directed graph.
  • 210. Course Schedule II (Medium) — topological order via post-order DFS.
  • 130. Surrounded Regions (Medium) — DFS from borders inward.
  • 417. Pacific Atlantic Water Flow (Medium) — two DFS passes from each ocean.
  • 1376. Time Needed to Inform All Employees (Medium) — DFS on a tree, take max.
  • 841. Keys and Rooms (Medium) — reachability from room 0.
  • 1971. Find if Path Exists in Graph (Easy) — warm-up DFS reachability.

docs.title: 02 — Number of Islands
docs.text: DFS • Position ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a 2D grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water), count the number of islands. Cells connect 4-directionally.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Walk the grid. Every time you find a '1', you've found a brand-new island — increment the count, then sink the whole island by DFS-flooding every connected '1' to '0' (or marking it visited). The invariant is that once a cell has been touched by the flood, it will never start a new island again — so the total cell-visits sum to O(m·n).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Two nested loops scan for unflooded land. Each find triggers a 4-directional DFS that overwrites land with water as it goes.

def numIslands(grid):
    if not grid: return 0
    m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])

    def dfs(r, c):
        if r < 0 or r >= m or c < 0 or c >= n: return
        if grid[r][c] != '1': return
        grid[r][c] = '0'                  # sink
        dfs(r+1, c); dfs(r-1, c)
        dfs(r, c+1); dfs(r, c-1)

    count = 0
    for r in range(m):
        for c in range(n):
            if grid[r][c] == '1':
                count += 1
                dfs(r, c)
    return count

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Grid: two L-shaped land masses separated by water. Scanner starts at (0,0).
  2. Hit '1' at (0,0): count = 1, DFS floods the entire first island — every connected cell becomes '0'.
  3. Scanner continues, skipping the now-watered cells.
  4. Hit '1' at (2,3): count = 2, DFS floods the second island.
  5. No more '1's. Return 2.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(m·n) space O(m·n) recursion worst case

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Comparing to integer 1 when the grid holds character '1' — silent bug, count stays 0.
  • Forgetting to bound-check before reading grid[r][c] — IndexError on the edges.
  • Mutating the input grid when the caller needs it pristine — use a visited set instead.
  • Including diagonal moves when the problem says 4-directional (or vice versa).
  • Recursion depth on a 1000×1000 all-land grid — switch to BFS or iterative stack.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 200. Number of Islands (Medium) — the original.
  • 695. Max Area of Island (Medium) — return area from DFS.
  • 463. Island Perimeter (Easy) — count exposed edges.
  • 1254. Number of Closed Islands (Medium) — exclude border-touching islands.
  • 1020. Number of Enclaves (Medium) — flood from borders, count interior land.
  • 305. Number of Islands II (Hard) — online with union-find.
  • 694. Number of Distinct Islands (Medium) — canonical shape via DFS path.
  • 130. Surrounded Regions (Medium) — DFS from borders to mark safe.
  • 1905. Count Sub Islands (Medium) — two-grid DFS with AND condition.
  • 827. Making A Large Island (Hard) — DFS to size each island, then try flips.

docs.title: 03 — Tree traversals (pre / in / post)
docs.text: DFS • Position ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Visit every node of a binary tree exactly once, in one of three canonical orders.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The three orders differ only in when you "visit" the node relative to its children. Preorder visits before recursing — you see a node before its subtree, perfect for serialization. Inorder visits between left and right — on a BST it yields sorted output. Postorder visits after both children return — perfect for any "combine the answers from below" computation (heights, sums, diameters).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Three nearly identical functions; the only thing that moves is the visit(node) line.

def preorder(node, out):
    if not node: return
    out.append(node.val)         # visit
    preorder(node.left, out)
    preorder(node.right, out)

def inorder(node, out):
    if not node: return
    inorder(node.left, out)
    out.append(node.val)         # visit
    inorder(node.right, out)

def postorder(node, out):
    if not node: return
    postorder(node.left, out)
    postorder(node.right, out)
    out.append(node.val)         # visit

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Tree: root 1, left 2, right 3, with 4 and 5 as children of 2.
  2. Preorder (root, left, right): visit 1 → recurse left → visit 2 → visit 4 → visit 5 → recurse right → visit 3. Output: 1, 2, 4, 5, 3.
  3. Inorder: left subtree first → 4, 2, 5, then root 1, then right 3. Output: 4, 2, 5, 1, 3.
  4. Postorder: children before parent → 4, 5, 2, 3, 1.
  5. The visualization animates the preorder walk; the other two are the same recursion with the visit-line moved.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(h) stack, h = tree height

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Confusing preorder with BFS — preorder is depth-first, BFS is level-by-level.
  • Assuming inorder gives sorted output on any tree — only on a BST.
  • Hitting recursion limits on a skewed tree of 10⁵ nodes — Python default stack is 1000.
  • Mutating the output list in place when the recursion expects a fresh list per call.
  • Writing an iterative version with one stack but forgetting that postorder needs a "visited" marker or two stacks.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 144. Binary Tree Preorder Traversal (Easy) — recursive and iterative.
  • 94. Binary Tree Inorder Traversal (Easy) — Morris traversal for O(1) space.
  • 145. Binary Tree Postorder Traversal (Easy) — trickiest iteratively.
  • 102. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (Medium) — BFS variant for contrast.
  • 589. N-ary Tree Preorder Traversal (Easy) — same idea, more children.
  • 590. N-ary Tree Postorder Traversal (Easy) — combine after all kids.
  • 100. Same Tree (Easy) — preorder structural compare.
  • 101. Symmetric Tree (Easy) — mirrored DFS.
  • 226. Invert Binary Tree (Easy) — swap children, recurse.
  • 543. Diameter of Binary Tree (Easy) — postorder height + global max.

docs.title: 04 — Path Sum (root-to-leaf)
docs.text: DFS • Position ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a binary tree and a target sum, return True if some root-to-leaf path's values add up to the target.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: DFS the tree, carrying the remaining target down. When you reach a leaf, the path matches iff the leaf's value equals what's left of the target. The invariant is that at any call, remaining equals target − sum(values on the current recursion stack above this node). No global state, no backtracking bookkeeping — just a parameter that shrinks.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
At each node, check if it's a leaf and matches; otherwise recurse with the reduced target into whichever children exist.

def hasPathSum(root, target):
    if not root: return False
    if not root.left and not root.right:
        return root.val == target
    remaining = target - root.val
    return (hasPathSum(root.left, remaining)
            or hasPathSum(root.right, remaining))

For "collect all paths" variants, pass a path list and append on the way down, pop on the way up — classic backtracking.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Tree: 5 → (4, 8), 4 → (11, _), 11 → (7, 2). Target 22.
  2. At 5: remaining = 22 − 5 = 17. Recurse left into 4.
  3. At 4: remaining = 13. Recurse left into 11.
  4. At 11: remaining = 2. Recurse left into 7 (leaf, val 7 ≠ 2 — fail). Backtrack, try right 2.
  5. At leaf 2: val 2 == remaining 2 — return True all the way up.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(h) stack, h = tree height

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Treating a node with one child as a leaf — only nodes with both children null are leaves.
  • Returning True at the root when the cumulative sum reaches target but you're not yet at a leaf.
  • For "Path Sum III" (any node to any descendant), forgetting prefix-sum + hashmap trick — naive O(n²) often TLEs.
  • Mutating a shared path list and forgetting the pop after recursion — paths bleed into siblings.
  • Integer overflow when sums get large (not Python, but bites in Java/C++).
Practice — LeetCode
  • 112. Path Sum (Easy) — the canonical version.
  • 113. Path Sum II (Medium) — return all matching paths.
  • 437. Path Sum III (Medium) — any-to-any with prefix-sum hashmap.
  • 124. Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (Hard) — postorder, return one-side max.
  • 129. Sum Root to Leaf Numbers (Medium) — carry digits as you descend.
  • 257. Binary Tree Paths (Easy) — all root-to-leaf strings.
  • 666. Path Sum IV (Medium) — implicit tree encoded in integers.
  • 988. Smallest String Starting From Leaf (Medium) — DFS + lexicographic compare.
  • 1457. Pseudo-Palindromic Paths in a Binary Tree (Medium) — DFS with bitmask parity.
  • 1376. Time Needed to Inform All Employees (Medium) — root-to-leaf with weighted edges.

docs.title: 05 — Cycle detection (DFS three-color)
docs.text: DFS • Position 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a directed graph, return True if it contains a cycle. Equivalently, decide whether a topological order exists.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A boolean visited set isn't enough for directed graphs — revisiting a previously-finished node is not a cycle. You need to distinguish three states: white (never seen), gray (currently on the DFS stack), and black (finished, subtree fully explored). The invariant is that hitting a gray node means you've closed a loop on the current recursion path — that's a back-edge and proves a cycle.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Walk every node. For each white node, run a DFS that flips it gray, recurses on neighbours, and flips it black on return. Any neighbour found gray is the cycle witness.

WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2

def hasCycle(graph, n):
    color = [WHITE] * n

    def dfs(u):
        color[u] = GRAY
        for v in graph[u]:
            if color[v] == GRAY:   # back-edge → cycle
                return True
            if color[v] == WHITE and dfs(v):
                return True
        color[u] = BLACK
        return False

    return any(color[u] == WHITE and dfs(u) for u in range(n))

For undirected graphs, replace the gray-check with a parent-pointer test: a cycle exists iff DFS hits a visited node that isn't the immediate parent.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Graph: 0 → 1 → 2 → 0 (a 3-cycle) plus 3 isolated. Colors start all white.
  2. Start DFS at 0: paint 0 gray, recurse into 1.
  3. Paint 1 gray, recurse into 2. Paint 2 gray, look at neighbour 0.
  4. 0 is gray — back-edge detected. Cycle confirmed, unwind with True.
  5. (Had there been no cycle, 2 would have turned black, then 1, then 0, and DFS would continue from 3.)

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V + E) space O(V) colors + recursion

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using a single visited boolean on a directed graph — false positives on diamonds (two paths meeting at a sink).
  • Forgetting to flip gray → black on return — every node will look like a cycle.
  • On undirected graphs, calling the trivial back-edge to parent a cycle — pass parent or track edge IDs.
  • Iterating only from one source — must restart DFS from every unseen node to cover disconnected components.
  • Mutating the graph during traversal (e.g., adding edges from a callback) — corrupts the invariant.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 207. Course Schedule (Medium) — cycle = can't finish.
  • 210. Course Schedule II (Medium) — topo order via post-order DFS.
  • 802. Find Eventual Safe States (Medium) — nodes not on any cycle.
  • 261. Graph Valid Tree (Medium) — connected + no cycle.
  • 684. Redundant Connection (Medium) — find the edge that closes the cycle.
  • 685. Redundant Connection II (Hard) — directed variant, in-degree + cycle.
  • 1059. All Paths from Source Lead to Destination (Medium) — color-based DFS with leaf check.
  • 1192. Critical Connections in a Network (Hard) — Tarjan's bridges via DFS low-link.
  • 886. Possible Bipartition (Medium) — 2-coloring via DFS.
  • 785. Is Graph Bipartite? (Medium) — alternating colors, conflict = odd cycle.

docs.title: DSA · 06 · Backtracking
docs.text: Try, recurse, undo — subsets, permutations, N-queens, word search.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem says "generate all ...", "find all ...", or "count all ..." valid configurations.
  • You need every path, combination, or permutation — not just one optimal answer.
  • The input is small (typically n ≤ 20) and the search space is exponential but heavily prunable.
  • You can describe the state as a partial solution that can be extended one decision at a time.
  • A natural pruning rule exists (constraint, used-set, monotonicity) that lets you abandon subtrees early.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Backtracking is DFS on a decision tree. At each node you make a choice, recurse, then undo the choice before trying the next. The choice you just made is the difference between this branch and its sibling — undoing it is what lets you reuse the same path buffer across the whole search instead of allocating fresh state per leaf. The skeleton is always: base case → capture solution → loop over candidates → choose → recurse → un-choose. Pruning is what separates a 2-second solution from a 2-hour timeout — sort, dedupe, and add constraint checks before the recursive call.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Subsets

Include-or-exclude at every index.

02 Permutations

Pick from remaining at each level.

03 Combinations

Choose k of n with a monotone start index.

04 N-Queens

Place row-by-row with column/diagonal guards.

05 Word Search

Grid DFS with mark/unmark per cell.

06 Generate Parentheses

Two counters, two pruning rules.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Subsets / combinations use a monotone start index to avoid duplicate orderings. Permutations use a used[] array (or pick-from-remaining) because order matters. Sorted-input + skip-duplicates (if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue) is the dedupe trick that turns Subsets II and Permutations II from broken to correct. Constraint propagation (columns/diagonals for N-Queens, character counts for word puzzles) is how interview-quality solutions hit the time limit. When the problem only asks for one valid configuration, return True up the stack the moment you find it — no need to enumerate the rest.

docs.title: 01 — Subsets
docs.text: Backtracking • Position ⅙

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a set of n distinct integers, return every possible subset (the power set) — 2ⁿ subsets in total.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Walk the array left to right. At each index you make one of two decisions: include this element in the current subset, or skip it. The DFS tree has depth n and 2ⁿ leaves — each leaf is one subset. The invariant is that path always holds the include-decisions for indices [0..i); on every recursive entry, path is a valid subset of the prefix.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
A start pointer marks where we are in the array. We record path at every node (not just leaves) because every node is a valid subset.

def subsets(nums):
    res, path = [], []

    def dfs(start):
        res.append(path[:])          # snapshot — current subset
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            path.append(nums[i])     # choose
            dfs(i + 1)               # explore
            path.pop()               # un-choose

    dfs(0)
    return res

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. nums = [1, 2, 3]. Call dfs(0). Record [].
  2. Pick 1 → path = [1], recurse with start=1. Record [1].
  3. From there pick 2 → [1,2], record; pick 3 → [1,2,3], record; pop back.
  4. Back at [1], pick 3 → [1,3], record; pop. Pop 1.
  5. Back at root, pick 2 → record [2]; descend; eventually we've recorded all 8 subsets.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · 2ⁿ) space O(n) recursion (output excluded)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Appending path instead of path[:] — every result ends up pointing at the same list, which is empty at the end.
  • Using start = i + 0 instead of i + 1 — generates combinations with repetition.
  • For Subsets II (duplicates allowed in input), forgetting to sort first and add if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue.
  • Recording only at leaves — you'll miss every proper-prefix subset including [].
  • Confusing subsets with permutations — subsets care about set membership, not order.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 78. Subsets (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 90. Subsets II (Medium) — duplicates: sort + skip.
  • 77. Combinations (Medium) — same skeleton, pick exactly k.
  • 39. Combination Sum (Medium) — reuse allowed, prune by remaining target.
  • 40. Combination Sum II (Medium) — no reuse + duplicates.
  • 216. Combination Sum III (Medium) — k numbers summing to n.
  • 491. Non-decreasing Subsequences (Medium) — dedupe via per-level set.
  • 17. Letter Combinations of a Phone Number (Medium) — fixed-depth branching.
  • 1980. Find Unique Binary String (Medium) — backtrack on bit choice.
  • 320. Generalized Abbreviation (Medium) — abbreviate-or-keep at each char.

docs.title: 02 — Permutations
docs.text: Backtracking • Position 2/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a list of n distinct integers, return all n! orderings.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Unlike subsets, order matters and every element must appear. So at depth k of the DFS tree, the choice is "which unused element goes next." A used[] boolean array (or removing from a candidate list) is what enforces "each element exactly once." The invariant is that path is a valid permutation of some subset of the input, and len(path) + count(used == False) == n at every node.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Branch on each unused index at every level; mark used before recursing, unmark on the way back.

def permute(nums):
    res, path = [], []
    used = [False] * len(nums)

    def dfs():
        if len(path) == len(nums):
            res.append(path[:])
            return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if used[i]: continue
            used[i] = True
            path.append(nums[i])
            dfs()
            path.pop()
            used[i] = False

    dfs()
    return res

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. nums = [1, 2, 3]. Root: path = [], used = [F, F, F].
  2. Pick 1 → path=[1], used=[T,F,F]. Recurse.
  3. Pick 2 → path=[1,2]. Recurse. Pick 3 → path=[1,2,3], record. Unwind.
  4. Backtrack to [1], pick 3 → path=[1,3], then 2 → record [1,3,2].
  5. The whole subtree under 1 yields [1,2,3] and [1,3,2]. Repeat for roots 2 and 3 — 6 permutations total.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · n!) space O(n) recursion + used array

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting to reset used[i] = False after recursing — the search collapses to a single path.
  • For Permutations II (input has duplicates), missing the dedupe rule: sort, then if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]: continue.
  • Swap-in-place permutations look elegant but corrupt the input — don't use them if the caller needs the original.
  • Recording at every node instead of only at full length — output is flooded with partial paths.
  • Using a set instead of a list for path — sets lose order, which is the whole point of permutations.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 46. Permutations (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 47. Permutations II (Medium) — duplicates: sort + used-prev guard.
  • 31. Next Permutation (Medium) — in-place lexicographic next.
  • 60. Permutation Sequence (Hard) — k-th permutation by math, not enumeration.
  • 526. Beautiful Arrangement (Medium) — backtrack with divisibility constraint.
  • 996. Number of Squareful Arrays (Hard) — adjacency pair must square.
  • 357. Count Numbers with Unique Digits (Medium) — counting variant.
  • 1079. Letter Tile Possibilities (Medium) — count distinct permutations.
  • 1641. Count Sorted Vowel Strings (Medium) — non-decreasing fixed-length sequences.
  • 22. Generate Parentheses (Medium) — constrained permutation of brackets.

docs.title: 03 — Combinations C(n, k)
docs.text: Backtracking • Position 3/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Return all ways to choose k numbers from 1..n. Order within a combination doesn't matter; [1,2] and [2,1] are the same.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Because order doesn't matter, fix a canonical ordering: always pick increasing indices. A monotone start pointer rises after every choice, so the same combination can never be generated twice. The invariant is that path holds a strictly-increasing sequence drawn from [1..n], and we stop branching once len(path) == k.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Stop the moment path has k items. While recursing, only try numbers ≥ start, and bail out early when there aren't enough remaining numbers to fill the slots.

def combine(n, k):
    res, path = [], []

    def dfs(start):
        if len(path) == k:
            res.append(path[:])
            return
        # pruning: need (k - len(path)) more, must leave that many
        for i in range(start, n - (k - len(path)) + 2):
            path.append(i)
            dfs(i + 1)
            path.pop()

    dfs(1)
    return res

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n=4, k=2. Start: path=[].
  2. Try 1 → path=[1]. Recurse. Try 2 → [1,2], record. Try 3 → [1,3], record. Try 4 → [1,4], record.
  3. Backtrack. Try 2 → path=[2]. Recurse. Try 3 → [2,3], record. Try 4 → [2,4], record.
  4. Try 3 → [3]. Recurse. Try 4 → [3,4], record.
  5. Try 4 → only 1 element left, can't fill k=2 — pruned. Final output: 6 combinations.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(k · C(n,k)) space O(k) recursion (output excluded)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Looping over all n numbers at every depth — produces duplicate orderings and explodes the runtime.
  • Recursing with dfs(i) instead of dfs(i + 1) — allows reuse, which is a different problem.
  • Skipping the pruning bound — passes small cases but TLEs on n=20, k=10.
  • Off-by-one in the pruning bound: the upper limit should be n - (k - len(path)) + 1 (inclusive).
  • Forgetting that LeetCode uses 1-indexed numbers — starting at 0 gives wrong output.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 77. Combinations (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 78. Subsets (Medium) — same skeleton without the k constraint.
  • 39. Combination Sum (Medium) — reuse allowed, prune by target.
  • 40. Combination Sum II (Medium) — no reuse + duplicates.
  • 216. Combination Sum III (Medium) — k numbers summing to n from 1..9.
  • 17. Letter Combinations of a Phone Number (Medium) — fixed-depth combination tree.
  • 89. Gray Code (Medium) — sequence each differing by one bit.
  • 1238. Circular Permutation in Binary Representation (Medium) — Gray code with a start.
  • 254. Factor Combinations (Medium) — divisor-based combinations.
  • 1922. Count Good Numbers (Medium) — counting variant by exponent.

docs.title: 04 — N-Queens
docs.text: Backtracking • Position 4/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Place n queens on an n × n board so that no two share a row, column, or diagonal. Return all valid placements.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Each row gets exactly one queen — so the decision per row is "which column?" That reduces the problem from n² cells to n choices per row, and the depth of the recursion is exactly n. The invariant is that rows [0..r) already hold a partial valid placement, and three sets — used columns, used "↘" diagonals (r-c), and used "↙" diagonals (r+c) — record which cells the next queen cannot enter.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Walk row by row. For each row, try every column that isn't blocked by the three guard sets; recurse, then undo.

def solveNQueens(n):
    res, path = [], []
    cols, diag1, diag2 = set(), set(), set()   # c, r-c, r+c

    def dfs(r):
        if r == n:
            res.append(["." * c + "Q" + "." * (n - c - 1) for c in path])
            return
        for c in range(n):
            if c in cols or (r - c) in diag1 or (r + c) in diag2:
                continue
            cols.add(c); diag1.add(r - c); diag2.add(r + c)
            path.append(c)
            dfs(r + 1)
            path.pop()
            cols.remove(c); diag1.remove(r - c); diag2.remove(r + c)

    dfs(0)
    return res

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n = 4. Row 0: try col 0 — recurse.
  2. Row 1: col 0/1 blocked (col + diag), col 2 ok — place. Row 2: every col blocked — backtrack.
  3. Row 1 try col 3 — place. Row 2: col 1 works. Row 3: only col 0 free but blocked by diag — backtrack all the way to row 0.
  4. Row 0: try col 1. Cascade down — col 3, col 0, col 2 all line up → solution! [1,3,0,2].
  5. Continue from row 0 col 2 — finds mirror [2,0,3,1]. Two solutions for n=4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n!) with diagonal pruning space O(n) path + 3 guard sets

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Re-checking conflicts with an O(n) scan per cell instead of the three O(1) guard sets — works but TLEs at n=12+.
  • Confusing the two diagonals: ↘ cells share r - c, ↙ cells share r + c.
  • Forgetting to remove from cols/diag1/diag2 on backtrack — false conflicts on sibling branches.
  • Building the board string with concatenation inside the hot loop instead of joining a list once at the leaf.
  • Returning the same path reference instead of a snapshot — every output ends up identical.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 51. N-Queens (Hard) — return all boards.
  • 52. N-Queens II (Hard) — count only, same backtracking.
  • 37. Sudoku Solver (Hard) — same idea, three constraint sets.
  • 36. Valid Sudoku (Medium) — checker that powers the solver.
  • 980. Unique Paths III (Hard) — backtrack with cell mask.
  • 1239. Maximum Length of a Concatenated String with Unique Characters (Medium) — bitmask backtracking.
  • 698. Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets (Medium) — assign items to k bins.
  • 473. Matchsticks to Square (Medium) — k=4 partition variant.
  • 1255. Maximum Score Words Formed by Letters (Hard) — include/exclude with letter budget.
  • 1849. Splitting a String Into Descending Consecutive Values (Medium) — string-partition backtracking.

docs.title: 05 — Word Search (grid DFS with backtracking)
docs.text: Backtracking • Position ⅚

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a 2D grid of letters and a target word, return True if the word can be spelled by walking 4-directionally between adjacent cells, with no cell reused.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: For every cell that matches the first letter, start a DFS that temporarily marks the cell as used, then tries the four neighbours for the next letter. The mark-then-recurse-then-unmark pattern is exactly what makes this backtracking and not plain DFS — the same cell must be available to other branches once we abandon this one. The invariant is that at depth i, path spells word[0..i] and every cell on path is currently marked.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Iterate cells, launch DFS on first-letter matches. The DFS swaps the cell to a sentinel before recursing and restores it on the way out.

def exist(board, word):
    m, n = len(board), len(board[0])

    def dfs(r, c, i):
        if i == len(word): return True
        if r < 0 or r >= m or c < 0 or c >= n: return False
        if board[r][c] != word[i]: return False
        board[r][c] = '#'                         # mark
        found = (dfs(r+1, c, i+1) or dfs(r-1, c, i+1)
              or dfs(r, c+1, i+1) or dfs(r, c-1, i+1))
        board[r][c] = word[i]                     # unmark
        return found

    return any(dfs(r, c, 0) for r in range(m) for c in range(n))

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Word "ABCCED", board with A at (0,0).
  2. Launch DFS at (0,0). Mark A. Try right to B (0,1) — match. Mark.
  3. From B, go right to C (0,2) — match. From C, down to C (1,2) — match.
  4. From (1,2), go down to E (2,2) — match. From E, left to D (2,1) — match. i == 6 → return True.
  5. The recursion unwinds, restoring every cell to its original letter so the board is left clean.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(m·n · 4^L) L = word length space O(L) recursion

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using a separate visited set instead of in-place marking — correct but doubles the per-call cost.
  • Forgetting to unmark the cell on the way out — sibling branches falsely see the cell as taken.
  • Returning False as soon as one neighbour fails instead of OR-ing all four directions.
  • Early-exit shortcut bug: if you store found = dfs(...) four times without short-circuiting, you do four times the work even after a hit.
  • For Word Search II (many words), running this for each word — use a Trie + DFS instead.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 79. Word Search (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 212. Word Search II (Hard) — Trie + grid DFS.
  • 980. Unique Paths III (Hard) — visit every empty cell once.
  • 1219. Path with Maximum Gold (Medium) — DFS that returns a max.
  • 489. Robot Room Cleaner (Hard) — DFS in an unknown grid with movement primitives.
  • 332. Reconstruct Itinerary (Hard) — backtracking on edges (Hierholzer).
  • 22. Generate Parentheses (Medium) — constrained string builder.
  • 51. N-Queens (Hard) — backtracking with constraint sets.
  • 17. Letter Combinations of a Phone Number (Medium) — fixed-depth tree.
  • 320. Generalized Abbreviation (Medium) — per-char choice.

docs.title: 06 — Generate Parentheses
docs.text: Backtracking • Position 6/6

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given n pairs, generate every well-formed string of n open and n close parens — e.g. n=3 produces ["((()))","(()())","(()) ()","()(())","()()()"].

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: At every step you have at most two choices: append '(' or append ')'. Two simple constraints kill every invalid branch before you recurse: you can only open while open < n, and you can only close while close < open. The invariant is that every prefix in path is itself a valid (possibly incomplete) parenthesis string — never has more ) than (, never exceeds n opens.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Carry two counters — open (so far) and close (so far). Two if-branches, both pruning.

def generateParenthesis(n):
    res, path = [], []

    def dfs(open_, close):
        if len(path) == 2 * n:
            res.append("".join(path))
            return
        if open_ < n:
            path.append("("); dfs(open_ + 1, close); path.pop()
        if close < open_:
            path.append(")"); dfs(open_, close + 1); path.pop()

    dfs(0, 0)
    return res

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n = 3. Root: path="", open=0, close=0.
  2. Open allowed → "(" → recurse with (1,0).
  3. Both allowed: try "((" → (2,0). Then "(((" → (3,0). Now open == n, must close. Three closes → "((()))". Record.
  4. Backtrack. From "(((" we can't open more, all closes used. Pop up to "((", now try close → "(()" → recurse.
  5. Continue exploring; each leaf is a valid string. Final output: 5 strings (Catalan number C₃).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(4ⁿ / √n) Catalan space O(n) recursion + path

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Generating all 2^(2n) strings and filtering — works for n ≤ 4, dies after.
  • Pruning rule reversed: if open > close: dfs(close) is wrong; the rule is on the choice you're about to make, i.e. close < open to add a close.
  • Tracking only total_length without open/close — you lose the ability to prune invalid prefixes.
  • Returning the path list reference (mutable) instead of "".join(path) — every result becomes empty.
  • Off-by-one: stopping at len(path) == n instead of 2 * n.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 22. Generate Parentheses (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 17. Letter Combinations of a Phone Number (Medium) — multi-choice generation.
  • 39. Combination Sum (Medium) — prune by remaining target.
  • 78. Subsets (Medium) — include/exclude generation.
  • 46. Permutations (Medium) — full-ordering generation.
  • 51. N-Queens (Hard) — constrained generation with guards.
  • 89. Gray Code (Medium) — sequence generation.
  • 320. Generalized Abbreviation (Medium) — per-char two-way branch.
  • 282. Expression Add Operators (Hard) — generate operator placements.
  • 241. Different Ways to Add Parentheses (Medium) — divide & conquer on operators.

docs.title: DSA · 07 · Heap / Priority Queue
docs.text: Top-K, median of stream, K closest, scheduling — anywhere you need fast min/max.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem mentions "top K", "K largest", "K smallest", or "K closest".
  • You need the median of a running stream or the smallest/largest element repeatedly.
  • You're merging K sorted sequences and want the next item in O(log K).
  • You're scheduling jobs by priority, cooldown, or cost (Dijkstra, task scheduler, IPO).
  • You need frequency-based answers ("top K frequent") and a sort would be overkill.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text:
A heap is a binary tree stored as an array where each parent is ≤ (min-heap) or ≥ (max-heap) its children. Python's heapq is a min-heap; negate values for max-heap behavior. The two operations you care about are push and pop, both O(log n). Building a heap from a list is O(n) via heapify. The common trick for top-K is to keep a heap of size K and evict the worst — that gives O(n log K) instead of O(n log n) for a full sort.

When you see "running" or "stream" or "as elements arrive," reach for a heap. When you see "K of something," reach for a size-K heap.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Top K Largest

Keep a min-heap of size K — the root is the K-th largest.

02 Merge K Sorted Lists

Heap of K heads — pop the smallest, push its successor.

03 Median of Stream

Two heaps — max-heap on the low half, min-heap on the high half.

04 K Closest Points

Max-heap of size K keyed on squared distance — evict the farthest.

05 Task Scheduler with Cooldown

Max-heap on frequency + cooldown queue for the n-step gap.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Lazy deletion — when you can't remove an arbitrary heap entry, mark it stale and skip it on pop.
  • Indexed heap / heap + hash — supports decrease-key in O(log n) (Dijkstra with updates).
  • Two-heap pattern — median, sliding-window median, IPO-style "pick best feasible."
  • Fixed-size heap — for top-K, the heap size is bounded by K, not n.

docs.title: 01 — Top K Largest
docs.text: Heap / Priority Queue • Position ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an array and an integer K, return the K largest elements (or the K-th largest).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A full sort is O(n log n), but we only need K elements. Keep a min-heap of size K as you scan the array. The root is the smallest of your current top-K candidates — if a new element beats it, replace the root. The invariant: the heap always contains the K largest elements seen so far, and its root is the K-th largest.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Maintain a min-heap. Push every element; whenever the heap exceeds size K, pop the smallest. At the end, the heap holds the answer; the root is the K-th largest.

import heapq

def top_k_largest(nums, k):
    heap = []
    for x in nums:
        heapq.heappush(heap, x)
        if len(heap) > k:
            heapq.heappop(heap)      # evict the smallest of the top-(K+1)
    return heap                       # K largest; heap[0] is K-th largest

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: nums = [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], K = 2.

  1. Push 3 → heap [3].
  2. Push 2 → heap [2, 3]. Size = K, no evict.
  3. Push 1 → heap [1, 3, 2]. Pop 1 → heap [2, 3].
  4. Push 5 → heap [2, 3, 5]. Pop 2 → heap [3, 5].
  5. Push 6 → heap [3, 5, 6]. Pop 3 → heap [5, 6]. Root 5 is the 2nd largest.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log K) space O(K)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Min-heap for largest — counter-intuitive but correct: you evict the smallest survivor.
  • Quickselect is O(n) average for the K-th element; use it when K is large relative to n.
  • For top-K frequent, hash-count first, then heap on (count, value) pairs.
  • Tie-breaking — when frequencies tie, problems often want lexicographic order; push (count, -ord(c)) or similar.
  • Python's heapq.nlargest(k, nums) is the one-liner — know it exists, but be ready to hand-roll in an interview.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 215 Kth Largest Element in an Array (Med) — the canonical problem.
  • 347 Top K Frequent Elements (Med) — hash-count, then size-K heap.
  • 692 Top K Frequent Words (Med) — tie-break on lexicographic order.
  • 451 Sort Characters By Frequency (Med) — full sort via heap dump.
  • 973 K Closest Points to Origin (Med) — same template, keyed on distance.
  • 703 Kth Largest Element in a Stream (Easy) — keep heap as state.
  • 1985 Find the Kth Largest Integer in the Array (Med) — heap on big-int strings.
  • 2099 Find Subsequence of Length K With the Largest Sum (Easy) — top-K with index recovery.
  • 1845 Seat Reservation Manager (Med) — min-heap of free seat numbers.
  • 1631 Path With Minimum Effort (Med) — Dijkstra-flavored heap usage.

docs.title: 02 — Merge K Sorted Lists
docs.text: Heap / Priority Queue • Position ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Merge K sorted linked lists (or arrays) into one sorted output.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: At any moment, the next element of the merged output must be the smallest among the current heads of all K lists. A min-heap of those K heads gives the next element in O(log K), and after popping we push the popped head's successor. The invariant: the heap always contains at most one live pointer per list, namely the smallest unmerged element of that list.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Seed the heap with (value, list_index, node) for every non-empty head. Loop: pop the smallest, append it to the result, and if the popped node has a successor, push that successor. Stop when the heap is empty.

import heapq

def merge_k_sorted(lists):
    heap, out = [], []
    for i, lst in enumerate(lists):
        if lst:
            heapq.heappush(heap, (lst[0], i, 0))  # (val, list_idx, pos)
    while heap:
        val, i, j = heapq.heappop(heap)
        out.append(val)
        if j + 1 < len(lists[i]):
            heapq.heappush(heap, (lists[i][j + 1], i, j + 1))
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: lists = [[1, 4, 5], [1, 3, 4], [2, 6]].

  1. Seed heap with heads: (1, 0, 0), (1, 1, 0), (2, 2, 0).
  2. Pop (1, 0, 0) → out=[1]. Push (4, 0, 1).
  3. Pop (1, 1, 0) → out=[1, 1]. Push (3, 1, 1).
  4. Pop (2, 2, 0) → out=[1, 1, 2]. Push (6, 2, 1).
  5. Continue popping (3, 4, 4, 5, 6) — final output [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(N log K) space O(K)

Where N is the total number of elements across all K lists.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Tuples must be comparable — for linked-list nodes, include a tiebreaker like the list index so Python doesn't try to compare nodes.
  • Don't reseed — only push the successor of the popped element, not all heads on every iteration.
  • Pairwise merge is O(N log K) too but uses O(1) extra space — prefer it when memory matters.
  • Empty input lists — skip them at seed time or guard inside the loop.
  • For streaming K-way merge (infinite lists), generators + heap let you yield lazily.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 23 Merge k Sorted Lists (Hard) — the canonical problem.
  • 21 Merge Two Sorted Lists (Easy) — the K=2 base case, no heap needed.
  • 88 Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — in-place pointer variant.
  • 378 Kth Smallest in Sorted Matrix (Med) — rows act as sorted lists.
  • 632 Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists (Hard) — heap + sliding max.
  • 295 Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — two-heap pattern.
  • 373 Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums (Med) — heap over pairs (i, j).
  • 1675 Minimize Deviation in Array (Hard) — heap of transformable max values.
  • 1953 Maximum Number of Weeks for Which You Can Work (Med) — scheduling with priority.
  • 1882 Process Tasks Using Servers (Med) — two heaps: free and busy servers.

docs.title: 03 — Median of Stream
docs.text: Heap / Priority Queue • Position ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Numbers arrive one at a time. After each insertion, return the median of all numbers seen so far.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Split the data into a lower half (everything ≤ median) and an upper half (everything ≥ median). Keep the lower half in a max-heap so its root is the largest of the small ones, and the upper half in a min-heap so its root is the smallest of the big ones. The invariant: len(low) == len(high) or len(low) == len(high) + 1, and max(low) ≤ min(high). The median is low.top() (odd count) or the average of both tops (even count).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Push to low (max-heap). Move low's top to high to keep high's root ≥ low's root. If high grew too big, move its top back to low.

import heapq

class MedianFinder:
    def __init__(self):
        self.low = []   # max-heap (store as negatives)
        self.high = []  # min-heap

    def add(self, x):
        heapq.heappush(self.low, -x)
        heapq.heappush(self.high, -heapq.heappop(self.low))
        if len(self.high) > len(self.low):
            heapq.heappush(self.low, -heapq.heappop(self.high))

    def median(self):
        if len(self.low) > len(self.high):
            return -self.low[0]
        return (-self.low[0] + self.high[0]) / 2

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: stream [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

  1. Add 1 → low=[-1], high=[]. Median = 1.
  2. Add 2 → push 2 into low, shuffle: low=[-1], high=[2]. Median = (1 + 2)/2 = 1.5.
  3. Add 3 → low=[-2, -1], high=[3]. Median = 2.
  4. Add 4 → low=[-2, -1], high=[3, 4]. Median = (2 + 3)/2 = 2.5.
  5. Add 5 → low=[-3, -1, -2], high=[4, 5]. Median = 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(log n) per insert, O(1) median space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
