• Always shuffle through both heaps — push into low, immediately move low's top to high, then rebalance. This keeps max(low) ≤ min(high) automatically.
  • Python max-heap — negate on push and pop. Off-by-one signs are the #1 bug here.
  • Even-count median is the average — return a float or be careful with integer division.
  • For sliding-window median, you need an indexed heap or a multiset; plain heaps can't remove arbitrary elements in O(log n).
  • Stream is mutable — don't store all numbers in a list "just in case"; the heaps are your full state.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 295 Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — the canonical problem.
  • 480 Sliding Window Median (Hard) — add lazy deletion or a multiset.
  • 4 Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Hard) — binary search, not heap, but related.
  • 1825 Finding MK Average (Hard) — three multisets / heaps for streaming stats.
  • 703 Kth Largest Element in a Stream (Easy) — single-heap warm-up.
  • 502 IPO (Hard) — two-heap "best feasible" pattern.
  • 1383 Maximum Performance of a Team (Hard) — sort + size-K heap.
  • 1882 Process Tasks Using Servers (Med) — two-heap scheduling.
  • 1834 Single-Threaded CPU (Med) — time-ordered + priority heap.
  • 295 Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — re-implement after a week.

docs.title: 04 — K Closest Points to Origin
docs.text: Heap / Priority Queue • Position ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a list of 2D points, return the K closest to the origin (Euclidean distance).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Same template as top-K largest, just keyed differently. We want the K smallest distances, so we keep a max-heap of size K: the heap's root is the worst (farthest) of our current K candidates. Any incoming point closer than the root displaces it. The invariant: the heap holds the K closest points seen so far, and its root is the K-th closest (i.e., the farthest of the keepers).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Push every point with key = squared distance. Whenever the heap exceeds K, pop the worst. No need to square-root — squared distance preserves ordering.

import heapq

def k_closest(points, k):
    heap = []  # max-heap: store (-dist², point)
    for x, y in points:
        d2 = x * x + y * y
        heapq.heappush(heap, (-d2, x, y))
        if len(heap) > k:
            heapq.heappop(heap)        # evict the farthest of the top-(K+1)
    return [[x, y] for _, x, y in heap]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: points = [[1, 3], [-2, 2], [5, 8], [0, 1]], K = 2.

  1. Push (1, 3) d²=10 → heap [(-10, 1, 3)].
  2. Push (-2, 2) d²=8 → heap [(-10, 1, 3), (-8, -2, 2)]. Size = K.
  3. Push (5, 8) d²=89 → heap grows. Pop root (-89, 5, 8) — wait, max-heap stores negatives so the most negative is the smallest d² in our representation. After push: [(-89, 5, 8), (-10, 1, 3), (-8, -2, 2)]. Pop the largest d² → pop (-8, -2, 2)? No — we want to keep the closest, so we evict the largest d². With negated keys, the smallest negative is the largest d². Pop the root if its d² > current candidate's d². Cleaner: store -d² as key, heap is min on negatives = max on d². Pop evicts the farthest. Result: [(-10, 1, 3), (-8, -2, 2)].
  4. Push (0, 1) d²=1 → push, pop (-10, 1, 3). Heap = [(-8, -2, 2), (-1, 0, 1)].
  5. Final K=2 closest: (-2, 2) and (0, 1).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log K) space O(K)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Use squared distance — no sqrt, no float drift, same ordering.
  • Max-heap for closest — same counter-intuitive flip as top-K largest. Negate keys for heapq.
  • Quickselect is O(n) average and beats heap when K is large; know both.
  • Ties — when two points have equal distance, problems usually allow any order. Confirm with the interviewer.
  • 3D / higher dims — same template, just d² = sum(c² for c in coords).
Practice — LeetCode
  • 973 K Closest Points to Origin (Med) — the canonical problem.
  • 692 Top K Frequent Words (Med) — same template, different key.
  • 347 Top K Frequent Elements (Med) — hash-count + heap.
  • 658 Find K Closest Elements (Med) — sorted input → two-pointer beats heap.
  • 215 Kth Largest (Med) — same template, simpler key.
  • 1985 Find the Kth Largest Integer (Med) — string-based comparator.
  • 264 Ugly Number II (Med) — heap to generate sorted sequence.
  • 313 Super Ugly Number (Med) — heap with K prime factors.
  • 786 K-th Smallest Prime Fraction (Med) — heap over pairs.
  • 1057 Campus Bikes (Med) — heap on (distance, worker, bike).

docs.title: 05 — Task Scheduler with Cooldown
docs.text: Heap / Priority Queue • Position 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a list of tasks (each labeled by a character) and a cooldown n (the same task can't run within n steps), find the minimum number of CPU cycles to finish all tasks.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: At each cycle, you want to run the task with the highest remaining count — that's a max-heap on frequencies. But you can't run a task again until its cooldown expires, so popped tasks go into a cooldown queue along with the time they become eligible. The invariant: the heap holds every task that's available now; the queue holds every task waiting for its cooldown to lapse.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Count frequencies, build a max-heap. Each cycle: pop the most-frequent task (if any), decrement, and if it still has runs left, push (eligible_time, count) to the cooldown queue. At the start of each cycle, move all queue entries whose eligibility has arrived back into the heap. If the heap is empty, the CPU idles. Stop when both structures are empty.

import heapq
from collections import Counter, deque

def least_interval(tasks, n):
    heap = [-c for c in Counter(tasks).values()]
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    queue = deque()   # (ready_time, neg_count)
    t = 0
    while heap or queue:
        t += 1
        if heap:
            cnt = heapq.heappop(heap) + 1   # one less remaining
            if cnt < 0:
                queue.append((t + n, cnt))
        if queue and queue[0][0] == t:
            heapq.heappush(heap, queue.popleft()[1])
    return t

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: tasks = ['A', 'A', 'A', 'B', 'B', 'B'], n = 2.

  1. t=1: pop A (cnt 3→2), push to queue with ready=4. Heap=[-3 B].
  2. t=2: pop B (cnt 3→2), queue=[(4, -2 A), (5, -2 B)]. Heap empty.
  3. t=3: heap empty → idle. Queue front ready at t=4.
  4. t=4: queue front pops back to heap, pop A (2→1), push ready=7. Queue=[(5, -2 B), (7, -1 A)].
  5. t=5..8: alternate B, A, idle/B, A → total 8 cycles.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(T log K) space O(K)

Where T is the total cycles and K is the number of distinct tasks (≤ 26 for letters → effectively O(T)).

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Idle cycles count — when the heap is empty but the queue isn't, the CPU still ticks forward.
  • Closed-form shortcut — max((maxFreq - 1) * (n + 1) + tieCount, len(tasks)) gives the answer in O(T). Mention it once you've coded the heap version.
  • Cooldown queue is FIFO — entries become eligible in insertion order because each lasts the same n cycles.
  • Don't push back zero-count tasks — they're done; pushing them wastes log K.
  • For task-with-deadline / per-task cost variants, the heap key changes but the queue mechanism stays the same.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 621 Task Scheduler (Med) — the canonical problem.
  • 358 Rearrange String k Distance Apart (Hard) — same template, output the schedule.
  • 767 Reorganize String (Med) — k=2 special case.
  • 1834 Single-Threaded CPU (Med) — heap on (processingTime, index).
  • 1882 Process Tasks Using Servers (Med) — two heaps for free/busy.
  • 1942 The Number of the Smallest Unoccupied Chair (Med) — heap of free chairs + arrival time heap.
  • 502 IPO (Hard) — sort by capital, max-heap on profit.
  • 295 Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — two-heap warm-up.
  • 1353 Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended (Med) — greedy + heap on end time.
  • 1942 The Number of the Smallest Unoccupied Chair (Med) — re-implement for two-heap drill.

docs.title: DSA · 08 · Graph Algorithms
docs.text: Dijkstra, MST, Union-Find, topological sort — the graph algorithm toolkit.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• Shortest paths between nodes with non-negative weights → Dijkstra; with negatives → Bellman-Ford; all-pairs → Floyd-Warshall.
  • Minimum spanning tree for "connect everything cheapest" → Kruskal or Prim.
  • Dependencies / ordering ("course schedule", "alien dictionary") → topological sort.
  • Connectivity / grouping ("number of provinces", "redundant connection") → Union-Find.
  • Strongly connected components in a directed graph ("critical connections") → Tarjan.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text:
Graph problems factor into two questions: what representation? (adjacency list almost always) and what traversal? (BFS for unweighted shortest paths, DFS for connectivity / topo, priority queue for weighted). The algorithms in this section are specialized traversals — Dijkstra is BFS with a priority queue, Kahn's topo is BFS over an in-degree map, Union-Find is "implicit" graph traversal via merging, and Tarjan is DFS with low-link bookkeeping.

Pick the algorithm by reading the problem's operation (shortest, connect, order, partition) and the edge weight constraints (none, non-negative, possibly negative, all-pairs).

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Topological Sort (Kahn)

BFS over in-degree zero — linear order of a DAG.

02 Dijkstra Shortest Path

Min-heap + non-negative weights — fastest single-source.

03 Bellman-Ford

Relax every edge V-1 times — handles negative weights and detects negative cycles.

04 Floyd-Warshall

All-pairs shortest paths via triple loop — O(V³) but trivially correct.

05 Union-Find (DSU)

Path compression + union by rank — near-constant per-op.

06 Kruskal MST

Sort edges, add if endpoints disjoint — Union-Find under the hood.

07 Prim MST

Grow the tree one cheapest edge at a time — heap variant of Dijkstra.

08 Tarjan SCC

DFS + low-link — strongly connected components and bridges in one pass.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text:
• Multi-source — seed Dijkstra/BFS with multiple starts; distance 0 for each.
  • K-shortest paths — relax the "visited" invariant; allow up to K relaxations per node.
  • 0-1 BFS — deque + push to front for 0-weight edges, back for 1-weight; replaces Dijkstra at O(V+E).
  • A* — Dijkstra + admissible heuristic; same code, different key.
  • Bidirectional search — meet in the middle; halves the explored frontier on average.

docs.title: 01 — Topological Sort (Kahn)
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position ⅛

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a directed acyclic graph (DAG), output a linear ordering of nodes such that every edge u → v has u before v. If the graph has a cycle, report it.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A node is "ready" once all of its prerequisites have been emitted — i.e., when its in-degree drops to zero. Kahn's algorithm peels off zero-in-degree nodes one at a time, like leaves off a tree, decrementing in-degrees as it goes. The invariant: at every step, the queue contains exactly the nodes whose prerequisites are all already emitted. If the loop ends without visiting every node, the leftover nodes form a cycle.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Build the in-degree map. Seed a queue with every zero-in-degree node. Pop, append to output, and decrement each neighbor's in-degree; when a neighbor hits zero, enqueue it. If the output length equals V, return it; otherwise the graph has a cycle.

from collections import deque, defaultdict

def topo_sort(n, edges):
    g = defaultdict(list)
    indeg = [0] * n
    for u, v in edges:
        g[u].append(v)
        indeg[v] += 1
    q = deque(i for i in range(n) if indeg[i] == 0)
    out = []
    while q:
        u = q.popleft()
        out.append(u)
        for v in g[u]:
            indeg[v] -= 1
            if indeg[v] == 0:
                q.append(v)
    return out if len(out) == n else []   # [] signals a cycle

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: n = 6, edges [(5,2), (5,0), (4,0), (4,1), (2,3), (3,1)].

  1. In-degrees: 0→1, 1→2, 2→1, 3→1, 4→0, 5→0. Queue starts with [4, 5].
  2. Pop 4 → out=[4]. Decrement 0 (→0) and 1 (→1). Queue=[5, 0].
  3. Pop 5 → out=[4, 5]. Decrement 2 (→0) and 0 (already 0). Queue=[0, 2].
  4. Pop 0 → out=[4, 5, 0]. No outgoing edges. Pop 2 → out=[4, 5, 0, 2]. Decrement 3 (→0). Queue=[3].
  5. Pop 3 → decrement 1 (→0). Pop 1 → out=[4, 5, 0, 2, 3, 1]. Length = 6 → done.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V + E) space O(V + E)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Cycle detection comes for free — if len(out) < n, you have a cycle. Don't add a separate DFS pass.
  • Multiple valid orderings exist — use a min-heap instead of a queue if the problem demands the lexicographically smallest.
  • DFS-based topo (post-order + reverse) is equally valid and sometimes cleaner; know both.
  • Self-loops count as a cycle (in-degree 1 from the get-go and never reachable from zero).
  • Disconnected DAG — works fine; every connected component contributes its zero-in-degree nodes.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 207 Course Schedule (Med) — pure cycle detection via topo.
  • 210 Course Schedule II (Med) — emit the order, not just feasibility.
  • 269 Alien Dictionary (Hard) — derive edges from adjacent word pairs.
  • 444 Sequence Reconstruction (Med) — unique topo order check.
  • 802 Find Eventual Safe States (Med) — reverse-graph topo.
  • 1136 Parallel Courses (Med) — topo with semester levels.
  • 1203 Sort Items by Groups Respecting Dependencies (Hard) — two-level topo.
  • 2127 Maximum Employees to Be Invited (Hard) — topo to peel off chains, then handle cycles.
  • 310 Minimum Height Trees (Med) — peel leaves (degree-1) inward.
  • 329 Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix (Hard) — topo on implicit DAG.

docs.title: 02 — Dijkstra Shortest Path
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position 2/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights and a source node, find the shortest distance from source to every other node.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Greedy BFS with a priority queue: always extend from the closest unfinalized node. Because weights are non-negative, once you pop a node from the heap, its distance is finalized — no later path can improve it. The invariant: the heap holds tentative distances; every popped node's distance is optimal. The proof rests on non-negativity: any unexplored path to an already-popped node would have to detour through a node currently in the heap, whose distance is already ≥ the one we popped.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Initialize dist[source] = 0, everything else infinity. Push (0, source). While the heap is non-empty: pop (d, u). Skip if d > dist[u] (stale entry). For each neighbor v with edge weight w, if d + w < dist[v], update and push (d + w, v).

import heapq

def dijkstra(n, graph, src):
    dist = [float('inf')] * n
    dist[src] = 0
    heap = [(0, src)]
    while heap:
        d, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if d > dist[u]:
            continue                  # stale
        for v, w in graph[u]:
            nd = d + w
            if nd < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = nd
                heapq.heappush(heap, (nd, v))
    return dist

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: nodes 0–4, edges 0→1 (4), 0→2 (1), 2→1 (2), 1→3 (1), 2→3 (5), 3→4 (3). Source = 0.

  1. Push (0, 0). dist=[0, ∞, ∞, ∞, ∞].
  2. Pop (0, 0). Relax 1 → dist[1]=4, push (4, 1). Relax 2 → dist[2]=1, push (1, 2).
  3. Pop (1, 2). Relax 1 → 1+2=3 < 4 → dist[1]=3, push (3, 1). Relax 3 → dist[3]=6, push (6, 3).
  4. Pop (3, 1). Relax 3 → 3+1=4 < 6 → dist[3]=4, push (4, 3). Stale (4, 1) will be discarded later.
  5. Pop (4, 3). Relax 4 → dist[4]=7. Pop remaining stale entries. Final: [0, 3, 1, 4, 7].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O((V + E) log V) space O(V + E)

With a Fibonacci heap, this drops to O(E + V log V), but interview answers use a binary heap.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Non-negative weights only — Dijkstra produces wrong answers on negative edges. Use Bellman-Ford.
  • Stale entries — heapq doesn't support decrease-key, so duplicates pile up. Check d > dist[u] and skip.
  • 0-1 BFS is faster (O(V+E)) when weights are only 0 or 1 — use a deque.
  • Track path by storing parent[v] = u on each successful relaxation; reconstruct by walking back.
  • Multi-source — push (0, s) for every source s at init; the algorithm handles it.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 743 Network Delay Time (Med) — the canonical single-source.
  • 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (Med) — Dijkstra with state (node, stops).
  • 1631 Path With Minimum Effort (Med) — relax on max along path, not sum.
  • 1514 Path with Maximum Probability (Med) — max-heap on product.
  • 778 Swim in Rising Water (Hard) — grid Dijkstra on max-elevation.
  • 1976 Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination (Med) — Dijkstra + path count DP.
  • 882 Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph (Hard) — Dijkstra on inflated graph.
  • 1928 Minimum Cost to Reach Destination in Time (Hard) — two-dim Dijkstra.
  • 1334 Find the City With the Smallest Number of Neighbors (Med) — Dijkstra from every node.
  • 2045 Second Minimum Time to Reach Destination (Hard) — track top-2 distances.

docs.title: 03 — Bellman-Ford
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position ⅜

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Single-source shortest paths in a weighted graph that may contain negative edges. Also detect negative cycles reachable from the source.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A shortest path in a graph with V nodes uses at most V−1 edges (otherwise it revisits a node and you can shortcut). So relax every edge V−1 times and every shortest distance will settle. The invariant: after k full passes, dist[v] equals the shortest path from source to v using at most k edges. A V-th pass that still improves any distance proves a negative cycle is reachable.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Init dist[source] = 0, infinity elsewhere. Loop V−1 times: for every edge (u, v, w), if dist[u] + w < dist[v], set dist[v] = dist[u] + w. Run one more pass; any further improvement signals a negative cycle.

def bellman_ford(n, edges, src):
    dist = [float('inf')] * n
    dist[src] = 0
    for _ in range(n - 1):
        updated = False
        for u, v, w in edges:
            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
                updated = True
        if not updated:
            break                       # early exit when stable
    # negative-cycle check
    for u, v, w in edges:
        if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
            return None                 # neg cycle reachable from src
    return dist

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V · E) space O(V)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Early exit when a full pass changes nothing — common test cases settle long before V−1.
  • Negative cycle detection is a separate V-th pass; don't claim cycles from intermediate passes.
  • Watch for overflow — dist[u] + w with dist[u] = inf should be guarded.
  • SPFA (Shortest Path Faster Algorithm) is a queue-based Bellman-Ford that's fast in practice but worst-case identical.
  • K-edge variant (problem 787) — run exactly K+1 passes; cap path edges and snapshot dist between passes to avoid relaxing twice in one pass.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (Med) — Bellman-Ford with K+1 passes is the cleanest solve.
  • 743 Network Delay Time (Med) — works here too (no negatives).
  • 1976 Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination (Med) — Dijkstra preferred, BF as comparison.
  • 882 Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph (Hard) — practice graph modeling.
  • 1786 Number of Restricted Paths (Med) — Dijkstra-then-DP, BF alternative.
  • 1928 Minimum Cost to Reach Destination (Hard) — time-constrained relaxation.
  • 1462 Course Schedule IV (Med) — transitive closure (BF-flavored).
  • 1631 Path With Minimum Effort (Med) — BF variant on max-along-path.
  • 1514 Path with Maximum Probability (Med) — relax on product.
  • 1334 Find the City (Med) — small V favors Floyd or BF-per-source.

docs.title: 04 — Floyd-Warshall
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position 4/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Compute the shortest distance between every pair of nodes in a weighted graph (no negative cycles).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: For every pair (i, j), consider routes that may pass through an intermediate node k. If dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j], the route through k wins. Iterate k from 0 to V−1; after k is processed, dist[i][j] is the shortest path using intermediates from {0..k} only. After the full loop, you've allowed every node as an intermediate — done. That's the loop invariant.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Init the distance matrix from the edge list (dist[i][i] = 0, edges fill in, rest infinity). Triple-loop: k outermost, then i, then j. Update on improvement.

def floyd_warshall(n, edges):
    INF = float('inf')
    dist = [[INF] * n for _ in range(n)]
    for i in range(n):
        dist[i][i] = 0
    for u, v, w in edges:
        dist[u][v] = min(dist[u][v], w)
    for k in range(n):
        for i in range(n):
            for j in range(n):
                if dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]:
                    dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]
    return dist

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: nodes 0–3, edges 0→1 (3), 0→3 (7), 1→0 (8), 1→2 (2), 2→0 (5), 2→3 (1), 3→0 (2).

  1. Init diagonals to 0, fill in direct edges. Everything else INF.
  2. k=0: route 1→0→3 = 8+7=15, no improvement vs INF (1→3 is INF, so set to 15). Similarly 2→0→3 = 5+7=12, 2→0→1 = 5+3=8.
  3. k=1: 0→1→2 = 3+2=5, update 0→2. 0→1→2→? continues next round.
  4. k=2: 0→2→3 = 5+1=6, beats 7. Update 0→3. 1→2→3 = 3, update 1→3 (15 → 3).
  5. k=3: 1→3→0 = 3+2=5, beats 1→0 (8). Final pairwise matrix complete.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V³) space O(V²)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• k is the outer loop — getting the loop order wrong silently produces wrong answers. Memorize: k, i, j.
  • Negative cycles — check dist[i][i] < 0 after the loop; any negative diagonal means a negative cycle is reachable through i.
  • V³ is brutal for V > ~500 — fall back to Dijkstra-per-source (O(V · (E + V) log V)) for sparse graphs.
  • Transitive closure — replace +/min with OR/AND to compute reachability in the same loop shape.
  • Path reconstruction — store next[i][j] = j (initially) and update next[i][j] = next[i][k] on relaxation; walk next to rebuild.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1334 Find the City (Med) — the canonical Floyd-Warshall problem.
  • 1462 Course Schedule IV (Med) — transitive closure flavor.
  • 1976 Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination (Med) — Dijkstra preferred; FW reference.
  • 743 Network Delay Time (Med) — FW on small graphs.
  • 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (Med) — FW with hop limit is awkward; BF cleaner.
  • 2101 Detonate the Maximum Bombs (Med) — reachability via FW.
  • 1971 Find if Path Exists (Easy) — reachability warm-up.
  • 1129 Shortest Path with Alternating Colors (Med) — BFS, but FW also works.
  • 1267 Count Servers that Communicate (Med) — row/col reachability counting.
  • 1361 Validate Binary Tree Nodes (Med) — reachability + structural checks.

docs.title: 05 — Union-Find (DSU)
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position ⅝

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Maintain a partition of N elements under two operations: find(x) returns the representative of x's group, and union(x, y) merges x's and y's groups. Detect whether two elements are in the same group in near-constant time.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Each group is a tree; the root is the representative. Path compression flattens the tree on every find so future queries are nearly direct. Union by rank always hangs the shorter tree under the taller one, keeping height in check. The invariant: every element's parent chain eventually reaches the root of its current group, and the amortized cost per op is O(α(n)) — the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for any n you'll see.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Store parent[i] (self-rooted initially) and rank[i] (initially 0). find recursively follows parents while compressing. union finds both roots, attaches the lower-rank one under the higher-rank one.

class DSU:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.parent = list(range(n))
        self.rank = [0] * n

    def find(self, x):
        if self.parent[x] != x:
            self.parent[x] = self.find(self.parent[x])    # path compression
        return self.parent[x]

    def union(self, x, y):
        rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y)
        if rx == ry:
            return False                                  # already joined
        if self.rank[rx] < self.rank[ry]:
            rx, ry = ry, rx
        self.parent[ry] = rx
        if self.rank[rx] == self.rank[ry]:
            self.rank[rx] += 1
        return True

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: N=6. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3), union(4,5), find(1), find(5).

  1. union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1.
  2. union(2,3): parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1.
  3. union(0,2): both rank 1 → parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Now group {0,1,2,3}.
  4. find(3): 3 → 2 → 0; compress so parent[3]=0, parent[2]=0. Returns 0.
  5. union(4,5): parent[5]=4. find(1)=0, find(5)=4 → different groups; final group count = 2.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(α(n)) per op (amortized) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Both optimizations matter — path compression alone or rank alone is O(log n); together you get α(n). Always do both.
  • Iterative find prevents recursion-depth blowups on huge inputs — two-pass: find the root, then re-walk to compress.
  • Union by size is equivalent to rank — pick one and stay consistent.
  • Per-group metadata (count, sum, etc.) is easy: maintain size[root] and update on union.
  • DSU doesn't support split — you can't undo a union without snapshots or a link-cut tree.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 547 Number of Provinces (Med) — connected components by unions.
  • 684 Redundant Connection (Med) — the edge whose endpoints are already joined.
  • 685 Redundant Connection II (Hard) — directed variant; in-degree + DSU.
  • 261 Graph Valid Tree (Med) — DSU rejects any cycle-forming edge.
  • 200 Number of Islands (Med) — DSU on grid cells.
  • 305 Number of Islands II (Hard) — DSU shines on dynamic adds.
  • 990 Satisfiability of Equality Equations (Med) — union equals, then verify not-equals.
  • 1319 Number of Operations to Make Network Connected (Med) — components minus 1.
  • 947 Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column (Med) — union by shared coord.
  • 721 Accounts Merge (Med) — DSU on emails, then collect groups.

docs.title: 06 — Kruskal MST
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position 6/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a weighted undirected graph, find a minimum spanning tree — a subset of edges that connects every node with the lowest total weight.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Greedy: sort edges by weight and add each one unless it would form a cycle. Cycle detection is exactly what Union-Find gives you in near-constant time. The invariant: the chosen edges always form a forest that is a subset of some MST. The exchange argument (cut property) proves it — at every step the cheapest edge crossing some cut must be in an MST, and we pick exactly such edges.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Sort the edges by weight ascending. Initialize DSU over V nodes. For each edge (u, v, w), if find(u) != find(v), union and add to the MST. Stop when you've added V−1 edges.

def kruskal(n, edges):
    edges.sort(key=lambda e: e[2])
    dsu = DSU(n)
    mst, total = [], 0
    for u, v, w in edges:
        if dsu.union(u, v):
            mst.append((u, v, w))
            total += w
            if len(mst) == n - 1:
                break
    return total, mst

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(E log E) space O(V + E)

The sort dominates; DSU adds α(V) per edge, negligible.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Disconnected graphs — Kruskal returns a minimum spanning forest; check len(mst) == V − 1 to confirm a spanning tree exists.
  • Duplicate-weight edges — any valid choice is optimal; the algorithm is correct under any deterministic tie-break.
  • Direction is ignored — MSTs are an undirected concept; if the input is directed, dedupe to undirected first.
  • Edge-list representation is required — Kruskal needs the edges, not the adjacency list. Prim is better when you only have adjacency.
  • Critical / pseudo-critical edges (LC 1489) — re-run Kruskal forcing each edge in or out to classify.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1135 Connecting Cities With Minimum Cost (Med) — the canonical MST problem.
  • 1584 Min Cost to Connect All Points (Med) — complete graph; Manhattan distances.
  • 1168 Optimize Water Distribution (Hard) — virtual-node trick.
  • 1489 Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST (Hard) — run Kruskal with/without each edge.
  • 1631 Path With Minimum Effort (Med) — MST-flavored on grid.
  • 1102 Path With Maximum Minimum Value (Med) — sort-descend variant.
  • 1697 Checking Existence of Edge Length Limited Paths (Hard) — offline queries + sorted edges.
  • 1724 Checking Existence of Edge Length Limited Paths II (Hard) — online version; needs persistent DSU.
  • 1462 Course Schedule IV (Med) — reachability via repeated union.
  • 778 Swim in Rising Water (Hard) — Kruskal-on-cells alternative to Dijkstra.

docs.title: 07 — Prim MST
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position ⅞

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a weighted undirected graph, find a minimum spanning tree — same goal as Kruskal, different growth strategy.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Grow the MST node by node. Start from any vertex; repeatedly add the cheapest edge that connects an in-tree node to an out-of-tree node. A min-heap of crossing edges makes this efficient. The invariant: the tree-so-far is always a subtree of some MST (cut property again). Prim is essentially Dijkstra where the key is the edge weight to the frontier, not the cumulative distance from a source.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Maintain a in_tree set and a min-heap of (edge_weight, neighbor) for edges crossing the cut. Pop the cheapest crossing edge; if its neighbor isn't in the tree yet, add it and push all of its outgoing edges.

import heapq

def prim(n, graph, start=0):
    in_tree = [False] * n
    heap = [(0, start)]
    total, taken = 0, 0
    while heap and taken < n:
        w, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if in_tree[u]:
            continue
        in_tree[u] = True
        total += w
        taken += 1
        for v, weight in graph[u]:
            if not in_tree[v]:
                heapq.heappush(heap, (weight, v))
    return total if taken == n else -1     # -1 if disconnected

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: V=4, adjacency {0: [(1,10), (2,6), (3,5)], 1: [(0,10), (3,15)], 2: [(0,6), (3,4)], 3: [(0,5), (1,15), (2,4)]}. Start = 0.

  1. Heap=[(0, 0)]. Pop (0, 0). Mark in-tree. Push (10, 1), (6, 2), (5, 3). total=0.
  2. Pop (5, 3). Mark in-tree. Push (15, 1), (4, 2). total=5.
  3. Pop (4, 2). Mark in-tree. Push (6, 0 — skipped) etc. total=9.
  4. Pop (6, 2): in-tree, skip. Pop (10, 1). Mark in-tree. total=19.
  5. Taken=V=4 → done. MST weight = 19.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
time O(E log V) space O(V + E)

With a Fibonacci heap, O(E + V log V). With an adjacency matrix and no heap, O(V²) — better for dense graphs.

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Skip in-tree on pop — like Dijkstra, you'll have stale heap entries. Discard if the target is already in the tree.
  • Disconnected graphs — Prim from one start only covers one component. Loop over un-marked starts to get the forest.
  • Adjacency-list representation is required — Prim works on neighbors; if you only have an edge list, Kruskal is more natural.
  • Dense vs sparse — Prim with a matrix is O(V²) (no heap). For E ≈ V², that beats E log V.
  • Tie-breaking — any choice among equal-cost crossing edges is optimal.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1584 Min Cost to Connect All Points (Med) — dense graph; Prim O(V²) shines.
  • 1135 Connecting Cities With Minimum Cost (Med) — same template as Kruskal.
  • 1489 Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST (Hard) — Prim works but Kruskal is cleaner.
  • 1168 Optimize Water Distribution (Hard) — virtual-node + Prim.
  • 1631 Path With Minimum Effort (Med) — Prim variant: minimize max edge.
  • 1102 Path With Maximum Minimum Value (Med) — reversed-key Prim.
  • 1697 Checking Existence of Edge Length Limited Paths (Hard) — DSU is cleaner; Prim possible.
  • 778 Swim in Rising Water (Hard) — minimax path; Dijkstra/Prim variant.
  • 1245 Tree Diameter (Med) — adjacent topic; two BFS pass.
  • 1462 Course Schedule IV (Med) — reachability comparison.

docs.title: 08 — Tarjan SCC
docs.text: Graph Algorithms • Position 8/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Find all strongly connected components (SCCs) of a directed graph — maximal sets of nodes where every pair has a path each way. The undirected sibling: find bridges (edges whose removal disconnects the graph).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: DFS assigns each node a discovery time disc[u]. We also track low[u] — the smallest disc reachable from u's DFS subtree via at most one back-edge. When low[u] == disc[u], u is the root of an SCC: nothing in its subtree could reach an ancestor, so the subtree (held in a stack) is exactly that SCC. The invariant: after DFS returns from u, low[u] is correct, and an unfinished node remains on the SCC stack iff it hasn't been emitted yet.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
DFS from every unvisited node. On entry, set disc[u] = low[u] = timer++ and push u on the stack. For each neighbor v: if unvisited, recurse and low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]); if v is on the stack, low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]). After exploring, if low[u] == disc[u], pop until u — that's an SCC.

def tarjan_scc(n, graph):
    disc = [-1] * n
    low = [0] * n
    on_stack = [False] * n
    stack, sccs = [], []
    timer = [0]

    def dfs(u):
        disc[u] = low[u] = timer[0]; timer[0] += 1
        stack.append(u); on_stack[u] = True
        for v in graph[u]:
            if disc[v] == -1:
                dfs(v)
                low[u] = min(low[u], low[v])
            elif on_stack[v]:
                low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v])
        if low[u] == disc[u]:
            comp = []
            while True:
                w = stack.pop(); on_stack[w] = False
                comp.append(w)
                if w == u:
                    break
            sccs.append(comp)

    for u in range(n):
        if disc[u] == -1:
            dfs(u)
    return sccs

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Example: V=5, edges 0→1, 1→2, 2→0, 1→3, 3→4.

  1. DFS(0): disc=0, low=0. Push 0. Visit 1 → disc=1, low=1. Push 1.
  2. From 1, visit 2: disc=2, low=2. Push 2. 2→0 is on-stack → low[2]=min(2, 0)=0. Back to 1: low[1]=min(1, 0)=0.
  3. From 1, visit 3: disc=3, low=3. Push 3. Visit 4: disc=4, low=4. Push 4. No outgoing → low[4]=disc[4] → pop SCC {4}.
  4. Back to 3: low[3]=min(3, 4)=3 → pop SCC {3}. Back to 1: low[1]=min(0, 3)=0.
  5. Back to 0: low[0]=min(0, 0)=0 → pop until 0 → SCC {2, 1, 0}. SCCs = [{4}, {3}, {0, 1, 2}].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(V + E) space O(V)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• on_stack is required — without it, you'd treat cross-edges into already-finished SCCs as back-edges and merge wrongly.
  • disc[v] vs low[v] on the back-edge branch — use disc[v] (Tarjan's specification); low[v] is wrong here even though it often happens to work.
  • Recursion depth — Python hits its limit on graphs with V ≈ 10⁴. Convert to an iterative DFS for big inputs.
  • For bridges (undirected), track parent and check low[v] > disc[u] on the edge u-v; don't go back to the parent edge.
  • Kosaraju (two DFS passes on G and reverse-G) is an alternative SCC algorithm — often easier to remember in an interview but slightly slower in constant factor.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1192 Critical Connections in a Network (Hard) — the canonical bridges problem.
  • 1568 Minimum Number of Days to Disconnect Island (Hard) — articulation points on grid.
  • 1489 Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST (Hard) — adjacent topic; Kruskal-based.
  • 802 Find Eventual Safe States (Med) — SCC + condensation reasoning.
  • 685 Redundant Connection II (Hard) — directed cycles; Tarjan helps.
  • 1559 Detect Cycles in 2D Grid (Med) — DFS with parent tracking.
  • 207 Course Schedule (Med) — cycle = non-trivial SCC.
  • 210 Course Schedule II (Med) — SCC on a DAG must all be singletons.
  • 947 Most Stones Removed (Med) — components via DSU; SCC overkill but instructive.
  • 928 Minimize Malware Spread II (Hard) — articulation-point flavored.

docs.title: DSA · 09 · Dynamic Programming
docs.text: Tabulation vs memoization — Fibonacci, knapsack, LIS, LCS, edit distance.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem asks for min / max / longest / shortest over a sequence of choices.
  • The problem asks to count the number of ways to reach a target, partition a set, or arrange items.
  • A yes/no feasibility question that has optimal substructure — the answer for size n is composed from answers for smaller sizes.
  • A naive recursion revisits the same subproblem many times (overlapping subproblems).
  • Greedy fails — local optima do not yield the global optimum, but the search space is still polynomial after deduplication.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Every DP follows the same four moves. First, identify the state — the minimal tuple of variables that fully describes a subproblem (e.g. (i, capacity) for knapsack, (i, j) for two-string DPs). Second, write the recurrence — express dp[state] in terms of strictly smaller states; this is where the decision at each step lives (take / skip, match / replace, jump 1 / jump 2). Third, pin down the base case — the smallest state whose answer is trivial (empty string, zero amount, single element). Fourth, choose direction: top-down memoization mirrors the recurrence and is easy to reason about; bottom-up tabulation fills a table in dependency order and is usually faster with less stack risk. Finally, if dp[i] only depends on a constant window of previous rows, roll the table down to O(1) or O(n) extra space.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01 Fibonacci

The simplest DP — 1D recurrence and rolling-2 space.

02 House Robber

Pick-or-skip with adjacency constraint.

03 Climbing Stairs

Counting ways with a finite step set.

04 Coin Change

Unbounded min-coins for a target amount.

05 Longest Increasing Subsequence

Patience sorting in O(n log n).

06 Longest Common Subsequence

The canonical 2D string DP.

07 Edit Distance

Levenshtein — insert, delete, replace.

08 0/1 Knapsack

Take or skip each item exactly once.

09 Unbounded Knapsack

Unlimited copies — loops swap order.

10 Word Break

String segmentation against a dictionary.

11 Palindrome Partitioning

Min cuts + palindrome interval table.

docs.title: DP problem-recognition cheat sheet
docs.text:
• 1D DP when state is a single index — Fibonacci, House Robber, Climbing Stairs, Coin Change, LIS, Word Break.
  • 2D DP when state involves two pointers or two dimensions — LCS, Edit Distance, knapsack (i, capacity), palindrome interval dp[i][j].
  • Knapsack family — outer loop choice matters. 0/1: items outer, capacity inner descending (so each item is used once). Unbounded: items outer, capacity inner ascending (reuse allowed). Combination Sum IV (permutation count): capacity outer, items inner.
  • LIS pattern — when the recurrence is dp[i] = max(dp[j]+1) over j < i with a predicate; if the predicate is "strictly less", patience sorting gives O(n log n).
  • Counting vs optimizing — counting uses += over transitions; optimizing uses min/max. Watch the initial value: 0 for sums, ∞ for min, -∞ for max.
  • Bitmask DP when n ≤ 20 and the state is "which subset have I used" — dp[mask] with 2^n states.
  • Interval DP — dp[i][j] depends on a split k between i and j; iterate by increasing length (matrix chain, palindrome partitioning, burst balloons).
  • Space optimization — if dp[i] only uses dp[i-1] (and maybe dp[i-2]), reduce 1D to two scalars; if a 2D table only uses the previous row, reduce to a 1D rolling array.

docs.title: 01 — Fibonacci — bottom-up tabulation
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 1/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Compute the n-th Fibonacci number where F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n ≥ 2.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The naive recursion fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) explodes into an exponential tree because the same subproblems repeat. DP fixes this by computing each F(i) exactly once and storing it. Since F(i) only ever looks at the two values immediately before it, we don't even need the full table — two scalars suffice. This is the cleanest example of "identify state, write recurrence, roll the array."

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i] = the i-th Fibonacci number.
  • Transition: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] for i ≥ 2.
  • Base case: dp[0] = 0, dp[1] = 1.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def fib(n: int) -> int:
    if n < 2:
        return n
    # rolling-2 space: prev2 = F(i-2), prev1 = F(i-1)
    prev2, prev1 = 0, 1
    for _ in range(2, n + 1):
        prev2, prev1 = prev1, prev1 + prev2
    return prev1

# Top-down memoization equivalent:
# from functools import cache
# @cache
# def fib(n): return n if n < 2 else fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For n = 6:

  1. Start with prev2 = 0 (F(0)), prev1 = 1 (F(1)).
  2. i = 2: new value 1 + 0 = 1. Shift → prev2 = 1, prev1 = 1.
  3. i = 3: new value 1 + 1 = 2. Shift → prev2 = 1, prev1 = 2.
  4. i = 4: new value 2 + 1 = 3. Shift → prev2 = 2, prev1 = 3.
  5. i = 5: new value 3 + 2 = 5. i = 6: new value 5 + 3 = 8. Return 8.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the base cases — n = 0 and n = 1 must short-circuit before the loop.
  • Off-by-one in the loop range; range(2, n + 1) runs exactly n - 1 iterations to land at F(n).
  • Using plain recursion without memoization — exponential time even for n = 40.
  • Integer overflow in languages with fixed-width ints — Fibonacci grows as φⁿ; for n > 90 use big integers.
  • Confusing 0-indexed and 1-indexed definitions; LeetCode 509 is 0-indexed.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 509. Fibonacci Number (Easy) — the textbook recurrence.
  • 1137. N-th Tribonacci Number (Easy) — extend the window to three.
  • 70. Climbing Stairs (Easy) — same recurrence in disguise.
  • 746. Min Cost Climbing Stairs (Easy) — minimize instead of count.
  • 198. House Robber (Medium) — pick-or-skip variant.
  • 213. House Robber II (Medium) — circular array twist.
  • 91. Decode Ways (Medium) — two-step recurrence with constraints.
  • 264. Ugly Number II (Medium) — three pointers feeding a sequence.
  • 873. Length of Longest Fibonacci Subsequence (Medium) — Fibonacci as a pattern in an array.
  • 1027. Longest Arithmetic Subsequence (Medium) — DP keyed by index and difference.

docs.title: 02 — House Robber
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 2/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an array of non-negative integers where nums[i] is the loot in house i, return the maximum total you can rob without robbing two adjacent houses.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: At each house you face a binary choice: rob it and skip the previous, or skip it and inherit the best result up to the previous house. That decision only depends on the two preceding answers, so this is a 1D DP with the same shape as Fibonacci — the difference is that we maximize instead of sum. The recurrence captures the adjacency constraint exactly.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i] = max loot achievable considering houses 0..i.
  • Transition: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).
  • Base case: dp[0] = nums[0], dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def rob(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    if not nums:
        return 0
    if len(nums) == 1:
        return nums[0]
    # rolling-2 scalars: prev2 = dp[i-2], prev1 = dp[i-1]
    prev2, prev1 = nums[0], max(nums[0], nums[1])
    for i in range(2, len(nums)):
        prev2, prev1 = prev1, max(prev1, prev2 + nums[i])
    return prev1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For nums = [2, 7, 9, 3, 1]:

  1. dp[0] = 2, dp[1] = max(2, 7) = 7.
  2. i = 2: max(7, 2 + 9) = 11. Best so far is to rob houses 0 and 2.
  3. i = 3: max(11, 7 + 3) = 11. Skipping house 3 wins.
  4. i = 4: max(11, 11 + 1) = 12. Robbing house 4 on top of {0, 2} adds 1.
  5. Answer: 12 (rob houses 0, 2, 4).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the dp[1] = max(nums[0], nums[1]) base — nums[1] alone is wrong when nums[0] is larger.
  • Updating prev2 and prev1 in the wrong order; use parallel assignment.
  • Confusing "adjacent" with "consecutive non-zero" — adjacency is purely about indices.
  • Negative numbers — the problem typically guarantees non-negative; if not, you may need to allow dp[i] = dp[i-1] to skip losses.
  • Missing the edge case len(nums) == 1.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 198. House Robber (Medium) — the canonical version.
  • 213. House Robber II (Medium) — circular street; run twice excluding either endpoint.
  • 337. House Robber III (Medium) — same idea on a binary tree with post-order DP.
  • 740. Delete and Earn (Medium) — bucket by value, then House Robber.
  • 256. Paint House (Medium) — three-color adjacency DP.
  • 265. Paint House II (Hard) — k colors, track best and second-best.
  • 276. Paint Fence (Medium) — no three same colors in a row.
  • 198. House Robber (Medium) — re-solve with memoization to compare paradigms.
  • 1027. Longest Arithmetic Subsequence (Medium) — DP keyed by index.
  • 2140. Solving Questions With Brainpower (Medium) — backward DP with skip distance.

docs.title: 03 — Climbing Stairs
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 3/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: You are climbing a staircase with n steps. Each move you may climb 1 or 2 steps. Return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: To land on step n your last move came from step n-1 (a single step) or step n-2 (a double step). The number of ways to reach n is therefore the sum of the ways to reach the two preceding steps — exactly the Fibonacci recurrence. The key reframing is counting paths rather than optimizing a value, which is why the transition uses += instead of max.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i] = number of distinct ways to reach step i.
  • Transition: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
  • Base case: dp[0] = 1 (one way to be at the start — do nothing), dp[1] = 1.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def climbStairs(n: int) -> int:
    if n <= 1:
        return 1
    prev2, prev1 = 1, 1  # dp[0], dp[1]
    for _ in range(2, n + 1):
        prev2, prev1 = prev1, prev1 + prev2
    return prev1

# Generalization: if step sizes come from a set S,
# dp[i] = sum(dp[i - s] for s in S if i - s >= 0)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For n = 5:

  1. dp[0] = 1, dp[1] = 1.
  2. dp[2] = dp[1] + dp[0] = 2. (Sequences: 1+1, 2.)
  3. dp[3] = dp[2] + dp[1] = 3. (1+1+1, 1+2, 2+1.)
  4. dp[4] = dp[3] + dp[2] = 5.
  5. dp[5] = dp[4] + dp[3] = 8. Return 8.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Defining dp[0] as 0 — it must be 1 for the counting to work (the empty sequence is one way).
  • Forgetting that 1+2 and 2+1 count as distinct sequences (order matters here).
  • Confusing this with the minimum cost variant (746) which uses min and includes a per-step cost.
  • Going recursive without memoization — exponential blow-up just like Fibonacci.
  • For larger step sets (e.g. {1, 2, 3} in Tribonacci), the window grows; widen the rolling buffer accordingly.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 70. Climbing Stairs (Easy) — the original.
  • 746. Min Cost Climbing Stairs (Easy) — optimize instead of count.
  • 1137. N-th Tribonacci Number (Easy) — three-step window.
  • 509. Fibonacci Number (Easy) — the underlying recurrence.
  • 91. Decode Ways (Medium) — same shape, but transitions are conditional.
  • 62. Unique Paths (Medium) — 2D extension of path counting.
  • 63. Unique Paths II (Medium) — with obstacles.
  • 64. Minimum Path Sum (Medium) — 2D optimization variant.
  • 198. House Robber (Medium) — different objective, identical state shape.
  • 213. House Robber II (Medium) — circular variant.

docs.title: 04 — Coin Change (min coins for amount)
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 4/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given coin denominations coins and a target amount, return the fewest coins needed to make up that amount. If it cannot be made, return -1. Each coin denomination may be used an unlimited number of times.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: For every amount a, the optimum is one more than the optimum of a - c for some coin c. We try every coin and take the smallest result. Because coins are reusable we sweep amounts in increasing order — by the time we evaluate dp[a], every dp[a - c] already reflects the best (possibly multi-coin) solution. The sentinel value amount + 1 (or inf) marks "unreachable" so min works without special-casing.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[a] = minimum coins needed to make amount a.
  • Transition: dp[a] = 1 + min(dp[a - c] for c in coins if a - c >= 0).
  • Base case: dp[0] = 0 (zero coins make amount 0). All other dp[a] start at inf.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def coinChange(coins: list[int], amount: int) -> int:
    INF = amount + 1  # safe sentinel; any real answer is at most `amount`
    dp = [0] + [INF] * amount
    for a in range(1, amount + 1):
        for c in coins:
            if c <= a:
                dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a - c] + 1)
    return dp[amount] if dp[amount] != INF else -1

# Top-down equivalent with memoization is also fine:
# @cache
# def best(a):
#     if a == 0: return 0
#     if a < 0:  return INF
#     return 1 + min(best(a - c) for c in coins)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For coins = [1, 2, 5], amount = 11:

  1. dp[0] = 0; everything else starts at 12.
  2. dp[1] = dp[0] + 1 = 1 (one 1).
  3. dp[2] = min(dp[1] + 1, dp[0] + 1) = 1 (one 2).
  4. Sweep up: dp[5] = 1, dp[6] = 2, ..., dp[10] = 2 (two 5s).
  5. dp[11] = min(dp[10] + 1, dp[9] + 1, dp[6] + 1) = 3 (5 + 5 + 1). Return 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(amount · len(coins)) space O(amount)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Using a greedy "always take the largest coin" — wrong for arbitrary denominations (e.g. [1, 3, 4] with amount 6).
  • Initializing dp with 0 everywhere — min then locks in 0 for unreachable amounts.
  • Confusing 322 (min count) with 518 (number of ways) — different recurrences and loop orders.
  • Forgetting the -1 return when dp[amount] stays at the sentinel.
  • For permutation-counting variants (377), the outer/inner loop order flips — capacity outside, coins inside.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 322. Coin Change (Medium) — the canonical min-coins problem.
  • 518. Coin Change 2 (Medium) — count distinct combinations.
  • 377. Combination Sum IV (Medium) — count permutations; flip loop order.
  • 416. Partition Equal Subset Sum (Medium) — 0/1 knapsack as a yes/no DP.
  • 494. Target Sum (Medium) — reformulate as subset-sum.
  • 879. Profitable Schemes (Hard) — two-dimension knapsack.
  • 983. Minimum Cost For Tickets (Medium) — DP over days with multiple ticket types.
  • 2742. Painting the Walls (Hard) — 0/1 knapsack reframed.
  • 279. Perfect Squares (Medium) — coins are square numbers.
  • 1449. Form Largest Integer With Digits That Add up to Target (Hard) — string-valued knapsack.

docs.title: 05 — Longest Increasing Subsequence (O(n log n))
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 5/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence — the elements need not be contiguous, but must appear in their original order.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The O(n²) DP is dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]. We can do better with patience sorting: maintain a tails array where tails[k] is the smallest possible tail of any increasing subsequence of length k + 1 seen so far. For each new number, binary-search the leftmost tail that is ≥ nums[i] and overwrite it; if none qualifies, append. The length of tails at the end equals the LIS length. The array tails itself is not the LIS, but its length is correct.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: tails[k] = smallest possible tail value of any length-(k+1) increasing subsequence.
  • Transition: for each x = nums[i], find j = lower_bound(tails, x). If j == len(tails), append x; otherwise set tails[j] = x.
  • Base case: tails = []. Every x either extends the longest run or improves a shorter run's tail.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
from bisect import bisect_left

def lengthOfLIS(nums: list[int]) -> int:
    tails: list[int] = []
    for x in nums:
        j = bisect_left(tails, x)   # strict increase → bisect_left
        if j == len(tails):
            tails.append(x)
        else:
            tails[j] = x
    return len(tails)

# For non-strict (allow equals), use bisect_right.
# For the O(n^2) DP that also recovers the actual subsequence:
#   dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for j < i if nums[j] < nums[i], default=0)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For nums = [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]:

  1. 10 → tails = [10].
  2. 9 → replace 10: tails = [9].
  3. 2 → replace 9: tails = [2]. 5 → append: tails = [2, 5].
  4. 3 → replace 5: tails = [2, 3]. 7 → append: tails = [2, 3, 7].
  5. 101 → append: tails = [2, 3, 7, 101]. 18 → replace 101: tails = [2, 3, 7, 18]. LIS length = 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Treating tails as the actual LIS — it is not; it's only correct as a length witness.
  • Using bisect_right when the problem demands strictly increasing — that admits equal values.
  • Forgetting that the binary search must be on the running tails array, not the input.
  • For 2D variants (Russian Doll Envelopes), sort by one dimension ascending and the other descending — the descending tiebreak prevents same-width envelopes from chaining.
  • The O(n²) DP is easier to extend (counting paths, reconstructing the sequence) — pick the right tool for the question.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 300. Longest Increasing Subsequence (Medium) — the canonical problem.
  • 354. Russian Doll Envelopes (Hard) — 2D LIS with clever sort.
  • 673. Number of Longest Increasing Subsequence (Medium) — count plus length.
  • 1671. Minimum Number of Removals to Make Mountain Array (Hard) — LIS from both ends.
  • 1964. Find the Longest Valid Obstacle Course at Each Position (Hard) — non-strict LIS prefixes.
  • 646. Maximum Length of Pair Chain (Medium) — interval-style LIS.
  • 491. Non-decreasing Subsequences (Medium) — enumerate rather than length.
  • 1626. Best Team With No Conflicts (Medium) — weighted LIS.
  • 368. Largest Divisible Subset (Medium) — LIS with a divisibility predicate.
  • 1027. Longest Arithmetic Subsequence (Medium) — DP keyed by (index, difference).

docs.title: 06 — Longest Common Subsequence
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 6/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given two strings s1 and s2, return the length of their longest common subsequence — a sequence of characters appearing in both, in order, but not necessarily contiguously.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Look at the last characters of the two prefixes s1[:i] and s2[:j]. If they match, that character extends an LCS of the shorter prefixes by 1. If they don't match, drop one character from either side and take the better answer. This produces the canonical 2D recurrence — the workhorse of string DP. LCS is also a template: edit distance, distinct subsequences, and palindromic subsequence (LCS of s and reverse(s)) all reuse this skeleton.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i][j] = length of LCS of s1[:i] and s2[:j].
  • Transition: if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1] then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; otherwise dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
  • Base case: dp[0][j] = dp[i][0] = 0 (empty prefix matches nothing).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def longestCommonSubsequence(s1: str, s2: str) -> int:
    n, m = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if s1[i - 1] == s2[j - 1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1
            else:
                dp[i][j] = max(dp[i - 1][j], dp[i][j - 1])
    return dp[n][m]

# Space-optimized: only the previous row is needed → O(min(n, m)) space.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For s1 = "abcde", s2 = "ace":

  1. Build (n+1) × (m+1) table of zeros.
  2. i = 1 ('a'): matches s2[0]='a' → dp[1][1] = 1. Carries right: dp[1][2] = 1, dp[1][3] = 1.
  3. i = 2 ('b'): no matches. Row becomes 0, 1, 1, 1 (inherit from above).
  4. i = 3 ('c'): matches s2[1]='c' → dp[3][2] = dp[2][1] + 1 = 2. dp[3][3] = 2.
  5. i = 4 ('d'): no matches, row stays 0, 1, 2, 2. i = 5 ('e'): matches s2[2]='e' → dp[5][3] = dp[4][2] + 1 = 3. Answer: 3 ("ace").

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · m) space O(n · m)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Off-by-one between string indices (0-based) and DP indices (1-based) — s1[i-1] is the character "ending at" dp[i].
  • Confusing subsequence (LCS, this problem) with substring (a different DP with dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 on match, else 0).
  • Forgetting the mismatch branch — without max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) you can't extend across gaps.
  • Trying to reconstruct the LCS using only one rolling row — you need the full table to backtrack.
  • For "longest palindromic subsequence" the trick is LCS(s, reverse(s)) — easy to overlook.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1143. Longest Common Subsequence (Medium) — the textbook problem.
  • 1035. Uncrossed Lines (Medium) — LCS in disguise.
  • 583. Delete Operation for Two Strings (Medium) — answer = n + m - 2 · LCS.
  • 712. Minimum ASCII Delete Sum for Two Strings (Medium) — weighted LCS.
  • 1312. Minimum Insertion Steps to Make a String Palindrome (Hard) — n - LPS, where LPS = LCS(s, reverse(s)).
  • 516. Longest Palindromic Subsequence (Medium) — LCS(s, reverse(s)) directly.
  • 72. Edit Distance (Hard) — extends the LCS table.
  • 718. Maximum Length of Repeated Subarray (Medium) — LCS but for substrings.
  • 392. Is Subsequence (Easy) — two-pointer warm-up.
  • 940. Distinct Subsequences II (Hard) — count distinct subsequences with DP.

docs.title: 07 — Edit Distance (Levenshtein)
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 7/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of single-character operations — insert, delete, or replace — needed to convert word1 into word2.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: This is LCS's optimization cousin. Each cell dp[i][j] asks: what is the cheapest way to convert the first i characters of word1 into the first j characters of word2? If the current characters already match, no operation is needed and we inherit dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise we pay 1 and pick the cheapest of three subproblems — insert (dp[i][j-1]), delete (dp[i-1][j]), or replace (dp[i-1][j-1]). The recurrence is symmetric and works for any pair of strings.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i][j] = min edits to convert word1[:i] into word2[:j].
  • Transition: if word1[i-1] == word2[j-1] then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
  • Base case: dp[i][0] = i (delete all of word1[:i]), dp[0][j] = j (insert all of word2[:j]).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def minDistance(word1: str, word2: str) -> int:
    n, m = len(word1), len(word2)
    dp = [[0] * (m + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
    for i in range(n + 1):
        dp[i][0] = i
    for j in range(m + 1):
        dp[0][j] = j
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        for j in range(1, m + 1):
            if word1[i - 1] == word2[j - 1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1]
            else:
                dp[i][j] = 1 + min(
                    dp[i - 1][j - 1],  # replace
                    dp[i - 1][j],      # delete
                    dp[i][j - 1],      # insert
                )
    return dp[n][m]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For word1 = "horse", word2 = "ros":

  1. Initialize: row 0 = [0,1,2,3] (insertions), column 0 = [0,1,2,3,4,5] (deletions).
  2. i=1 ('h'): no matches with 'r','o','s'. Row fills as 1, 2, 3, 3.
  3. i=2 ('o'): matches 'o' at j=2 → dp[2][2] = dp[1][1] = 2. Row: 2, 2, 2, 3.
  4. i=3 ('r'): matches 'r' at j=1 → dp[3][1] = dp[2][0] = 2. Row: 3, 2, 3, 3.
  5. Continue. Final cell dp[5][3] = 3 — replace 'h'→'r', delete 'e', delete 'r' (or equivalent edit script).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · m) space O(n · m)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting that base cases are not zero — they are the lengths of the non-empty prefix.
  • Mixing up which neighbor corresponds to which operation (insert, delete, replace) — write a small comment by each min argument.
  • Trying to roll to one row prematurely — you need dp[i-1][j-1] (top-left), so cache it in a scalar before overwriting.
  • Penalizing matches accidentally — when characters match, the cost should be dp[i-1][j-1], not dp[i-1][j-1] + 1.
  • For weighted edit distance (different costs for insert/delete/replace), multiply the recurrence by the appropriate weight; the structure stays the same.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 72. Edit Distance (Hard) — the canonical Levenshtein.
  • 583. Delete Operation for Two Strings (Medium) — only deletes allowed.
  • 161. One Edit Distance (Medium) — single-edit check; two-pointer suffices.
  • 712. Minimum ASCII Delete Sum (Medium) — weighted variant.
  • 583. Delete Operation (Medium) — practice with LCS framing.
  • 1216. Valid Palindrome III (Hard) — bounded edit distance to a palindrome.
  • 712. Minimum ASCII Delete Sum (Medium) — re-solve with rolling rows.
  • 132. Palindrome Partitioning II (Hard) — different problem, same min-DP flavor.
  • 97. Interleaving String (Medium) — 2D boolean DP on string indices.
  • 115. Distinct Subsequences (Hard) — counting variant of the LCS skeleton.

docs.title: 08 — 0/1 Knapsack
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 8/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given n items with weights w[i] and values v[i], and a knapsack of capacity W, choose a subset of items to maximize total value subject to the weight limit. Each item may be picked at most once.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: At item i you either take it (adding its value, spending its weight) or skip it. The full table dp[i][c] records the best value using only the first i items at capacity c. The decision is a max over those two branches. Because each item is considered once, the 1D space-optimized version must sweep capacity right to left — that prevents the same item from being counted twice (which would silently turn it into unbounded knapsack).

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[c] = max value achievable with capacity c after processing the items considered so far.
  • Transition: for each item (w, v), for c from W down to w: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c - w] + v).
  • Base case: dp[c] = 0 for all c before any item is processed.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def knapsack01(weights: list[int], values: list[int], W: int) -> int:
    dp = [0] * (W + 1)
    for w, v in zip(weights, values):
        # iterate descending so each item is used at most once
        for c in range(W, w - 1, -1):
            dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c - w] + v)
    return dp[W]

# 2D version (clearer but heavier):
# dp = [[0] * (W + 1) for _ in range(n + 1)]
# for i in 1..n, for c in 0..W:
#     dp[i][c] = dp[i-1][c]
#     if c >= w[i-1]:
#         dp[i][c] = max(dp[i][c], dp[i-1][c - w[i-1]] + v[i-1])

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For weights = [2, 3, 4, 5], values = [3, 4, 5, 6], W = 5:

  1. Start with dp = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0].
  2. Item (2, 3): descending c = 5..2 → dp[5] = max(0, dp[3] + 3) = 3, dp[4] = 3, dp[3] = 3, dp[2] = 3.
  3. Item (3, 4): dp[5] = max(3, dp[2] + 4) = 7 (items 1 and 2). dp[4] = max(3, dp[1] + 4) = 4. dp[3] = 4.
  4. Item (4, 5): dp[5] = max(7, dp[1] + 5) = 7. dp[4] = max(4, dp[0] + 5) = 5.
  5. Item (5, 6): dp[5] = max(7, dp[0] + 6) = 7. Final answer: 7 (take items with weights 2 and 3).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · W) space O(W)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Sweeping capacity ascending with the 1D array — this lets the same item be reused, silently solving unbounded knapsack.
  • Forgetting W is pseudo-polynomial in input size — exponential in the bit-length of W (NP-hard in the strong sense).
  • Mixing weights and values indices — keep a single (w, v) tuple iterator.
  • Adding items with weight 0 — they should always be taken, but the descending sweep range(W, -1, -1) still works; just be careful with the lower bound.
  • Trying to recover the chosen items from the 1D array — you need the full 2D table to backtrack.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 416. Partition Equal Subset Sum (Medium) — boolean knapsack with target = sum/2.
  • 494. Target Sum (Medium) — convert to subset-sum.
  • 879. Profitable Schemes (Hard) — two-dimension knapsack (people + profit).
  • 474. Ones and Zeroes (Medium) — capacity is a pair (m, n).
  • 1049. Last Stone Weight II (Medium) — minimize |sum1 - sum2| via knapsack.
  • 956. Tallest Billboard (Hard) — DP keyed on signed difference.
  • 1230. Toss Strange Coins (Medium) — probabilistic knapsack.
  • 2742. Painting the Walls (Hard) — knapsack with "free workers" reframing.
  • 1981. Minimize the Difference Between Target and Chosen Elements (Medium) — set-based knapsack.
  • 1235. Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling (Hard) — weighted interval scheduling, knapsack-like DP.

docs.title: 09 — Unbounded Knapsack
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 9/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given n item types with weights w[i] and values v[i], and a knapsack of capacity W, choose items to maximize total value. Each item type may be used any number of times (including zero).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The 0/1 knapsack recurrence flips its loop order to allow reuse. Concretely: when filling dp[c], we are allowed to also use the same item that was just considered, because there are infinitely many copies. To express this we sweep capacity left to right so dp[c - w] + v may already include item i itself. The same trick — outer items, inner capacity ascending — also produces Coin Change and Coin Change II.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[c] = max value achievable at capacity c (using any number of any item types considered so far).
  • Transition: for each item (w, v), for c from w up to W: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c - w] + v).
  • Base case: dp[0] = 0; all other dp[c] start at 0.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def unboundedKnapsack(weights: list[int], values: list[int], W: int) -> int:
    dp = [0] * (W + 1)
    for w, v in zip(weights, values):
        # iterate ascending so each item can be reused
        for c in range(w, W + 1):
            dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c - w] + v)
    return dp[W]

# Equivalent single-loop view:
# for c in 1..W:
#     for (w, v) in items:
#         if c >= w: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c - w] + v)
# Either order works for max/min; for counting permutations vs combinations
# the loop order DOES matter (see Coin Change vs Combination Sum IV).

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For weights = [2, 3, 4], values = [4, 5, 6], W = 8:

  1. Start dp = [0]*9.
  2. Item (2, 4) ascending: dp[2]=4, dp[3]=4, dp[4]=8, dp[5]=8, dp[6]=12, dp[7]=12, dp[8]=16.
  3. Item (3, 5): dp[3]=max(4, 0+5)=5, dp[4]=max(8, dp[1]+5)=8, dp[5]=max(8, dp[2]+5)=9, dp[6]=max(12, dp[3]+5)=12.
  4. dp[7]=max(12, dp[4]+5)=13, dp[8]=max(16, dp[5]+5)=16.
  5. Item (4, 6): dp[8]=max(16, dp[4]+6)=16. Best = 16 — take four copies of item 1.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n · W) space O(W)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Sweeping descending like 0/1 knapsack — that disables reuse and silently reverts to 0/1.
  • For Combination Sum IV (377), the outer loop must be capacity, inner loop coins, to count permutations. For Coin Change II (518), the outer loop must be coins, inner loop capacity, to count combinations.
  • Forgetting that ratio-greedy (highest value-per-weight) only solves the fractional knapsack — for integer unbounded knapsack you still need DP.
  • Items with weight 0 and positive value — infinite answer; reject or handle explicitly.
  • Mixing up "min coins" (min, sentinel inf) with "max value" (max, init 0).
Practice — LeetCode
  • 322. Coin Change (Medium) — min-coins unbounded knapsack.
  • 518. Coin Change 2 (Medium) — count combinations.
  • 377. Combination Sum IV (Medium) — count permutations (flip loop order).
  • 279. Perfect Squares (Medium) — squares as the "coins".
  • 983. Minimum Cost For Tickets (Medium) — unbounded ticket choices.
  • 1449. Form Largest Integer With Digits That Add up to Target (Hard) — string-valued knapsack.
  • 638. Shopping Offers (Medium) — bundles as bulk items.
  • 343. Integer Break (Medium) — partition with multiplicative score.
  • 871. Minimum Number of Refueling Stops (Hard) — knapsack-like DP plus heap.
  • 1306. Jump Game III (Medium) — reachability flavor (BFS or DP).

docs.title: 10 — Word Break
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 10/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a string s and a dictionary wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Let dp[i] mean "the prefix s[:i] can be segmented." For dp[i] to be true, some split point j < i must satisfy dp[j] == true and s[j:i] is a dictionary word. Sweep i left to right and try every j. Dictionary membership is a set lookup, so each split test is O(L) for hashing the substring. The recurrence is a Boolean OR — once any split works, the prefix is reachable.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: dp[i] = True if s[:i] is segmentable into dictionary words.
  • Transition: dp[i] = any(dp[j] and s[j:i] in dict for j in range(i)).
  • Base case: dp[0] = True (the empty prefix is trivially segmentable).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def wordBreak(s: str, wordDict: list[str]) -> bool:
    words = set(wordDict)
    max_len = max((len(w) for w in words), default=0)
    n = len(s)
    dp = [False] * (n + 1)
    dp[0] = True
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        # only look back as far as the longest dictionary word
        start = max(0, i - max_len)
        for j in range(start, i):
            if dp[j] and s[j:i] in words:
                dp[i] = True
                break
    return dp[n]

# A Trie over wordDict lets you walk forward from each j and prune
# whenever a prefix has no extensions — useful when wordDict is large.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For s = "leetcode", wordDict = ["leet", "code"]:

  1. dp[0] = True.
  2. i = 1..3 ("l", "le", "lee"): no dictionary word matches; dp[i] = False.
  3. i = 4 ("leet"): j = 0, dp[0] = True and s[0:4] = "leet" ∈ dict → dp[4] = True.
  4. i = 5..7: no matches.
  5. i = 8 ("code"): j = 4, dp[4] = True and s[4:8] = "code" ∈ dict → dp[8] = True. Return True.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n² · L) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting dp[0] = True — the empty prefix is the seed that makes the recurrence fire.
  • Using a list instead of a set for wordDict — degrades each lookup from O(L) to O(N · L).
  • Skipping the max_len cap — without it, you scan up to O(n) per i even when the dictionary words are short, multiplying by O(L) for hashing.
  • Confusing 139 (Boolean) with 140 (enumerate all segmentations) — 140 needs backtracking and may be exponential.
  • Substring slicing in tight loops — for huge s, build a Trie and advance pointers instead of allocating substrings.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 139. Word Break (Medium) — the canonical Boolean DP.
  • 140. Word Break II (Hard) — enumerate all segmentations.
  • 472. Concatenated Words (Hard) — Word Break per word in the input list.
  • 91. Decode Ways (Medium) — same shape with numeric constraints.
  • 132. Palindrome Partitioning II (Hard) — segment by palindromes; min cuts.
  • 343. Integer Break (Medium) — partition an integer for max product.
  • 808. Soup Servings (Medium) — probabilistic DP with memoization.
  • 152. Maximum Product Subarray (Medium) — DP carrying min and max simultaneously.
  • 96. Unique Binary Search Trees (Medium) — Catalan recurrence.
  • 95. Unique Binary Search Trees II (Medium) — enumerate structures, not just count.

docs.title: 11 — Palindrome Partitioning
docs.text: Dynamic Programming • Position 11/11

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a string s, return the minimum number of cuts needed to partition s so that every part is a palindrome. (Variant 131 asks to enumerate all such partitions; 132 asks for the min cuts.)

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Two DPs collaborate. First, precompute a Boolean table pal[i][j] — is s[i..j] a palindrome? — using the interval-expansion recurrence pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (j - i < 2 or pal[i+1][j-1]). Second, define cuts[i] as the minimum cuts needed for the prefix s[:i+1]. For each i, try every j ≤ i such that s[j..i] is a palindrome; that piece costs cuts[j-1] + 1 cuts (or 0 if j == 0). Take the min.

docs.title: State & recurrence
docs.text:
• State: pal[i][j] ∈ {0, 1} = is s[i..j] a palindrome? cuts[i] = min cuts for s[0..i].
  • Transition: pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (j - i < 2 or pal[i+1][j-1]). cuts[i] = 0 if pal[0][i], else min(cuts[j-1] + 1) over all j ∈ [1, i] with pal[j][i].
  • Base case: pal[i][i] = True. cuts[-1] = -1 (conceptually, no cut needed before the empty prefix).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def minCut(s: str) -> int:
    n = len(s)
    pal = [[False] * n for _ in range(n)]
    # length-1 and length-2 palindromes
    for i in range(n):
        pal[i][i] = True
    for i in range(n - 1):
        pal[i][i + 1] = s[i] == s[i + 1]
    # longer lengths
    for length in range(3, n + 1):
        for i in range(n - length + 1):
            j = i + length - 1
            pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and pal[i + 1][j - 1]
    # cuts DP
    cuts = [0] * n
    for i in range(n):
        if pal[0][i]:
            cuts[i] = 0
            continue
        best = i  # worst case: cut between every character
        for j in range(1, i + 1):
            if pal[j][i]:
                best = min(best, cuts[j - 1] + 1)
        cuts[i] = best
    return cuts[n - 1]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
For s = "aab":

  1. Build pal: pal[0][0]=pal[1][1]=pal[2][2]=True; pal[0][1]=True ("aa"); pal[1][2]=False ("ab"); pal[0][2]=False ("aab").
  2. cuts[0]: pal[0][0] is true → cuts[0] = 0.
  3. cuts[1]: pal[0][1] is true ("aa") → cuts[1] = 0.
  4. cuts[2]: pal[0][2] is false. Try j=1: pal[1][2] false. Try j=2: pal[2][2] true → cuts[1] + 1 = 1.
  5. Final answer: cuts[2] = 1. One cut between "aa" and "b".

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n²) space O(n²)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Building pal in the wrong order — you must fill shorter intervals before longer ones, because pal[i][j] depends on pal[i+1][j-1].
  • Forgetting the length-2 base (s[i] == s[i+1]) — without it, pal[i+1][j-1] for j = i + 1 reads an uninitialized cell.
  • Confusing 131 (enumerate all partitions, classic backtracking with palindrome check) with 132 (min cuts, two-DP solution).
  • Forgetting to short-circuit cuts[i] = 0 when the whole prefix is already a palindrome.
  • Using Manacher's algorithm prematurely — the O(n²) DP is plenty fast for typical constraints and far easier to write correctly.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 131. Palindrome Partitioning (Medium) — enumerate all partitions via backtracking + palindrome check.
  • 132. Palindrome Partitioning II (Hard) — the canonical min-cuts DP.
  • 1278. Palindrome Partitioning III (Hard) — exactly k parts, with character-change cost.
  • 1745. Palindrome Partitioning IV (Hard) — split into exactly three palindromes.
  • 5. Longest Palindromic Substring (Medium) — reuses the `pal[i][j]` table.
  • 647. Palindromic Substrings (Medium) — count all palindromic substrings.
  • 516. Longest Palindromic Subsequence (Medium) — interval DP on subsequences.
  • 1312. Minimum Insertion Steps to Make a String Palindrome (Hard) — n - LPS.
  • 1771. Maximize Palindrome Length From Subsequences (Hard) — palindrome over a concatenation.
  • 730. Count Different Palindromic Subsequences (Hard) — distinct palindromic subsequences modulo prime.

docs.title: DSA · 10 · Greedy
docs.text: Local choice → global optimum when an exchange argument holds.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem asks for a "minimum/maximum X with constraints" and a sort order makes the right choice obvious.
  • Interval problems: pick the one that ends first, the one that starts latest, the one that covers the most.
  • Jump-style problems where you track the farthest you can reach and ratchet a frontier forward.
  • Scheduling, partitioning, and packing where you can prove "swapping in the greedy choice never makes the answer worse."
  • Huffman-like constructions where repeatedly taking the two smallest elements rebuilds an optimal tree.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Sort the input by the right key — earliest finish time, smallest weight, longest reach — and sweep once. Maintain a tiny piece of state (a running frontier, a current end, a heap of pending items) and commit to the greedy choice without looking back. The hard work isn't the loop; it's the exchange argument that says "for any optimal solution that disagrees with my choice, I can swap in my choice and not get worse." When that argument doesn't hold (e.g., coin change with arbitrary denominations), greedy silently lies — fall back to DP.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Activity Selection

Earliest finish time wins.

02Jump Game

Track the farthest reachable index.

03Gas Station

Reset start on every deficit.

04Huffman Coding

Repeatedly merge the two smallest.

05Interval Merging

Sort by start, extend the active end.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: "Pick the interval that ends earliest" solves activity selection, arrow shots, and non-overlap counts — they're the same problem with different objectives. Jump-style problems (Jump Game I/II, Video Stitching, Tap Watering) all reduce to "what's the farthest I can reach from anywhere in the current frontier?" Huffman generalizes to any "merge two smallest" cost-minimization (connect sticks, halve array sum). When a greedy proof fails, it usually fails because the input has a hidden dependency that DP would catch — e.g., gas station works greedy because the total-fuel test guarantees a solution exists.

docs.title: 01 — Activity Selection
docs.text: Greedy • Position ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given intervals, pick the maximum number that don't overlap.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Sort by finish time. Always commit to the activity that ends earliest among those still available — it leaves the most room on the right for everything that follows.
