The exchange argument: any optimal schedule whose first pick ends later than the greedy pick can swap that first pick for the greedy one without losing any later activities, because the greedy pick frees up strictly more future time. Iterate the argument and the greedy schedule is at least as long as any optimal one.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Sort by end. Walk left to right; keep an activity if its start is ≥ the last kept end.

def max_activities(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # by finish time
    count, last_end = 0, float("-inf")
    for s, e in intervals:
        if s >= last_end:
            count += 1
            last_end = e
    return count

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Sorting by start time instead of end — gives wrong answer on small inputs like [(1,10),(2,3),(4,5)].
  • Off-by-one on touching intervals: decide upfront whether [1,3) and [3,5) count as overlapping. LC 435 treats touching as not overlapping; LC 252/253 may differ.
  • Forgetting that the count is what matters, not the specific schedule — many optima exist.
  • Greedy by shortest duration is a famous wrong heuristic; counterexample: [(0,5),(4,6),(5,10)].
  • For weighted activity selection (each interval has a value), greedy fails — use DP with binary search.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 435 Non-overlapping Intervals (Med) — same problem, minimize removals = n − greedy count.
  • 452 Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons (Med) — count of "lanes" = activity selection on ends.
  • 56 Merge Intervals (Med) — sort by start, extend or push.
  • 1288 Remove Covered Intervals (Med) — sort by start asc, end desc; track running max end.
  • 1353 Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended (Med) — heap of ends per day.
  • 1024 Video Stitching (Med) — sort by start, jump farthest end within reach.
  • 757 Set Intersection Size At Least Two (Hard) — variant requiring two points per interval.
  • 646 Maximum Length of Pair Chain (Med) — direct restatement of activity selection.
  • 763 Partition Labels (Med) — last-occurrence map + extend the active end.
  • 134 Gas Station (Med) — greedy reset on deficit (sibling pattern).

docs.title: 02 — Jump Game (greedy farthest)
docs.text: Greedy • Position ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given nums where nums[i] is the max jump from index i, can you reach the last index? (And for Jump Game II, in how few jumps?)

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: You don't need to enumerate paths — just track the farthest index reachable so far. Sweep left to right; at each i ≤ reach, update reach = max(reach, i + nums[i]). If reach ever stalls at an index < n-1 while i catches up to it, you're stuck. For Jump Game II, the trick is to think in levels: maintain a current jump's right boundary; when i hits it, you must have used another jump, and the new boundary is the farthest reached so far. This is BFS on indices without the queue.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def can_jump(nums):
    reach = 0
    for i, v in enumerate(nums):
        if i > reach:
            return False
        reach = max(reach, i + v)
    return True

def jump_ii(nums):
    jumps = end = farthest = 0
    for i in range(len(nums) - 1):
        farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
        if i == end:                # consumed current jump
            jumps += 1
            end = farthest
    return jumps

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. nums = [2,3,1,1,4]. reach = 0.
  2. i=0, v=2 → reach = max(0, 0+2) = 2.
  3. i=1, v=3 → reach = max(2, 1+3) = 4. Already covers last index.
  4. Loop completes without i > reach. Return True.
  5. Jump II on same array: at i=0, farthest=2, end=0 → jump (jumps=1, end=2). At i=1, farthest=4. At i=2, end consumed → jump (jumps=2, end=4). Answer = 2.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Updating reach for indices i > reach — you can't stand there, so you can't jump from there.
  • For Jump Game II, looping to n instead of n-1: the last index doesn't trigger another jump.
  • Off-by-one when nums[i] = 0 — you stall, not fail, unless i == reach < n-1.
  • Confusing "max jump = nums[i]" with "exact jump" — Jump Game IV uses different rules.
  • Trying DP first; the O(n²) DP times out on large inputs where greedy is the intended solution.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 55 Jump Game (Med) — canonical reachability sweep.
  • 45 Jump Game II (Med) — minimum jumps via BFS-by-level.
  • 1306 Jump Game III (Med) — graph reachability with ±nums[i].
  • 1340 Jump Game V (Hard) — DP with memo, not greedy.
  • 1696 Jump Game VI (Med) — monotonic deque on DP.
  • 134 Gas Station (Med) — circular variant of the frontier idea.
  • 871 Minimum Number of Refueling Stops (Hard) — heap of skipped stations.
  • 1024 Video Stitching (Med) — Jump Game II on intervals.
  • 1326 Minimum Number of Taps to Open to Water a Garden (Hard) — intervals → Jump Game II.
  • 763 Partition Labels (Med) — last-occurrence farthest pointer.

docs.title: 03 — Gas Station (circular tour)
docs.text: Greedy • Position ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: At station i you gain gas[i] and pay cost[i] to drive to i+1 (circular). Return the starting index that lets you complete the loop, or -1.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Two facts unlock the O(n) greedy. First, a solution exists iff sum(gas) ≥ sum(cost) — otherwise no start can possibly work. Second, if you start at i and run out of gas at j, then no station in [i, j] can be a valid start: each of those would have arrived at j with even less fuel than you did. So you reset the candidate start to j+1 and keep sweeping. One pass, no backtracking — the exchange argument is "any failed prefix is forever unusable."

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def can_complete(gas, cost):
    if sum(gas) < sum(cost):
        return -1
    tank, start = 0, 0
    for i in range(len(gas)):
        tank += gas[i] - cost[i]
        if tank < 0:           # i is unreachable from start
            start, tank = i + 1, 0
    return start

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. gas = [1,2,3,4,5], cost = [3,4,5,1,2]. Total gas 15, total cost 15 → solution exists.
  2. i=0: tank = 1−3 = −2. Reset: start=1, tank=0.
  3. i=1: tank = 2−4 = −2. Reset: start=2, tank=0.
  4. i=2: tank = 3−5 = −2. Reset: start=3, tank=0.
  5. i=3: tank = 4−1 = 3. i=4: tank = 3 + 5−2 = 6. Loop ends. Answer = 3.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the global feasibility check — without it, you might return a "start" that still fails.
  • Trying to simulate from every station — O(n²) and unnecessary.
  • Resetting tank to gas[i+1] - cost[i+1] instead of 0 — you advance start past the failure first.
  • Misreading the cost: cost[i] is from i to i+1, not from i-1 to i.
  • Assuming uniqueness — the problem guarantees a unique answer when one exists, but writing code that depends on uniqueness can break on variants.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 134 Gas Station (Med) — canonical problem.
  • 871 Minimum Number of Refueling Stops (Hard) — heap of skipped tanks.
  • 55 Jump Game (Med) — sibling frontier-sweep pattern.
  • 45 Jump Game II (Med) — minimum-step variant.
  • 763 Partition Labels (Med) — running deficit-style sweep.
  • 1326 Minimum Number of Taps to Open to Water a Garden (Hard) — frontier on intervals.
  • 1024 Video Stitching (Med) — Jump Game II on segments.
  • 1306 Jump Game III (Med) — BFS reachability.
  • 1696 Jump Game VI (Med) — DP + monotonic deque.
  • 1340 Jump Game V (Hard) — DP with sorted indices.

docs.title: 04 — Huffman Coding
docs.text: Greedy • Position ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given symbol frequencies, build a prefix-free binary code that minimizes total encoded length: sum(freq[c] · depth[c]).

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Two observations drive the proof. (1) The two least-frequent symbols must be siblings at the deepest level — if not, swap them with whatever's down there and the total weighted depth strictly improves. (2) After merging those two into a single "super-symbol" with frequency = sum, the residual problem is the same shape on one fewer leaf. So a min-heap of frequencies, repeatedly extracting two and pushing their sum, builds an optimal tree from the leaves up. The shape of the tree records the code; the algorithm itself never tracks bits.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
import heapq

def huffman_cost(freqs):
    heap = list(freqs)
    heapq.heapify(heap)
    total = 0
    while len(heap) > 1:
        a = heapq.heappop(heap)
        b = heapq.heappop(heap)
        total += a + b              # contributes to depth of both
        heapq.heappush(heap, a + b)
    return total

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Frequencies [5, 9, 12, 13, 16, 45]. Heap initialized.
  2. Pop 5, 9 → merge into 14 (cost += 14). Heap: [12, 13, 14, 16, 45].
  3. Pop 12, 13 → merge into 25 (cost += 25). Heap: [14, 16, 25, 45].
  4. Pop 14, 16 → merge into 30 (cost += 30). Heap: [25, 30, 45]. Pop 25, 30 → 55 (cost += 55). Heap: [45, 55].
  5. Pop 45, 55 → 100 (cost += 100). Total weighted path length = 224.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Pushing the sum before popping the second smallest — you'll merge a fresh sum with itself.
  • Using a max-heap by accident (negation flip) — Python's heapq is min by default; double-check Java's PriorityQueue reverse comparator.
  • For ties, the tree shape isn't unique but the cost is — don't assert specific codes in tests.
  • Single-symbol input: cost is 0 (or 1 if the problem demands at least one bit per symbol — check the spec).
  • Forgetting that "merging two smallest" generalizes — LC 1167, 2208, 2336 all use the same heap loop with different per-step costs.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 1167 Minimum Cost to Connect Sticks (Med) — Huffman in disguise.
  • 2233 Maximum Product After K Increments (Med) — min-heap increments.
  • 1675 Minimize Deviation in Array (Hard) — max-heap halving.
  • 2208 Minimum Operations to Halve Array Sum (Med) — max-heap greedy halve.
  • 2336 Smallest Number in Infinite Set (Med) — heap + set.
  • 1054 Distant Barcodes (Med) — max-heap by count.
  • 358 Rearrange String k Distance Apart (Hard) — heap + cooldown queue.
  • 1405 Longest Happy String (Med) — max-heap by remaining count.
  • 621 Task Scheduler (Med) — max-heap with idle slots.
  • 295 Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — dual-heap pattern.

docs.title: 05 — Interval Merging
docs.text: Greedy • Position 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given intervals, merge all overlapping ones into the smallest set of disjoint intervals that covers the same points.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Sort by start time. Sweep left to right keeping one "active" interval [s, e]. For each new [s', e']: if s' ≤ e, they overlap — extend e = max(e, e'). Otherwise the active interval is done; push it and start a new one with [s', e']. The proof: after sorting by start, anything that overlaps the active interval must overlap it on the left, so a single "running end" is all you need. The same scan trivially answers room-count (max active at once), non-overlap count, and intersection enumeration with sibling variants.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def merge(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    out = []
    for s, e in intervals:
        if out and s <= out[-1][1]:
            out[-1][1] = max(out[-1][1], e)
        else:
            out.append([s, e])
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Input [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Already sorted by start.
  2. Push [1,3]. Active = [1,3].
  3. [2,6]: 2 ≤ 3 → extend end to max(3,6) = 6. Active = [1,6].
  4. [8,10]: 8 > 6 → push [8,10]. Active = [8,10].
  5. [15,18]: 15 > 10 → push [15,18]. Result: [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Sorting by end works for activity selection but breaks merging — you'll miss overlaps that end before a later-starting longer one.
  • Touching vs overlapping: [1,3] and [3,5] — decide whether to merge based on the problem statement (LC 56 merges them).
  • For "meeting rooms II", don't try to merge — use a min-heap of end times instead.
  • For inserting a new interval (LC 57), don't re-sort; do a three-phase sweep (before, overlap-and-extend, after).
  • Mutating the input list while iterating — make out separate or work on indices.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 56 Merge Intervals (Med) — canonical sweep.
  • 57 Insert Interval (Med) — three-phase variant.
  • 252 Meeting Rooms (Easy) — sort and check adjacent overlaps.
  • 253 Meeting Rooms II (Med) — heap of end times.
  • 435 Non-overlapping Intervals (Med) — sort by end, count keeps.
  • 763 Partition Labels (Med) — last-occurrence map → merge.
  • 986 Interval List Intersections (Med) — two-pointer on two sorted lists.
  • 759 Employee Free Time (Hard) — merge, then gaps.
  • 1851 Minimum Interval to Include Each Query (Hard) — offline sort + heap.
  • 2008 Maximum Earnings From Taxi (Med) — DP on sorted intervals.

docs.title: DSA · 11 · Trie
docs.text: Prefix trees for autocomplete, word dictionaries, multi-pattern search.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• Many strings share prefixes and you want one structure that indexes them all.
  • Queries are by prefix rather than exact match — autocomplete, "starts with X", suggestion lists.
  • You have a dictionary of words and a grid/stream to scan for all matches simultaneously (otherwise it's N × M substring checks).
  • You need wildcard matching (.) over a fixed alphabet — branch the search at the wildcard step.
  • XOR-maximization problems on integers — a binary trie of bits is the secret weapon.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Every node holds an array (or hashmap) of children, indexed by character, plus a flag for "a word ends here." Insertion walks the string and creates nodes on demand; search walks and asks "does the path exist?"; startsWith is the same as search but doesn't require the end flag. Once you have this skeleton, every variant — wildcards, weighted suggestions, grid DFS — just adds a small piece of per-node state (a count, a top-K cache, the word string) without changing the traversal.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Insert / Search

The three core operations.

02Word Dictionary

DFS at every '.' wildcard.

03Word Search II

Trie + grid DFS, prune dead branches.

04Autocomplete

Cache top-K at each node.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Binary tries (32-level trees over bit positions) solve "max XOR pair" in O(n · 32). Suffix tries / suffix arrays generalize to substring queries. For huge alphabets, swap fixed-size arrays for hash maps. When the dictionary is static, you can compress single-child chains into a "radix tree" for less memory, but the textbook trie is almost always fast enough. The Aho-Corasick automaton is "trie + failure links" — the next step up for multi-pattern matching at scale.

docs.title: 01 — Trie insert / search / startsWith
docs.text: Trie • Position ¼

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Implement insert(word), search(word), startsWith(prefix) over a dictionary of lowercase strings.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A trie collapses shared prefixes into one branch. Every node owns a 26-slot array (one per letter) and an is_end flag. Insert walks the string, creating children on demand. Search walks the same path and returns node.is_end at the final step. startsWith is identical to search except it ignores is_end — reaching the last character is enough. The win is structural: N words with a shared K-character prefix cost O(K + sum of suffix lengths) total, not O(N·K).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
class Trie:
    def __init__(self):
        self.kids = {}
        self.end = False

    def insert(self, word):
        node = self
        for ch in word:
            node = node.kids.setdefault(ch, Trie())
        node.end = True

    def _walk(self, s):
        node = self
        for ch in s:
            node = node.kids.get(ch)
            if node is None: return None
        return node

    def search(self, word):       return bool((n := self._walk(word)) and n.end)
    def startsWith(self, prefix): return self._walk(prefix) is not None

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Empty root. Insert "cat": create chain c → a → t, mark t.end = True.
  2. Insert "car": walk c → a (exists), create r child, mark r.end = True.
  3. Insert "cap": walk c → a, create p child, mark p.end = True. Node 'a' now has three children.
  4. search("car") walks c → a → r, sees end=True. Returns True.
  5. search("ca") walks c → a, sees end=False. Returns False. startsWith("ca") returns True.

The companion trie-search visualization shows the same tree being traversed — search/startsWith re-use the walk; only the terminal check differs.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(L) per op space O(Σ L)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting is_end and letting search("ca") return True after inserting "cat".
  • Using a 26-slot array when input may contain uppercase/digits — switch to a hashmap.
  • Storing the full word at every node (wastes memory); store it only at end nodes if you need to retrieve it.
  • Iterative is cleaner than recursive for insert/search — saves stack frames on long strings.
  • For deletion, you need a count of descendants or a children_count field; naive "unmark end" leaks nodes.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 208 Implement Trie (Med) — canonical problem.
  • 211 Design Add and Search Words Data Structure (Med) — adds '.' wildcard.
  • 648 Replace Words (Med) — find shortest prefix in dictionary.
  • 676 Implement Magic Dictionary (Med) — exactly-one mismatch search.
  • 720 Longest Word in Dictionary (Med) — DFS picking lexicographically smallest.
  • 1268 Search Suggestions System (Med) — trie + cached top-3.
  • 14 Longest Common Prefix (Easy) — trivial via trie walk.
  • 421 Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array (Med) — binary trie.
  • 642 Design Search Autocomplete System (Hard) — weighted trie + heap.
  • 745 Prefix and Suffix Search (Hard) — pair-encoded trie.

docs.title: 02 — Word Dictionary with '.' wildcard
docs.text: Trie • Position 2/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Add words to a dictionary, then search where . matches any single character. search("b.d") over {bad, dad} returns True.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A normal trie can't handle wildcards iteratively because at . you must explore every child. Switch the search to DFS: at a regular character take the single matching child; at . recurse into all children. The branching factor is 26 at every dot, so a word with w wildcards costs O(26^w · L). In practice that's fine because most queries have ≤ 2 dots and the trie prunes hard — any branch whose child is missing dies immediately.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
class WordDictionary:
    def __init__(self):
        self.kids = {}
        self.end = False

    def addWord(self, w):
        node = self
        for ch in w:
            node = node.kids.setdefault(ch, WordDictionary())
        node.end = True

    def search(self, w, node=None):
        node = node or self
        for i, ch in enumerate(w):
            if ch == ".":
                return any(self.search(w[i+1:], c) for c in node.kids.values())
            node = node.kids.get(ch)
            if node is None: return False
        return node.end

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Add "bad", "dad", "mad". Root has three children: b, d, m, each leading to a → d (end).
  2. search("pad"): root.kids['p'] is None → return False.
  3. search("bad"): walk b → a → d, hits end → True.
  4. search(".ad"): at ., recurse on "ad" from each of b, d, m. Each branch walks a → d and finds end. Returns True.
  5. search("b.."): at b, normal step. At first ., recurse "." from 'a'. From 'a', at second '.', recurse "" from 'd' — and d.end == True. Returns True.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(26^w · L) space O(Σ L)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Slicing w[i+1:] is convenient but allocates — pass i+1 as an index for tighter inner loops.
  • Returning True as soon as you enter a wildcard branch, instead of recursing into the remaining suffix.
  • Forgetting to check node.end at the end of the walk — search("ba") over {bad} must return False.
  • Using a 26-slot array when problems allow . to match any character — . itself is not stored.
  • Confusing ? (regex one char) with . (LC convention) and "*" (zero or more). For LC 10/44 use DP, not trie.
Practice — LeetCode

  • 1166 Design File System (Med) — path-as-trie.
  • 588 Design In-Memory File System (Hard) — tree of files/dirs.
  • 642 Design Search Autocomplete System (Hard) — weighted + streaming.
  • 745 Prefix and Suffix Search (Hard) — pair-encoded keys.
  • 1032 Stream of Characters (Hard) — reverse trie over a stream.
  • 1268 Search Suggestions System (Med) — top-K per node.

  • 425 Word Squares (Hard) — prefix-trie backtracking.

docs.title: 03 — Word Search II (trie + grid DFS)
docs.text: Trie • Position ¾

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a grid of letters and a list of words, return every word that can be spelled by a path of adjacent (4-dir) cells without revisiting any cell.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Running Word Search I once per word is O(W · 4^L). Instead, build a trie from all words and run a single DFS from every cell, carrying the current trie node alongside the path. At each step you check whether the current letter is a child of the node — if not, prune instantly. When you hit node.end, record the word and clear the end-flag (or null the leaf) to avoid duplicates. Add one more optimization: when a trie node has no children left, splice it out so future paths bail earlier. This is the difference between "passes LC tests" and "passes in 50 ms."

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def findWords(board, words):
    root = {}
    for w in words:                  # build trie
        node = root
        for ch in w: node = node.setdefault(ch, {})
        node["$"] = w                # word at terminal

    R, C, out = len(board), len(board[0]), []

    def dfs(r, c, node):
        ch = board[r][c]
        nxt = node.get(ch)
        if not nxt: return
        if "$" in nxt:
            out.append(nxt.pop("$"))  # dedupe
        board[r][c] = "#"
        for dr, dc in ((1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)):
            nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc
            if 0 <= nr < R and 0 <= nc < C and board[nr][nc] != "#":
                dfs(nr, nc, nxt)
        board[r][c] = ch
        if not nxt: node.pop(ch)     # prune dead branch

    for r in range(R):
        for c in range(C):
            dfs(r, c, root)
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Board [["o","a","a","n"],["e","t","a","e"],["i","h","k","r"],["i","f","l","v"]], words ["oath","pea","eat","rain"].
  2. Build trie. Root has o, p, e, r children. "pea" lives under p → e → a.
  3. DFS from (0,0)='o' → trie.o exists, descend; walk a (1)→t (1,1)→h (2,1). Hit $ → record "oath".
  4. DFS from (1,1)='t': trie.t missing → return immediately. This is the prune savings.
  5. DFS from (1,0)='e' → e → a → t. Records "eat". "rain" needs an 'r' at any cell — found at (2,3), but no full path; not recorded.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(R·C·4^L) space O(Σ L)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Not deduping found words — pop $ from the node, or use a set.
  • Forgetting to restore board[r][c] after recursion — corrupts later starting cells.
  • Iterating all words inside DFS instead of indexing them in the trie — kills the optimization.
  • Trie node pruning is what makes it fast; skip it and large inputs TLE.
  • Diagonal moves are not allowed in LC 79/212 — only 4-direction.
Practice — LeetCode

  • 79 Word Search (Med) — single-word base case.
  • 980 Unique Paths III (Hard) — grid backtracking with state.
  • 1023 Camelcase Matching (Med) — pattern subsequence on trie.

docs.title: 04 — Autocomplete (trie + best children)
docs.text: Trie • Position 4/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a stream of search queries with weights, design a structure that returns the top-3 suggestions matching the current prefix, updated as the user types each character.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A trie walks each new character in O(1). The expensive part is "give me the best-K queries below this node." Two strategies: (a) at each node cache a sorted list of top-K candidates and update it on insert — fast queries, slower writes; (b) at query time DFS below the node collecting candidates into a heap — slower queries, no cache. For LC 1268 the dictionary is static, so option (b) is fine after sorting the words. For LC 642 the stream is hot — option (a) wins. Either way, the trie does the prefix selection; the candidate ranking is a separate per-node concern.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
class Node:
    __slots__ = ("kids", "top")
    def __init__(self):
        self.kids, self.top = {}, []      # top = sorted list of (-freq, word)

def insert(root, word, freq, K=3):
    node = root
    for ch in word:
        node = node.kids.setdefault(ch, Node())
        node.top.append((-freq, word))
        node.top.sort()                   # or use a small heap
        node.top = node.top[:K]

def suggest(root, prefix):
    node = root
    for ch in prefix:
        node = node.kids.get(ch)
        if not node: return []
    return [w for _, w in node.top]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. Insert ("apple", 3), ("app", 2), ("apricot", 1), ("banana", 5) with K = 3.
  2. After inserts, node 'a' has top = [(-3,"apple"), (-2,"app"), (-1,"apricot")]. Node 'ap' same. Node 'app' has top = [(-3,"apple"), (-2,"app")].
  3. User types 'a' → suggest returns ["apple", "app", "apricot"].
  4. Types 'p' (prefix "ap") → same list (all start with "ap").
  5. Types 'p' (prefix "app") → returns ["apple", "app"]. Types 'l' (prefix "appl") → returns ["apple"].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(L·K) insert / O(L) query space O(Σ L · K)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Sorting the cached list with the wrong tie-breaker — LC 642 wants higher frequency first, then lexicographically smaller.
  • Updating top-K only at the deepest node — every ancestor on the insert path needs the update.
  • Recomputing top-K from scratch at each insert instead of merging the new candidate into the existing K.
  • For LC 1268 (static dictionary), people overbuild — bisect on a sorted list gives O(log n) per character, no trie needed.
  • For very long prefixes, the cached list at each ancestor wastes memory — switch to query-time DFS if Σ L · K blows up.
Practice — LeetCode

  • 1268 Search Suggestions System (Med) — static, sorted-array trick.
  • 720 Longest Word in Dictionary (Med) — DFS picking smallest lex.
  • 588 Design In-Memory File System (Hard) — trie of paths.
  • 745 Prefix and Suffix Search (Hard) — pair-encoded trie.
  • 1166 Design File System (Med) — path-as-trie.
  • 1032 Stream of Characters (Hard) — reverse trie.
