  • 692 Top K Frequent Words (Med) — ranking primitive used inside autocomplete.

docs.title: DSA · 12 · Bit Manipulation
docs.text: XOR tricks, popcount DP, bitmask DP for exponential state compression.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The phrase "appears once, all others appear twice" almost always wants XOR.
  • "Power of two / four / single bit set" reduces to one line with n & (n-1).
  • Subset enumeration with n ≤ 20 — an int is your subset, bit i means element i is in.
  • Counting bits across a range — recursive DP f(x) = f(x >> 1) + (x & 1).
  • TSP-style problems on small graphs — state = (visited_mask, last_node).

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Every problem in this section pivots on one of four primitives. XOR ( a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a, commutative ) for "cancel duplicates." n & (n-1) to clear the lowest set bit — counts bits in O(popcount) and detects powers of two. n & -n to isolate the lowest set bit (the "Brian Kernighan trick"). Iterating subsets of a mask with sub = (sub - 1) & mask — used in submask DP. Once you recognize which primitive applies, the code is usually shorter than its proof.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Single Number (XOR)

Fold everything; duplicates cancel.

02Counting Bits

O(n) DP on popcount.

03Power of Two

One AND, one comparison.

04Bitmask TSP

Subset DP for n ≤ 20.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: For "single number among triples" (LC 137), simulate a 3-state counter per bit with two ints and bit ops. For "two singles among pairs" (LC 260), XOR the whole array → result is a^b; pick any set bit of that, partition by that bit, XOR each half. Bitmask DP scales as O(2^n · n²) for TSP and O(3^n) for "iterate all submasks of all masks" — the second sum is famous: Σ C(n,k) · 2^k = 3^n. For huge n, switch to meet-in-the-middle (split into two halves of 2^(n/2) each).

docs.title: 01 — Single Number (XOR fold)
docs.text: Bit Manipulation • Position ¼

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Every element appears twice except one. Find the one. Constant extra space.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: XOR has three properties that collapse this problem to a one-liner: a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a, and it's commutative + associative. Fold XOR across the whole array and every duplicated pair annihilates, leaving the lone element behind. No hash map, no sort, no extra memory — just one accumulator. This generalizes: for "two singles among pairs" XOR the whole thing to get a ^ b, then pick any set bit of a^b (it's a bit where they differ) and partition.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def single_number(nums):
    out = 0
    for x in nums:
        out ^= x
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. nums = [4, 1, 2, 1, 2]. out = 0.
  2. XOR 4 → out = 4 (binary 100).
  3. XOR 1 → out = 5 (101).
  4. XOR 2 → out = 7 (111). XOR 1 → out = 6 (110). XOR 2 → out = 4 (100).
  5. Loop ends. Return 4. The two 1s and two 2s cancelled; 4 survived.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Reaching for a hash map "to be safe" — the problem explicitly tests that you know XOR.
  • For LC 137 (every element thrice except one), naive XOR doesn't work — pairs of three don't cancel. Use two-bit counter trick: ones = (ones ^ x) & ~twos; twos = (twos ^ x) & ~ones.
  • For LC 260 (two singles), forgetting to mask with x & -x to isolate the diff bit.
  • Confusing "missing number" (LC 268) with single number — for missing, XOR all indices and all values.
  • Sign extension in languages with signed integers: in Java, beware Integer.MIN_VALUE; in Python, ints are unbounded so no issue.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 136 Single Number (Easy) — canonical XOR fold.
  • 137 Single Number II (Med) — two-bit state per position.
  • 260 Single Number III (Med) — partition by isolated diff bit.
  • 268 Missing Number (Easy) — XOR indices and values.
  • 287 Find the Duplicate Number (Med) — Floyd's cycle, not XOR.
  • 389 Find the Difference (Easy) — XOR two strings.
  • 421 Maximum XOR of Two Numbers (Med) — binary trie.
  • 461 Hamming Distance (Easy) — popcount of XOR.
  • 645 Set Mismatch (Easy) — XOR pairs to find dup and missing.
  • 1009 Complement of Base 10 Integer (Easy) — XOR with mask of same width.

docs.title: 02 — Counting Bits
docs.text: Bit Manipulation • Position 2/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: For every integer in [0, n], count the number of set bits. Return all n+1 answers.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Three reformulations of the same DP, increasing in cleverness. (a) popcount(i) = popcount(i >> 1) + (i & 1) — the top bits of i are the same as i//2, plus possibly one more for the parity. (b) popcount(i) = popcount(i & (i-1)) + 1 — i & (i-1) clears the lowest set bit, so the rest is recursive. © popcount(i) = popcount(i - lowest_pow2(i)) + 1 — strip the highest power of two and recurse. All three are O(n); pick the one you remember.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def count_bits(n):
    bits = [0] * (n + 1)
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        bits[i] = bits[i >> 1] + (i & 1)
    return bits

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n = 8. bits = [0, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _].
  2. i=1: bits[0] + 1 = 1. i=2: bits[1] + 0 = 1.
  3. i=3: bits[1] + 1 = 2. i=4: bits[2] + 0 = 1.
  4. i=5: bits[2] + 1 = 2. i=6: bits[3] + 0 = 2. i=7: bits[3] + 1 = 3.
  5. i=8: bits[4] + 0 = 1. Final: [0,1,1,2,1,2,2,3,1].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Calling a library popcount(i) in a loop — O(n · log n), fine but misses the point of the problem.
  • Off-by-one in array size — you need n+1 entries, not n.
  • Trying bits[i] = bits[i-1] + … — wrong; the recurrence is on i >> 1, not i - 1.
  • For 64-bit ints in Java/C++, use unsigned right shift to avoid sign extension.
  • In Python, bin(i).count("1") works but allocates a string per number; the DP is what's expected.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 338 Counting Bits (Easy) — canonical popcount DP.
  • 191 Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — per-number popcount via `n &= n-1`.
  • 762 Prime Number of Set Bits in Binary Representation (Easy) — popcount + primality.
  • 1356 Sort Integers by The Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — popcount as sort key.
  • 477 Total Hamming Distance (Med) — count per bit position.
  • 461 Hamming Distance (Easy) — popcount of XOR.
  • 693 Binary Number with Alternating Bits (Easy) — XOR with right-shift.
  • 1238 Circular Permutation in Binary Representation (Med) — Gray code with start.
  • 89 Gray Code (Med) — `i ^ (i >> 1)`.
  • 405 Convert a Number to Hexadecimal (Easy) — 4-bit nibble loop.

docs.title: 03 — Power of Two (n & (n-1))
docs.text: Bit Manipulation • Position ¾

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Return True iff n is a power of two — i.e., has exactly one set bit.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Subtracting 1 from a power of two flips all bits at and below the lone set bit: 1000 - 1 = 0111. AND them together and you get zero. For any other positive integer, at least one higher bit survives the AND. So n > 0 and (n & (n-1)) == 0 is the entire check. The same idiom counts set bits one at a time (Brian Kernighan's algorithm — each iteration clears one bit), and lets you isolate the lowest set bit with n & -n in two's complement.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def is_power_of_two(n):
    return n > 0 and (n & (n - 1)) == 0

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n = 16 (10000). n-1 = 15 (01111). n & (n-1) = 00000 = 0. Return True.
  2. n = 12 (1100). n-1 = 11 (1011). AND = 1000 ≠ 0. Return False.
  3. n = 1 (1). n-1 = 0. AND = 0. Return True.
  4. n = 0. Guard n > 0 returns False.
  5. n = -8. Guard returns False (signed negatives have the sign bit set; AND would give nonzero anyway).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(1) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the n > 0 guard — (0 & -1) == 0, so 0 would falsely pass.
  • For power-of-three/four/etc., the bit trick doesn't generalize cleanly. LC 326: check n > 0 and (3^19) % n == 0. LC 342: power of two and the set bit is at an even position (n & 0xAAAAAAAA == 0).
  • Confusing n & -n (isolates lowest set bit) with n & (n-1) (clears lowest set bit).
  • Using Math.log — floating-point error makes it unreliable for large powers.
  • In Java/C++, watch sign on Integer.MIN_VALUE: n-1 overflows for n = 0x80000000, but the AND still yields 0.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 231 Power of Two (Easy) — canonical `n & (n-1)`.
  • 326 Power of Three (Easy) — divide by 3 or check largest power.
  • 342 Power of Four (Easy) — power of two + even-position set bit.
  • 191 Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — Kernighan iteration.
  • 461 Hamming Distance (Easy) — popcount of XOR.
  • 67 Add Binary (Easy) — bit-wise carry loop.
  • 89 Gray Code (Med) — `i ^ (i >> 1)`.
  • 190 Reverse Bits (Easy) — 32-loop or byte-swap.
  • 268 Missing Number (Easy) — XOR or sum.
  • 1009 Complement of Base 10 Integer (Easy) — XOR with width mask.

docs.title: 04 — Bitmask DP — Travelling Salesman
docs.text: Bit Manipulation • Position 4/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given an n×n distance matrix (n ≤ 20), find the shortest tour visiting every city exactly once and returning to the start.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The state is (set of visited cities, current city) — once you know which cities you've visited and where you are, the future is independent of the past. Encode the visited set as a bitmask: bit i set means city i is done. There are 2^n · n states; each looks at n predecessors. Total O(2^n · n²) time and O(2^n · n) space. For n = 20 that's roughly 4·10^8 — tight but doable. The shift from "permutation search" (n! ≈ 2.4·10^18 at n=20) to "subset DP" (~ 4·10^8) is what makes the problem tractable.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
def tsp(dist):
    n = len(dist)
    INF = float("inf")
    dp = [[INF] * n for _ in range(1 << n)]
    dp[1][0] = 0                                    # start at city 0
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        for u in range(n):
            if dp[mask][u] == INF or not (mask >> u) & 1: continue
            for v in range(n):
                if (mask >> v) & 1: continue        # v already visited
                nmask = mask | (1 << v)
                dp[nmask][v] = min(dp[nmask][v], dp[mask][u] + dist[u][v])
    full = (1 << n) - 1
    return min(dp[full][u] + dist[u][0] for u in range(1, n))

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
1. n=4, start at city 0. State (0001, 0) = 0 (visited only city 0, currently at 0).
  2. Transition from (0001, 0): visit 1 → (0011, 1) = dist[0][1]; visit 2 → (0101, 2) = dist[0][2]; visit 3 → (1001, 3) = dist[0][3].
  3. From (0011, 1): visit 2 → (0111, 2); visit 3 → (1011, 3). Each new state takes min over all predecessors u in the smaller mask.
  4. Eventually fill dp[1111][u] for u ∈ {1,2,3}.
  5. Answer = min over u of dp[1111][u] + dist[u][0] (return to start).

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(2^n · n²) space O(2^n · n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Iterating masks out of order — must go increasing so subsets are filled before supersets.
  • Forgetting to require (mask >> u) & 1 — you'll transition from impossible states.
  • Returning min(dp[full]) without adding dist[u][0] — that's the open-tour answer, not the closed cycle.
  • Using n > 22 — memory blows up. For larger n, look at branch-and-bound or held-karp with bitset compression.
  • Submask iteration uses sub = (sub - 1) & mask (not just decrement) — easy to mis-write and burn a contest.
Practice — LeetCode
  • 943 Find the Shortest Superstring (Hard) — TSP over string-overlap costs.
  • 1125 Smallest Sufficient Team (Hard) — set-cover bitmask DP.
  • 691 Stickers to Spell Word (Hard) — bitmask of needed letters.
  • 847 Shortest Path Visiting All Nodes (Hard) — BFS on `(node, mask)`.
  • 1349 Maximum Students Taking Exam (Hard) — row-by-row bitmask DP.
  • 1986 Minimum Number of Work Sessions (Med) — submask DP.
  • 1601 Maximum Number of Achievable Transfer Requests (Hard) — enumerate request subsets.
  • 1066 Campus Bikes II (Med) — assignment via bitmask DP.
  • 1681 Minimum Incompatibility (Hard) — subset partition DP.
  • 526 Beautiful Arrangement (Med) — permutation count via mask DP.

docs.title: DSA · 13 · Sorting
docs.text: From elementary swaps to non-comparison radix — pick the right tool.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• Output needs to be ordered — by a key, by frequency, by custom comparator.
  • A faster downstream algorithm requires sorted input (binary search, two pointers, sweep line).
  • You need a stable order that preserves the relative position of equal keys.
  • Input keys are small integers or fixed-width strings — non-comparison sorts win.
  • The data is mostly sorted already — insertion sort is linear; quicksort degrades.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Every comparison sort is bounded by Ω(n log n) — you cannot beat it without exploiting structure in the keys. Within that bound the trade-offs are about stability, in-place behavior, cache locality, and worst-case vs average. Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) but allocates; quicksort is faster in practice but quadratic on adversarial input; heap sort is in-place O(n log n) but cache-hostile. When keys are integers in a bounded range, counting and radix sort drop to O(n + k) — linear time, at the cost of O(n + k) auxiliary memory. Pick by reading the constraints: range of values, stability requirement, memory budget.

docs.title: Comparison table
docs.text: Algorithm Time avg Time worst Space Stable In-place Bubble O(n²) O(n²) O(1) yes yes Insertion O(n²) O(n²) O(1) yes yes Selection O(n²) O(n²) O(1) no yes Merge O(n log n) O(n log n) O(n) yes no Quick O(n log n) O(n²) O(log n) stack no yes Heap O(n log n) O(n log n) O(1) no yes Counting O(n + k) O(n + k) O(n + k) yes no Radix O(d·(n + k)) O(d·(n + k)) O(n + k) yes no

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Bubble sort

Adjacent swaps bubble the largest element to the end on each pass.

02Insertion sort

Grow a sorted prefix one element at a time by shifting larger keys right.

03Selection sort

Scan for the minimum and swap it into place — n passes, minimal writes.

04Merge sort

Divide, recurse, merge — stable O(n log n) with O(n) auxiliary space.

05Quick sort (Lomuto)

Pick a pivot, partition, recurse — fastest in practice, quadratic worst case.

06Heap sort

Build a max-heap then sift the root to the end — in-place O(n log n).

07Counting sort

Tally occurrences then write keys back in order — linear when k is small.

08Radix sort (LSD)

Counting-sort by each digit from least to most significant — linear for fixed width.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Hybrid sorts — Timsort (Python, Java) and Introsort (C++ STL) blend insertion / merge / quick / heap to win on real-world data. Partial sorts — nth_element (quickselect) returns the k-th order statistic in O(n) average. External sorts — when data doesn't fit in memory, merge sort streams runs from disk. Custom comparators — most languages let you sort by key (Python key=, Java Comparator), which is how "sort by frequency then lexicographic" problems are usually solved.

docs.title: 01 — Bubble Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position ⅛

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order using only adjacent swaps.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Walk the array left to right; whenever two adjacent elements are out of order, swap them. The largest element "bubbles" to the end on every pass. Invariant: after pass i, the last i elements are in their final sorted positions. After at most n − 1 passes the entire array is sorted, and if a pass finishes without a swap the array is already sorted.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Repeated adjacent comparison with an early-exit flag.

def bubble_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n - 1):
        swapped = False
        for j in range(n - 1 - i):       # last i are sorted
            if a[j] > a[j + 1]:
                a[j], a[j + 1] = a[j + 1], a[j]
                swapped = True
        if not swapped:                  # already sorted
            return

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [5, 1, 4, 2, 8].

  1. Pass 1 compares pairs (5,1) (5,4) (5,2) (5,8) — three swaps land 8 at the end: [1, 4, 2, 5, 8].
  2. Pass 2 ignores the trailing 8, swaps (4,2): [1, 2, 4, 5, 8].
  3. Pass 3 makes no swaps — the swapped flag stays false, so the algorithm terminates early.
  4. Final array: [1, 2, 4, 5, 8]. Total: 4 swaps, 9 comparisons.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n²) space O(1) stable? yes in-place? yes

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the swapped flag — without it you always do n − 1 passes even on sorted input.
  • Iterating j to n − 1 instead of n − 1 − i — wastes comparisons over the already-sorted tail.
  • Using < instead of > flips the order; use > for ascending, < for descending.
  • Treating bubble sort as a serious production algorithm — it isn't. It's a teaching tool.
  • Confusing "stable" with "sorted" — bubble sort is stable because it only swaps strictly greater pairs.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — try every algo here against the same input.
  • Sort Array By Parity II (Easy) — two pointers, but adjacent swaps fix parity locally.
  • Sort Colors (Med) — Dutch national flag is essentially specialized bubble.
  • Sort Array By Parity (Easy) — partition warm-up.
  • Squares of a Sorted Array (Easy) — two pointers beats sort.
  • Wiggle Sort (Med) — single pass of conditional adjacent swaps.
  • Wiggle Sort II (Med) — harder; needs sort + interleave.
  • Sort Characters By Frequency (Med) — sort by count.
  • Meeting Rooms (Easy) — sort intervals, scan.
  • Sorting the Sentence (Easy) — index-based placement.

docs.title: 02 — Insertion Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position 2/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order by growing a sorted prefix one element at a time.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Imagine sorting a hand of cards: pick up the next card and slide it left past every larger card already in your hand. Invariant: at the start of iteration i, the subarray a[0..i-1] is sorted (a permutation of the original first i elements). On each iteration you insert a[i] into that prefix by shifting larger elements right. Best case (nearly sorted input) is O(n) because the inner loop rarely shifts.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Right-shift larger elements; drop the current key into its place.

def insertion_sort(a):
    for i in range(1, len(a)):
        key = a[i]
        j = i - 1
        while j >= 0 and a[j] > key:
            a[j + 1] = a[j]              # shift right
            j -= 1
        a[j + 1] = key                   # insert

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [5, 2, 4, 6, 1].

  1. i = 1, key = 2. Shift 5 right → [2, 5, 4, 6, 1]. Prefix [2, 5] sorted.
  2. i = 2, key = 4. Shift 5 right → [2, 4, 5, 6, 1].
  3. i = 3, key = 6. No shifts — 6 ≥ 5. Prefix already correct.
  4. i = 4, key = 1. Shifts 6, 5, 4, 2 all right → [1, 2, 4, 5, 6].
  5. Done. Total: 6 shifts, 7 comparisons.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n²) space O(1) stable? yes in-place? yes

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Off-by-one on a[j + 1] = key — you wrote key into the slot after the smallest larger element.
  • Using strict > keeps stability; flipping to >= would break it by shifting equal keys past their original order.
  • Starting i at 0 instead of 1 — the prefix of length 1 is already sorted.
  • Quadratic in the worst case — don't reach for it on adversarial input.
  • Despite the bad asymptotics, insertion sort is the fastest sort for very small or nearly-sorted arrays — that's why Timsort uses it for runs of length ≤ 32.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Insertion Sort List (Med) — insertion sort on a linked list.
  • Sort an Array (Med) — benchmark all sorts.
  • Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — in-place merge from the end.
  • Height Checker (Easy) — count out-of-place positions.
  • Wiggle Sort (Med) — local swaps reach the goal in one pass.
  • H-Index (Med) — sort then scan.
  • Longest Word in Dictionary through Deleting (Med) — sort dictionary by length/lexicographic.
  • Sort Array by Increasing Frequency (Easy) — sort with a custom comparator.
  • Sort Integers by The Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — popcount then sort.
  • Car Fleet (Med) — sort by position, scan from the right.

docs.title: 03 — Selection Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position ⅜

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order by repeatedly selecting the smallest remaining element.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: On pass i you scan the suffix a[i..n-1], find its minimum, and swap it into position i. Invariant: after pass i, a[0..i] contains the i + 1 smallest elements in sorted order, and they are all ≤ every element in the unsorted suffix. Unlike bubble sort it makes at most n − 1 swaps total — useful when writes are expensive (e.g., flash memory).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Find min in suffix, swap to front of suffix, repeat.

def selection_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n - 1):
        min_idx = i
        for j in range(i + 1, n):
            if a[j] < a[min_idx]:
                min_idx = j
        if min_idx != i:
            a[i], a[min_idx] = a[min_idx], a[i]

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [64, 25, 12, 22, 11].

  1. i = 0: scan finds min 11 at index 4. Swap → [11, 25, 12, 22, 64].
  2. i = 1: scan suffix [25, 12, 22, 64] — min is 12 at index 2. Swap → [11, 12, 25, 22, 64].
  3. i = 2: min of [25, 22, 64] is 22. Swap → [11, 12, 22, 25, 64].
  4. i = 3: min of [25, 64] is 25, already in place. No swap.
  5. Done. Total: 3 swaps regardless of how scrambled the input was.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n²) space O(1) stable? no in-place? yes

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Not stable — swapping the min into position can leapfrog over an equal key, breaking original order. Example: [5a, 5b, 3] becomes [3, 5b, 5a].
  • Always O(n²) — even on sorted input, you still scan every suffix to confirm the minimum.
  • Forgetting to if min_idx != i before swapping is a micro-optimization, not a correctness bug.
  • Confusing selection with quickselect — quickselect finds one order statistic in O(n) average; selection sort finds all of them in O(n²).
  • Useful when minimizing writes matters more than minimizing comparisons.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Kth Largest Element in an Array (Med) — partial selection sort works.
  • Sort an Array (Med) — benchmark.
  • Sort Integers by The Power Value (Med) — compute key, sort.
  • Sort Integers by The Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — sort by popcount.
  • Sort Array by Increasing Frequency (Easy) — frequency comparator.
  • Sorting the Sentence (Easy) — index-based placement.
  • Least Number of Unique Integers (Med) — count, sort by frequency.
  • Array Partition I (Easy) — sort and sum every other.
  • Rank Transform of an Array (Easy) — sort to assign ranks.
  • Sort Colors (Med) — Dutch flag generalizes selection.

docs.title: 04 — Merge Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position 4/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order with guaranteed O(n log n) time.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Divide the array in half, sort each half recursively, then merge the two sorted halves in linear time. Invariant: every recursive call returns a sorted subarray; the merge step assumes both inputs are sorted and produces a sorted output by repeatedly taking the smaller front element. The recursion tree has log n levels each doing O(n) work — hence O(n log n). Merge sort is the canonical example of "divide and conquer" and is the basis of Python's Timsort and Java's Arrays.sort for objects.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Split, recurse, merge into an auxiliary buffer.

def merge_sort(a):
    if len(a) <= 1:
        return a
    mid = len(a) // 2
    left  = merge_sort(a[:mid])
    right = merge_sort(a[mid:])
    return merge(left, right)

def merge(L, R):
    out, i, j = [], 0, 0
    while i < len(L) and j < len(R):
        if L[i] <= R[j]:                 # <= preserves stability
            out.append(L[i]); i += 1
        else:
            out.append(R[j]); j += 1
    out.extend(L[i:]); out.extend(R[j:])
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10].

  1. Split into [38, 27, 43, 3] and [9, 82, 10].
  2. Recurse left: split → [38, 27] and [43, 3] → both merge to [27, 38] and [3, 43] → merge to [3, 27, 38, 43].
  3. Recurse right: split → [9] and [82, 10] → right merges to [10, 82] → merge to [9, 10, 82].
  4. Final merge: walk pointers across [3, 27, 38, 43] and [9, 10, 82] taking the smaller head each time → [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82].
  5. Total: 3 levels of recursion, ~7 comparisons per level.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(n) stable? yes in-place? no

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Allocating new lists at every level is convenient but doubles peak memory — production implementations preallocate one O(n) buffer and ping-pong.
  • Using < in the merge breaks stability when keys tie — always use <= to take from the left first.
  • Forgetting the out.extend(L[i:]) tail copy — one side will have leftovers when the other empties.
  • The base case must include len(a) == 1, not just len(a) == 0.
  • For linked lists, merge sort is the right answer because it doesn't need random access — see Sort List.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — implement from scratch.
  • Sort List (Med) — merge sort on a linked list.
  • Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — the merge step in isolation.
  • Merge k Sorted Lists (Hard) — k-way merge with a heap or pairwise.
  • Merge Two Sorted Lists (Easy) — linked-list merge.
  • Count of Smaller Numbers After Self (Hard) — count inversions during merge.
  • Count of Range Sum (Hard) — merge sort on prefix sums.
  • Reverse Pairs (Hard) — variant of counting inversions.
  • Median of Two Sorted Arrays (Hard) — virtual merge with binary search.
  • Intersection of Three Sorted Arrays (Easy) — three-pointer merge.

docs.title: 05 — Quick Sort (Lomuto)
docs.text: Sorting • Position ⅝

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order with the lowest constant factors of any comparison sort.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Pick a pivot, partition the array so that elements less than the pivot are on the left and the rest on the right, then recurse on both sides. Invariant (Lomuto partition): at index i, the slice a[lo..i] holds every element ≤ pivot found so far, and a[i+1..j-1] holds elements > pivot. After partition the pivot sits in its final sorted position. Average case is O(n log n) because the pivot splits roughly evenly, but pathological pivots (e.g., always the largest) degrade to O(n²). Randomizing or median-of-three pivot selection makes the bad case astronomically unlikely.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Lomuto partition + recurse on both sides.

def quick_sort(a, lo=0, hi=None):
    if hi is None: hi = len(a) - 1
    if lo < hi:
        p = partition(a, lo, hi)
        quick_sort(a, lo, p - 1)
        quick_sort(a, p + 1, hi)

def partition(a, lo, hi):
    pivot = a[hi]                        # randomize for safety
    i = lo - 1
    for j in range(lo, hi):
        if a[j] <= pivot:
            i += 1
            a[i], a[j] = a[j], a[i]
    a[i + 1], a[hi] = a[hi], a[i + 1]
    return i + 1

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [3, 6, 8, 2, 5] with pivot = last element.

  1. partition(lo=0, hi=4), pivot = 5. Scan: 3 ≤ 5 swap with itself → i = 0. 6 > 5 skip. 8 > 5 skip. 2 ≤ 5 swap a[1]↔a[3] → [3, 2, 8, 6, 5], i = 1. Finalize: swap a[2]↔a[4] → [3, 2, 5, 6, 8]. Pivot lands at index 2.
  2. Recurse left on [3, 2] with pivot 2: scan finds i = -1, swap → [2, 3].
  3. Recurse right on [6, 8] with pivot 8: scan finds i = 0, swap with itself → unchanged.
  4. Final array: [2, 3, 5, 6, 8].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst space O(log n) stack avg stable? no in-place? yes

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Always choosing the last (or first) element as pivot is the textbook way to get O(n²) on sorted input. Randomize or pick median-of-three.
  • Recursing on both halves without tail-call optimization can blow the stack on adversarial input — recurse on the smaller side, iterate on the larger.
  • Lomuto is simpler but does more swaps than Hoare partitioning — both are correct.
  • Quick sort is not stable — partitioning leapfrogs equal keys.
  • The same partition routine, run recursively on only one side, gives you Quickselect for the k-th order statistic in O(n) average.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — bring your own implementation.
  • Kth Largest Element in an Array (Med) — quickselect, average O(n).
  • Sort Colors (Med) — three-way partition (Dutch flag).
  • Wiggle Sort II (Med) — quickselect + virtual indexing.
  • Sort Characters By Frequency (Med) — bucket or sort by count.
  • K Closest Points to Origin (Med) — quickselect by distance.
  • Top K Frequent Elements (Med) — quickselect on (count, value) pairs.
  • Top K Frequent Words (Med) — same with lexicographic tiebreaker.
  • Wiggle Sort (Med) — local fix in one pass.
  • Find the Kth Largest Integer in the Array (Med) — quickselect with string compare.

docs.title: 06 — Heap Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position 6/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array in ascending order in-place with guaranteed O(n log n) time.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Treat the array as a binary max-heap (parent ≥ children), then repeatedly swap the root (the current max) with the last unsorted element and sift the new root down. Invariant: after k extracts the last k slots of the array hold the k largest elements in sorted order, and the prefix a[0..n-k-1] is still a valid max-heap. Building the initial heap is O(n) — surprisingly tight — and each of the n − 1 extractions costs O(log n). Heap sort is the only O(n log n) algorithm that's both in-place and worst-case guaranteed.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Heapify bottom-up, then repeatedly swap-and-sift.

def heap_sort(a):
    n = len(a)
    for i in range(n // 2 - 1, -1, -1):  # build max-heap
        sift_down(a, i, n)
    for end in range(n - 1, 0, -1):      # extract max
        a[0], a[end] = a[end], a[0]
        sift_down(a, 0, end)

def sift_down(a, i, n):
    while True:
        l, r, largest = 2 * i + 1, 2 * i + 2, i
        if l < n and a[l] > a[largest]: largest = l
        if r < n and a[r] > a[largest]: largest = r
        if largest == i: return
        a[i], a[largest] = a[largest], a[i]
        i = largest

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [4, 10, 3, 5, 1].

  1. Build heap from index n/2 − 1 = 1. Sift 10 — already a max. Sift index 0 (4): largest child is 10, swap → [10, 4, 3, 5, 1]; sift again from index 1: largest child is 5, swap → [10, 5, 3, 4, 1]. Heap is built.
  2. Extract: swap a[0] ↔ a[4] → [1, 5, 3, 4, 10], sift in size-4 prefix → [5, 4, 3, 1, 10].
  3. Extract: swap a[0] ↔ a[3] → [1, 4, 3, 5, 10], sift in size-3 → [4, 1, 3, 5, 10].
  4. Extract: swap a[0] ↔ a[2] → [3, 1, 4, 5, 10], sift in size-2 → [3, 1, 4, 5, 10].
  5. Extract: swap a[0] ↔ a[1] → [1, 3, 4, 5, 10]. Sorted.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n log n) space O(1) stable? no in-place? yes

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Building the heap top-down (siftUp on each element) is O(n log n); building bottom-up (siftDown from n/2 − 1) is O(n) — use the latter.
  • Mixing up min-heap vs max-heap — for ascending sort you need a max-heap.
  • Index math: with 0-based arrays, children of i are 2i+1 and 2i+2, parent is (i-1)//2. With 1-based arrays, children are 2i and 2i+1. Pick one and stick with it.
  • Cache locality is poor — heap sort touches non-adjacent indices, which is why it's slower in practice than quicksort despite identical asymptotics.
  • Not stable; for a stable O(n log n) in-place sort, you need block merge sort, which is genuinely hard to implement.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — implement heap sort.
  • Kth Largest Element in an Array (Med) — min-heap of size k.
  • Top K Frequent Elements (Med) — heap on (count, value).
  • Top K Frequent Words (Med) — heap with custom comparator.
  • Sort Characters By Frequency (Med) — max-heap by count.
  • K Closest Points to Origin (Med) — max-heap of size k by distance.
  • Find Median from Data Stream (Hard) — two heaps.
  • Merge k Sorted Lists (Hard) — min-heap of list heads.
  • IPO (Hard) — two heaps for capital and profit.
  • Minimize Deviation in Array (Hard) — max-heap with conditional halving.

docs.title: 07 — Counting Sort
docs.text: Sorting • Position ⅞

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort an array of integers in a known small range in linear time.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Don't compare — tally. Build a count array cnt[v] of how many times each value v appears, then walk cnt in order and write each value back to the output that many times. To keep stability and to handle satellite data, do a prefix-sum pass over cnt so that cnt[v] becomes the position where the next v should land in the output, then iterate the input from right to left placing each element. Invariant (stable version): after the right-to-left placement, equal keys appear in their original relative order. Total work is O(n + k) where k is the range of values.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Tally → prefix sum → place from right.

def counting_sort(a):
    if not a: return a
    lo, hi = min(a), max(a)
    k = hi - lo + 1
    cnt = [0] * k
    for v in a:                          # tally
        cnt[v - lo] += 1
    for i in range(1, k):                # prefix sum
        cnt[i] += cnt[i - 1]
    out = [0] * len(a)
    for v in reversed(a):                # place from right for stability
        cnt[v - lo] -= 1
        out[cnt[v - lo]] = v
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [4, 2, 2, 8, 3, 3, 1]. Range = [1, 8], k = 8.

  1. Tally: cnt = [1, 2, 2, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1] (one 1, two 2s, two 3s, one 4, one 8).
  2. Prefix sum: cnt = [1, 3, 5, 6, 6, 6, 6, 7] — cnt[v] is now "how many elements ≤ v".
  3. Walk input right-to-left. Last element 1: cnt[0] − 1 = 0, place at out[0]. Next 3: cnt[2] − 1 = 4, place at out[4]. Continue.
  4. After all placements: out = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 8].
  5. Total: 3 linear passes, zero comparisons.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n + k) space O(n + k) stable? yes in-place? no

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Blows up when k is huge — sorting [0, 10⁹] of length 5 with a count array of size 10⁹ is a disaster. Use only when k = O(n).
  • Forgetting to shift by lo when the minimum is negative — your indices go out of bounds.
  • Iterating the input left-to-right during placement breaks stability — always right-to-left after prefix sums.
  • The "simple" version that just writes v cnt[v] times works for plain integers but loses satellite data — for objects keyed by an integer, you need the stable placement.
  • Counting sort is the stable subroutine that makes radix sort work.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — bounded range = counting wins.
  • Relative Sort Array (Easy) — counting + custom ordering.
  • Height Checker (Easy) — counting sort of heights ≤ 100.
  • H-Index (Med) — counting on citations.
  • Rank Transform of an Array (Easy) — counting / hashing.
  • Sort Array by Increasing Frequency (Easy) — count, sort by count.
  • Sort Characters By Frequency (Med) — bucket by frequency.
  • Sort Colors (Med) — counting on three values.
  • Sort Integers by Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — bucket by popcount.
  • Sorting the Sentence (Easy) — counting-style placement.

docs.title: 08 — Radix Sort (LSD)
docs.text: Sorting • Position 8/8

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Sort fixed-width integers (or strings) in linear time when keys are too sparse for counting sort.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Run counting sort on the least-significant digit first, then the next digit, then the next — d passes total. Because each pass uses a stable counting sort, keys with the same current digit stay in the order established by earlier (less-significant) passes. Invariant: after pass i, the array is correctly sorted by the bottom i digits. After d passes the entire key is sorted. For 64-bit integers in base 256 that's 8 passes regardless of n — total cost O(8n) which is linear in n.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Iterate digits from least to most significant, stable-sorting on each.

def radix_sort(a, base=10):
    if not a: return a
    lo = min(a)
    a = [x - lo for x in a]              # shift to non-negative
    max_v = max(a)
    exp = 1
    while exp <= max_v:
        a = counting_by_digit(a, exp, base)
        exp *= base
    return [x + lo for x in a]

def counting_by_digit(a, exp, base):
    cnt = [0] * base
    for v in a: cnt[(v // exp) % base] += 1
    for i in range(1, base): cnt[i] += cnt[i - 1]
    out = [0] * len(a)
    for v in reversed(a):
        d = (v // exp) % base
        cnt[d] -= 1
        out[cnt[d]] = v
    return out

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Sort [170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66] in base 10.

  1. Pass 1 (exp = 1, ones digit): digits [0, 5, 5, 0, 2, 4, 2, 6]. After stable counting sort → [170, 90, 802, 2, 24, 45, 75, 66].
  2. Pass 2 (exp = 10, tens digit): digits [7, 9, 0, 0, 2, 4, 7, 6]. Stable sort → [802, 2, 24, 45, 66, 170, 75, 90].
  3. Pass 3 (exp = 100, hundreds digit): digits [8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0]. Stable sort → [2, 24, 45, 66, 75, 90, 170, 802].
  4. Done — max value is 802, 3 digits, 3 passes.
  5. Each pass is a stable counting sort; correctness rides on that stability.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(d · (n + k)) space O(n + k) stable? yes in-place? no

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Stability is mandatory — if the per-digit sort isn't stable, earlier digits get scrambled. Use counting sort, not insertion or selection.
  • LSD-radix needs fixed-width keys; for variable-length strings, use MSD-radix or left-pad.
  • Negative numbers: shift everything by −min before sorting, then shift back.
  • Choosing base 10 is intuitive but inefficient. Real implementations use base 256 (one byte per pass) — 4 passes for a 32-bit int, 8 for 64-bit.
  • "Radix sort is linear" hides the constant d. For genuinely large keys (e.g., 1000-digit strings), radix can lose to comparison sorts.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Sort an Array (Med) — bring radix to the benchmark.
  • Maximum Gap (Hard) — classic radix or bucket sort application.
  • Relative Sort Array (Easy) — bounded values let radix shine.
  • H-Index (Med) — counting/radix on citations.
  • Rank Transform of an Array (Easy) — sort to assign ranks.
  • Sort Integers by Number of 1 Bits (Easy) — composite key sort.
  • Sort Array by Increasing Frequency (Easy) — pair sort.
  • Sorting the Sentence (Easy) — index-based placement.
  • Sort Array By Parity (Easy) — partition by single bit.
  • Wiggle Sort (Med) — sort then interleave.

docs.title: DSA · 14 · Linked List
docs.text: Reverse, cycle, merge, LRU cache — pointer surgery patterns.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• The problem statement literally hands you a ListNode head — most LC linked-list tags.
  • You need to detect a cycle, find its entry, or measure list length without extra memory.
  • Two lists must be merged or intersected without converting to arrays.
  • You need O(1) insert/delete given a node reference (LRU/LFU caches).
  • A position is described relative to the end ("Nth from end", "middle") — two-pointer or fast/slow wins.

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Almost every linked-list problem is one of three pointer tricks. Dummy head — allocate a placeholder before the real head so you don't special-case the first node. Fast/slow pointers — fast moves two steps for every one of slow; they detect cycles, find the middle, and locate the N-th-from-end node in one pass. Three-pointer reverse — prev, cur, next walk the list flipping arrows. Combined with a hash map (node → DLL node) you get the constant-time LRU cache. The discipline is to draw the arrows on paper before you write code — pointer bugs are silent and brutal.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Reverse linked list

Flip arrows in place with three pointers — the foundational operation.

02Cycle detection (Floyd)

Tortoise and hare — detect, then locate the entry node in O(1) extra space.

03Merge two sorted lists

Dummy head + two pointers stitch two sorted lists in one pass.

04Remove Nth from end

Two pointers offset by N find the predecessor in a single pass.

05LRU Cache

HashMap + doubly-linked list — O(1) get and put with eviction.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Doubly-linked lists add prev pointers — a few problems (LRU, design problems) genuinely need them. Circular lists show up in Josephus and round-robin schedulers. Random pointers (LC 138) test deep-copy with mapping tables. Skip lists appear in some design problems but aren't typical interview fare. The same pointer-surgery skills generalize to trees — many tree problems are easier once you think of them as linked lists with two children.

docs.title: 01 — Reverse Linked List
docs.text: Linked List • Position ⅕

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Reverse a singly-linked list and return the new head.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Walk the list with three pointers — prev (last visited), cur (current), next (lookahead). At every step you save cur.next, point cur.next back to prev, then advance both pointers one step. Invariant: at the start of each iteration, the prefix from prev back to the original tail is already reversed, and cur is the head of the remaining unreversed suffix. When cur becomes None, prev is the new head.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Iterative three-pointer flip.

def reverse_list(head):
    prev, cur = None, head
    while cur:
        nxt = cur.next                   # save
        cur.next = prev                  # flip
        prev = cur                       # advance prev
        cur = nxt                        # advance cur
    return prev                          # new head

Recursive variant (O(n) stack):

def reverse_list(head):
    if not head or not head.next: return head
    new_head = reverse_list(head.next)
    head.next.next = head
    head.next = None
    return new_head

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Reverse 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → None.

  1. prev = None, cur = 1. Save nxt = 2. Flip 1.next = None. Advance: prev = 1, cur = 2.
  2. Save nxt = 3. Flip 2.next = 1. Advance: prev = 2, cur = 3. List from prev: 2 → 1 → None.
  3. Save nxt = 4. Flip 3.next = 2. Advance: prev = 3, cur = 4.
  4. Save nxt = 5. Flip 4.next = 3. Advance: prev = 4, cur = 5.
  5. Save nxt = None. Flip 5.next = 4. Advance: prev = 5, cur = None. Loop exits, return 5. Final list: 5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1 → None.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting to save cur.next before mutating — you lose the rest of the list.
  • Returning head instead of prev — head is now the new tail, not the new head.
  • Recursive version blows the stack on long lists; prefer iterative.
  • For partial reversal (LC 92, 25), introduce a dummy head and reverse a sublist, then stitch it back — don't try to handle head edges in the loop.
  • The same three-pointer pattern reverses doubly-linked lists too; just swap prev and next fields on every node.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Reverse Linked List (Easy) — the canonical version.
  • Reverse Linked List II (Med) — reverse positions m..n with a dummy head.
  • Reverse Nodes in K-Group (Hard) — chained partial reversals.
  • Swap Nodes in Pairs (Med) — reverse in groups of 2.
  • Palindrome Linked List (Easy) — reverse second half, compare.
  • Reorder List (Med) — split, reverse second half, weave.
  • Rotate List (Med) — find tail, close ring, break at offset.
  • Partition List (Med) — two dummy heads, then concatenate.
  • Odd Even Linked List (Med) — interleave with two dummy heads.
  • Reverse Nodes in Even Length Groups (Med) — conditional group reversal.

docs.title: 02 — Cycle Detection (Floyd)
docs.text: Linked List • Position ⅖

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Determine whether a linked list has a cycle, and if so, return the node where the cycle begins.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Send two pointers down the list — slow advances one step, fast advances two. If there's no cycle, fast falls off the end (None). If there is a cycle, the fast pointer eventually laps the slow one and they meet inside the loop. Invariant: every step, the gap (fast − slow) mod cycle_length decreases by 1, so within at most cycle_length extra iterations after both enter the loop they collide. To find the start of the cycle, reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time — they meet at the entry point (provable from arithmetic on 2 · slow_distance = fast_distance).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Two-phase Floyd's tortoise and hare.

def detect_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            break
    else:
        return None                      # no cycle

    slow = head
    while slow is not fast:              # phase 2: find entry
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next
    return slow

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
List 3 → 2 → 0 → -4 → (back to 2) — cycle entry at node 2.

  1. slow = 3, fast = 3. Step: slow = 2, fast = 0.
  2. Step: slow = 0, fast = 2 (jumped through -4 and looped). Not equal.
  3. Step: slow = -4, fast = -4. Equal — cycle confirmed.
  4. Phase 2: reset slow = 3 (head). Step both: slow = 2, fast = 2. Equal.
  5. Return 2 — the cycle entry node.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• The loop condition while fast and fast.next — if you only check fast, you'll crash on fast.next.next.
  • Starting slow and fast at different nodes breaks the math; start them both at head.
  • Hash-set solution is O(n) space — the whole point of Floyd is O(1) space. Mention both in interviews.
  • Phase 2 needs is, not == — you're comparing node identity, not values (though for LC it usually works either way because node values can repeat).
  • The same trick finds duplicates in an array treated as a function (LC 287) — nums[i] becomes the "next pointer".
Practice — LeetCode
  • Linked List Cycle (Easy) — detection only.
  • Linked List Cycle II (Med) — return cycle entry.
  • Find the Duplicate Number (Med) — Floyd on an implicit function.
  • Happy Number (Easy) — Floyd on digit-square sequence.
  • Circular Array Loop (Med) — Floyd with direction constraint.
  • Palindrome Linked List (Easy) — fast/slow finds middle.
  • Middle of the Linked List (Easy) — pure fast/slow.
  • Remove Nth Node From End of List (Med) — two-pointer offset.
  • Intersection of Two Linked Lists (Easy) — pointer aliasing.
  • Reorder List (Med) — middle + reverse + merge.

docs.title: 03 — Merge Two Sorted Lists
docs.text: Linked List • Position ⅗

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given two sorted linked lists, splice them into a single sorted list and return its head.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Walk two pointers down the input lists; on each step append whichever has the smaller head to the output and advance that pointer. Invariant: the output is a sorted list whose elements are exactly the consumed prefix of both inputs. When one list is exhausted, the rest of the other is already sorted and can be linked in O(1) — no copying. A dummy head lets you avoid special-casing the first append.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Two-pointer merge into a dummy-headed list.

def merge_two(l1, l2):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    tail = dummy
    while l1 and l2:
        if l1.val <= l2.val:
            tail.next = l1
            l1 = l1.next
        else:
            tail.next = l2
            l2 = l2.next
        tail = tail.next
    tail.next = l1 or l2                 # tail link
    return dummy.next

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Merge 1 → 2 → 4 and 1 → 3 → 4.

  1. tail = dummy. Compare 1 ≤ 1 — take from l1. Output: 1. Advance l1 → 2.
  2. Compare 2 > 1 — take from l2. Output: 1 → 1. Advance l2 → 3.
  3. Compare 2 ≤ 3 — take from l1. Output: 1 → 1 → 2. Advance l1 → 4.
  4. Compare 4 > 3 — take from l2. Output: 1 → 1 → 2 → 3. Advance l2 → 4.
  5. Compare 4 ≤ 4 — take from l1. Advance l1 → None. Loop exits. Tail-link l2 = 4. Final: 1 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 4.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n + m) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the dummy head — your first if has to special-case "is this the very first node" and the code doubles in size.
  • Using < instead of <= is correct for ordering but breaks stability across the two inputs (rare to matter).
  • Forgetting the tail link tail.next = l1 or l2 leaves the merged list short.
  • Returning dummy instead of dummy.next returns a leading zero.
  • For k sorted lists, generalize with a min-heap of (value, list_id, node) — see LC 23 Merge k Sorted Lists.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Merge Two Sorted Lists (Easy) — the canonical version.
  • Merge k Sorted Lists (Hard) — heap-based k-way merge.
  • Sort List (Med) — merge sort = repeated merge.
  • Merge Sorted Array (Easy) — array version, merge from the back.
  • Partition List (Med) — two dummy heads, then concatenate.
  • Merge In Between Linked Lists (Med) — splice one list into another.
  • Convert Binary Number in a Linked List to Integer (Easy) — single traversal.
  • Next Greater Node In Linked List (Med) — monotonic stack on a list.
  • Odd Even Linked List (Med) — two dummy heads, interleave.
  • Swapping Nodes in a Linked List (Med) — two-pointer offset.

docs.title: 04 — Remove Nth From End
docs.text: Linked List • Position ⅘

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Remove the N-th node from the end of a singly-linked list in one pass.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: You can't index from the end without first knowing the length — unless you maintain a constant gap. Send fast N steps ahead, then advance both fast and slow until fast reaches the tail; at that point slow is sitting on the predecessor of the node to remove. Invariant: fast is always exactly N nodes ahead of slow. Combine with a dummy head so removing the actual head node (N == length) is the same code path as removing any other.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Two pointers with an N-step offset and a dummy head.

def remove_nth_from_end(head, n):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    fast = slow = dummy
    for _ in range(n):                   # advance fast n steps
        fast = fast.next
    while fast.next:                     # both move until fast at tail
        fast = fast.next
        slow = slow.next
    slow.next = slow.next.next           # skip the target
    return dummy.next

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Remove 2nd from end of 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5, expected 1 → 2 → 3 → 5.

  1. Dummy → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5. fast = slow = dummy.
  2. Advance fast 2 steps: fast = 2.
  3. Advance both until fast.next is None: fast = 3, slow = 1 → fast = 4, slow = 2 → fast = 5, slow = 3. Loop exits — slow = 3.
  4. slow.next.next = 5, so slow.next = 5 skips over 4.
  5. Return dummy.next = 1. Final list: 1 → 2 → 3 → 5.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(1)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• No dummy head → removing the first node (when N == length) is a special case that's easy to get wrong.
  • Off-by-one on the offset — advancing fast N steps from dummy makes the gap N, which is what you want; advancing from head makes the gap N − 1.
  • Loop condition while fast.next vs while fast — the first one stops with fast at the tail (so slow is at the predecessor); the second stops with fast = None (so slow is at the target). Pick one consistent variant.
  • Failing to free the removed node is harmless in Python; in C/C++ that's a leak.
  • The same offset trick gives you "find the middle" (gap = n/2) and "find Kth from end" (gap = K).
Practice — LeetCode
  • Remove Nth Node From End of List (Med) — canonical.
  • Middle of the Linked List (Easy) — fast/slow with a 1-step gap doubled.
  • Palindrome Linked List (Easy) — find middle, reverse, compare.
  • Reorder List (Med) — middle + reverse + weave.
  • Rotate List (Med) — find tail, count length, break at offset.
  • Partition List (Med) — two dummy heads.
  • Reverse Linked List II (Med) — locate two boundaries, reverse between.
  • Swapping Nodes in a Linked List (Med) — two pointers, swap values.
  • Delete the Middle Node (Med) — fast/slow + predecessor.
  • Swap Nodes in Pairs (Med) — pointer surgery in pairs.

docs.title: 05 — LRU Cache
docs.text: Linked List • Position 5/5

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Design a cache supporting get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1), evicting the least-recently-used entry when capacity is exceeded.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: You need two things in O(1): lookup by key (hash map) and ordering by recency with constant-time removal from the middle (doubly-linked list). Keep a hash map key → DLL node. The DLL has a dummy head and dummy tail; the node next to head is most-recently-used, the node before tail is least-recently-used. Invariant: every key in the map points to a node currently in the DLL, and DLL order is exactly recency order from MRU (front) to LRU (back). Every operation touches one map slot and at most two DLL pointer flips.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
HashMap + doubly-linked list with sentinel head/tail.

class Node:
    __slots__ = ("k", "v", "prev", "nxt")
    def __init__(self, k, v):
        self.k, self.v = k, v
        self.prev = self.nxt = None

class LRUCache:
    def __init__(self, cap):
        self.cap = cap
        self.m = {}
        self.head = Node(0, 0)           # sentinels
        self.tail = Node(0, 0)
        self.head.nxt = self.tail
        self.tail.prev = self.head

    def _remove(self, n):
        n.prev.nxt, n.nxt.prev = n.nxt, n.prev

    def _add_front(self, n):
        n.prev = self.head
        n.nxt = self.head.nxt
        self.head.nxt.prev = n
        self.head.nxt = n

    def get(self, k):
        if k not in self.m: return -1
        n = self.m[k]
        self._remove(n); self._add_front(n)
        return n.v

    def put(self, k, v):
        if k in self.m:
            n = self.m[k]; n.v = v
            self._remove(n); self._add_front(n)
            return
        if len(self.m) == self.cap:
            lru = self.tail.prev
            self._remove(lru); del self.m[lru.k]
        n = Node(k, v); self.m[k] = n; self._add_front(n)

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Capacity 2. Operations: put(1, A), put(2, B), get(1), put(3, C), get(2).

  1. put(1, A) — new node, map {1: A}, DLL head ↔ 1 ↔ tail.
  2. put(2, B) — new node added at front, DLL head ↔ 2 ↔ 1 ↔ tail.
  3. get(1) — found; move 1 to front. DLL head ↔ 1 ↔ 2 ↔ tail. Returns A.
  4. put(3, C) — at capacity. Evict tail.prev = 2, drop from map. Insert 3 at front. DLL head ↔ 3 ↔ 1 ↔ tail, map {1, 3}.
  5. get(2) — not in map; return -1.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(1) per op space O(capacity)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Skipping sentinel head/tail — every insertion/deletion then has to handle "is this the only node" / "is this the head/tail" edge cases.
  • Forgetting to update the map on eviction — you leak a key pointing to a freed node.
  • Updating the value but forgetting to move the node to front on put of an existing key — the LRU order goes stale.
  • In Java/Python, LinkedHashMap / OrderedDict gives this almost for free; interviewers usually want you to implement the DLL by hand.
  • LFU (LC 460) generalizes the same idea with a frequency dimension — a freq → DLL map plus the key map.
Practice — LeetCode
  • LRU Cache (Med) — canonical.
  • LFU Cache (Hard) — LRU + frequency dimension.
  • All O`one Data Structure (Hard) — DLL of buckets by count.
  • Design Front Middle Back Queue (Med) — two deques.
  • Design Skiplist (Hard) — probabilistic ordered map.
  • Design HashSet (Easy) — bucket array of lists.
  • Design HashMap (Easy) — bucket array with key/value pairs.
  • Insert Delete GetRandom O(1) (Med) — array + index map.
  • Insert Delete GetRandom O(1) Duplicates allowed (Hard) — set of indices per value.
  • Design Underground System (Med) — two hash maps.

docs.title: DSA · 15 · Monotonic Stack
docs.text: Next greater element, daily temperatures, largest rectangle.

docs.title: When to use this pattern
docs.text:
• Problem mentions "next greater / previous smaller / nearest taller" — verbatim trigger.
  • You need histogram-style answers: largest rectangle, maximal trapping, span.
  • Brackets / expression matching / score-of-parentheses — a vanilla stack pattern.
  • A scan needs to remember a sorted subset of indices seen so far.
  • You're doing O(n²) range comparisons that boil down to "for each i, find the closest j with property P".

docs.title: The shape of the solution
docs.text: Sweep the array once with an auxiliary stack of indices (not values — you usually need the position). Maintain a monotone order: e.g., values strictly decreasing from bottom to top for "next greater". Before pushing the current index, pop every stacked index whose value violates the order — those popped indices have their answer right now (the current index is their next-greater). Amortized analysis: every index is pushed and popped at most once, so total work across the loop is O(n) even though the inner pop loop can spike. Brackets are a degenerate special case: push openers, pop on matching closers.

docs.title: Topics in this section
docs.text:
01Valid Parentheses

Plain stack: push openers, match on close — the gentle introduction.

02Next Greater Element

Monotonic decreasing stack of indices — every pop gets its answer.

03Daily Temperatures

Next-greater applied to temperatures — record the *distance*, not the value.

04Largest Rectangle in Histogram

For each bar, find nearest smaller left and right with one pass and a sentinel.

docs.title: Common variations
docs.text: Two passes — left-to-right for "previous smaller", right-to-left for "next smaller". Circular arrays (LC 503) — extend the sweep to 2n iterations. Monotonic deques (LC 239 sliding window max) — same monotone discipline, both ends mutable. Cartesian trees are built from a monotonic stack and unlock RMQ-flavored problems. The hardest monotonic-stack problems are the ones where the invariant changes (e.g., LC 84 → LC 85 maximal rectangle of 0/1 matrix).

docs.title: 01 — Valid Parentheses
docs.text: Monotonic Stack • Position ¼

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given a string of ()[]{}, decide if every opener is matched by a same-type closer in correct nesting order.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: A stack matches the LIFO grammar of nested brackets perfectly. Push every opener as you see it; when you see a closer, the most recently opened bracket must match it — peek the stack and pop. Invariant: at every point in the scan, the stack holds the prefix of openers that have not yet been closed, with the most recent on top. If the stack is ever empty when a closer arrives, or the top doesn't match, the string is invalid. If the stack is non-empty at the end, you have unmatched openers.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Stack of openers with a closer→opener match table.

def is_valid(s):
    match = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}
    stack = []
    for ch in s:
        if ch in '([{':
            stack.append(ch)
        else:
            if not stack or stack[-1] != match[ch]:
                return False
            stack.pop()
    return not stack

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Validate "({[]})".

  1. ( — push. Stack: [(].
  2. { — push. Stack: [(, {].
  3. [ — push. Stack: [(, {, [].
  4. ] — top [ matches, pop. Stack: [(, {].
  5. } — top { matches, pop. Stack: [(]. Then ) — top ( matches, pop. Stack empty. Return true.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Forgetting the empty-stack check before peeking — pops on ] with no opener crash or silently misbehave.
  • Counting brackets without a stack (count_paren, count_bracket) works only for single-type strings; mixed types need a stack to enforce order.
  • Returning True at end without checking the stack is empty — "(((" returns true otherwise.
  • A dict.get(ch) for non-brackets returns None, which can mask typos in the alphabet. Validate input shape if it matters.
  • Variants like LC 32 (Longest Valid Parentheses) need the index on the stack, not the character — track positions to compute lengths.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Valid Parentheses (Easy) — canonical.
  • Generate Parentheses (Med) — backtracking with a counter.
  • Longest Valid Parentheses (Hard) — stack of indices.
  • Valid Parenthesis String (Med) — two stacks for `*` flexibility.
  • Score of Parentheses (Med) — stack-based accumulator.
  • Minimum Add to Make Parentheses Valid (Med) — counter or stack.
  • Minimum Remove to Make Valid Parentheses (Med) — track unmatched indices.
  • Maximum Nesting Depth of the Parentheses (Easy) — running max of counter.
  • Minimum Number of Swaps to Make the String Balanced (Med) — counter trick.
  • Minimum Insertions to Balance a Parentheses String (Med) — variant with `((` rule.

docs.title: 02 — Next Greater Element
docs.text: Monotonic Stack • Position 2/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: For every index i in an array, find the value of the first index j > i with a[j] > a[i], or -1 if none exists.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: Sweep left to right with a stack of indices whose answers are still unknown. Keep the stack monotonically decreasing by value (top = smallest). When the current value a[i] is greater than the value at the stack top, that top index's "next greater" is exactly a[i] — pop and record. Continue popping while the order is violated, then push i. Invariant: every index on the stack has a strictly larger value below it (or no element below) and is still waiting for its next greater. Each index is pushed and popped at most once → O(n) total.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Single pass, monotonic-decreasing stack of indices.

def next_greater(a):
    n = len(a)
    ans = [-1] * n
    stack = []                           # indices, a[stack] strictly decreasing
    for i, v in enumerate(a):
        while stack and a[stack[-1]] < v:
            ans[stack.pop()] = v
        stack.append(i)
    return ans

For a circular array (LC 503), loop i from 0 to 2n − 1 and index with i % n, only pushing on the first pass.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Find next greater for [2, 1, 2, 4, 3, 1].

  1. i = 0, v = 2. Stack empty. Push. stack = [0].
  2. i = 1, v = 1. a[0] = 2 ≥ 1, no pop. Push. stack = [0, 1].
  3. i = 2, v = 2. a[1] = 1 < 2 → pop 1, ans[1] = 2. a[0] = 2 ≥ 2, stop. Push. stack = [0, 2].
  4. i = 3, v = 4. Pop 2: ans[2] = 4. Pop 0: ans[0] = 4. Stack empty. Push. stack = [3].
  5. i = 4, v = 3. a[3] = 4 ≥ 3, no pop. Push. i = 5, v = 1. a[4] = 3 ≥ 1, no pop. Push. End. Stack [3, 4, 5] left with -1 answers. Final: [4, 2, 4, -1, -1, -1].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Storing values on the stack instead of indices works for "next greater value" but loses position — most variants need indices.
  • Using ≤ instead of < in the pop test gives "next strictly greater"; many problems want "next greater or equal" — read carefully.
  • Forgetting the strict-decreasing invariant — if you allow plateau equal values to stack, you'll over-pop on duplicates.
  • For previous greater, sweep right-to-left or invert the stack logic.
  • The trapping rain water problem (LC 42) is two monotone scans (or one stack) — same toolkit.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Next Greater Element I (Easy) — with a lookup array.
  • Next Greater Element II (Med) — circular array, double pass.
  • Next Greater Element III (Med) — digit permutation, not stack.
  • Daily Temperatures (Med) — record distance instead of value.
  • Online Stock Span (Med) — previous greater span.
  • Next Greater Node In Linked List (Med) — same algo on a list.
  • Largest Rectangle in Histogram (Hard) — previous + next smaller.
  • Maximal Rectangle (Hard) — LC 84 per row.
  • Trapping Rain Water (Hard) — monotonic stack of bars.
  • Sum of Subarray Minimums (Med) — contribution counting with PLE/NLE.

docs.title: 03 — Daily Temperatures
docs.text: Monotonic Stack • Position ¾

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: For each day, find how many days you must wait until a warmer temperature. Return 0 if no warmer day comes.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: This is next greater element with the answer recorded as a distance instead of a value. Sweep left to right with a stack of indices whose answers are still unknown; pop whenever the current temperature exceeds the top, and the answer for the popped index is i − popped_index. Invariant: stacked indices have strictly decreasing temperatures, and each holds the position of a day still waiting for warmth. Every index is pushed and popped once → O(n).

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Monotone decreasing stack of indices; emit i − stack.pop() on each pop.

def daily_temperatures(t):
    n = len(t)
    ans = [0] * n
    stack = []                           # indices, t[stack] strictly decreasing
    for i, v in enumerate(t):
        while stack and t[stack[-1]] < v:
            j = stack.pop()
            ans[j] = i - j
        stack.append(i)
    return ans

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Temperatures [73, 74, 75, 71, 69, 72, 76, 73].

  1. i = 0 (73): push. stack = [0].
  2. i = 1 (74 > 73): pop 0, ans[0] = 1. Push. stack = [1].
  3. i = 2 (75 > 74): pop 1, ans[1] = 1. Push. stack = [2].
  4. i = 3 (71): push. i = 4 (69): push. stack = [2, 3, 4].
  5. i = 5 (72 > 69 > 71): pop 4 → ans[4] = 1; pop 3 → ans[3] = 2; stop at 75. Push. i = 6 (76 > 75 > 72): pop 5 → ans[5] = 1; pop 2 → ans[2] = 4. Push. i = 7 (73): push. End. ans = [1, 1, 4, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0].

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Recording t[i] (the warmer temperature) instead of i − j (the distance) — common slip when adapting from next-greater code.
  • Using strict < vs ≤ — for "warmer" (strictly greater) you need <.
  • Initializing ans to -1 instead of 0 — the problem specifies 0 for "no warmer day".
  • O(n²) naïve solutions pass the small constraints but TLE on stress tests — practice the stack version.
  • The same template works for "next smaller" (flip comparator) and "previous warmer/colder" (reverse sweep).
Practice — LeetCode
  • Daily Temperatures (Med) — canonical.
  • Next Greater Element I (Easy) — value not distance.
  • Next Greater Element II (Med) — circular variant.
  • Online Stock Span (Med) — previous-greater span, online.
  • Sum of Subarray Minimums (Med) — contribution × stack span.
  • Next Greater Node In Linked List (Med) — same algo on linked list.
  • Final Prices With a Special Discount in a Shop (Easy) — next ≤ instead of next >.
  • Trapping Rain Water (Hard) — monotonic stack of bars.
  • Largest Rectangle in Histogram (Hard) — previous and next smaller.
  • Maximal Rectangle (Hard) — LC 84 per row.

docs.title: 04 — Largest Rectangle in Histogram
docs.text: Monotonic Stack • Position 4/4

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Given heights of adjacent bars in a histogram, return the area of the largest axis-aligned rectangle that fits inside.

docs.title: Intuition
docs.text: The optimal rectangle's height is some bar a[k]; its width spans from the nearest smaller bar on the left of k to the nearest smaller bar on the right of k, exclusive. If we can compute, for every k, those two boundaries in O(n), we just take the max of a[k] · (right − left − 1). A monotone increasing stack of indices does exactly that: when a new bar shorter than the top arrives, the top has its right boundary (the new index) and its left boundary (the new stack top after pop). Invariant: indices on the stack form a strictly increasing sequence of bar heights. Pushing a sentinel 0 at the end forces every remaining stacked bar to be resolved.

docs.title: Algorithm
docs.text:
Monotone increasing stack of indices + sentinel.

def largest_rectangle(heights):
    stack = []                           # strictly increasing heights[stack]
    best = 0
    heights = heights + [0]              # sentinel forces final flush
    for i, h in enumerate(heights):
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h:
            top = stack.pop()
            left = stack[-1] if stack else -1
            width = i - left - 1
            best = max(best, heights[top] * width)
        stack.append(i)
    return best

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Heights [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3], sentinel appended → [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3, 0].

  1. i = 0 (2): push. i = 1 (1 < 2): pop 0, left = -1, width = 1 − (−1) − 1 = 1, area = 2. Push 1. stack = [1].
  2. i = 2 (5), i = 3 (6): both bigger, push. stack = [1, 2, 3].
  3. i = 4 (2): pop 3 (height 6), left = 2, width = 4 − 2 − 1 = 1, area = 6. Pop 2 (height 5), left = 1, width = 4 − 1 − 1 = 2, area = 10. Push 4. stack = [1, 4]. Best = 10.
  4. i = 5 (3 > 2): push. stack = [1, 4, 5].
  5. i = 6 (sentinel 0): pop 5, left = 4, width = 1, area = 3. Pop 4 (height 2), left = 1, width = 6 − 1 − 1 = 4, area = 8. Pop 1 (height 1), left = -1, width = 6 − (−1) − 1 = 6, area = 6. Final best = 10.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text: time O(n) space O(n)

docs.title: Pitfalls
docs.text:
• Missing the sentinel 0 at the end — bars left on the stack never get their width computed.
  • Width formula off-by-one — it's i − left − 1, where left is the index of the previous smaller bar (or -1).
  • Stacking values instead of indices — you need positions to compute width.
  • Using ≥ instead of > for pop: equal heights are fine to keep stacked; popping them adds work and tiny correctness traps.
  • LC 85 (Maximal Rectangle of 0/1 matrix) is this algorithm run per row over cumulative heights — once you have LC 84, LC 85 is a one-screen extension.
Practice — LeetCode
  • Largest Rectangle in Histogram (Hard) — canonical.
  • Maximal Rectangle (Hard) — LC 84 per row.
  • Trapping Rain Water (Hard) — companion stack pattern.
  • Sum of Subarray Minimums (Med) — contribution × span.
  • Count Submatrices With All Ones (Med) — per-row histograms.
  • Maximal Square (Med) — DP version of the same idea.
  • Range Sum Query 2D Immutable (Med) — prefix-sum primitive used in matrix variants.
  • Maximum Score of a Good Subarray (Hard) — extend left/right under a constraint.
  • Maximum Subarray Min-Product (Med) — min × prefix-sum span, stack-based.
  • Max Chunks To Make Sorted (Med) — monotone stack of chunk maxes.
